topics.title: Write a Java program to print a string to the console
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to print a string to the console

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Printing a String to the Console in Java

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Java, **printing a string to the console** refers to the process of displaying a sequence of characters (a *String literal* or *String object*) on the standard output stream (**stdout**), which is normally mapped to the terminal/command prompt. This is achieved using the predefined static method `println()` or `print()` of the `System.out` object — a public static final field of the `java.lang.System` class.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snippet:** *"`System.out` is an instance of `PrintStream` connected to the console. The `println(String x)` method prints the string and terminates the line by writing a line-separator (`\n`)."*

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **public address (PA) system** in a college auditorium:

| Real-World PA System | Java Console Output |
|---|---|
| The **microphone** you speak into | The `String` literal you pass as an argument |
| The **amplifier + speaker** that broadcasts sound | The `System.out` object (a `PrintStream`) |
| The **person pressing "End" after a sentence** | The `println()` method (adds newline `\n`) |
| The **person just speaking continuously** | The `print()` method (no newline) |
| The **audience (terminal)** that receives the announcement | The **Console/Command Prompt** |

So when you write `System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");`, you are essentially **handing a written note (the string)** to the **Java PA system (`System.out`)**, which **reads it aloud on the console** for the user to see.

### 📌 Key Standard Metrics & Identifiers

- **Default Console Encoding:** UTF-8 (platform dependent on Windows — often `Cp1252`)
- **Standard Output Stream Identifier:** `System.out`
- **Line Separator Constant:** `System.lineSeparator()` → returns `\n` (Unix) or `\r\n` (Windows)
- **Method Signature:** `public void println(String x)` — inherited from `java.io.PrintStream`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** You do **NOT** need to `import java.lang.*;` explicitly because `System`, `String`, and all wrapper classes are auto-imported as part of the `java.lang` package.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Anatomy of a "Hello World" Java Program

### 🔍 The 5 Pillars of a Java Program That Prints a String

1. **Class Declaration** — Every Java program must reside inside a `class`.
2. **Main Method** — The JVM entry point. Must be `public static void main(String[] args)`.
3. **String Literal** — A sequence of characters enclosed in **double quotes** `" "`.
4. **Output Statement** — `System.out.println()` / `System.out.print()` / `System.out.printf()`.
5. **Compilation & Execution** — `javac FileName.java` → `java ClassName`.

### 🧩 The Program Structure (Source Code)

```java
// File: HelloKTU.java
public class HelloKTU {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Declare a String variable
        String greeting = "Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme!";
        
        // Step 2: Print the string using println (with newline)
        System.out.println(greeting);
        
        // Step 3: Print a literal directly
        System.out.println("Hello, Future Engineers!");
    }
}
```

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Each Token

| Token | Why It Exists | What Happens If Removed |
|---|---|---|
| `public` | Allows JVM (outside the class) to call `main` | `Main method not found` error |
| `static` | Lets JVM invoke `main` **without creating an object** | `Non-static method cannot be referenced` error |
| `void` | `main` returns nothing to the OS | Compile-time error |
| `String[] args` | Holds **command-line arguments** | Still compiles if renamed, but JVM specifically looks for this signature |
| `System` | Final class in `java.lang` providing system utilities | Compile error |
| `out` | Static `PrintStream` field bound to **stdout** | Compile error |
| `println` | Prints argument + platform-specific line separator | Compile error |

### 📊 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Print Method Comparison

| Method | Signature | Adds Newline? | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `System.out.print(x)` | `void print(String x)` | ❌ No | Build output on same line | `print("Hi")` → `Hi` |
| `System.out.println(x)` | `void println(String x)` | ✅ Yes | Standard console output | `println("Hi")` → `Hi\n` |
| `System.out.printf(fmt, args)` | `PrintStream printf(String, Object...)` | ❌ No | Formatted output (C-style) | `printf("Name: %s", "KTU")` |
| `System.out.format(...)` | Same as `printf` | ❌ No | Alias for `printf` | Identical to `printf` |
| `System.out.flush()` | `void flush()` | ❌ No | Forces buffered output to be written | Used in network/file streams |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Escape Sequences in Strings:** `\n` (newline), `\t` (tab), `\"` (double quote), `\\` (backslash). Example: `System.out.println("Line1\nLine2");` prints two lines.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Debugging:** Developers print variable states to trace logic flow.
- **Logging (basic):** Pre-cursor to proper logging frameworks (Log4j, SLF4J).
- **CLI Tools:** Java command-line utilities (Maven, Gradle) use console output extensively.
- **IoT/Embedded:** Java ME on Raspberry Pi uses console print for sensor data display.
- **Automation Scripts:** `System.out.println` in test scripts (TestNG/JUnit) reports test status.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Execution Analysis & Code Walkthrough

### 🔬 Step-by-Step JVM Execution Flow

Let us trace **exactly** what happens when you run `java HelloKTU` for the program above.

**Step 1 — Compilation Phase (`javac HelloKTU.java`)**
The Java compiler (`javac`) reads the source file, performs:
- Lexical analysis (tokenization)
- Syntax analysis (parsing → AST)
- Semantic analysis (type checking)
- Bytecode generation → produces `HelloKTU.class`

**Step 2 — Class Loading Phase**
The JVM's **ClassLoader subsystem** loads `HelloKTU.class` into method area memory.

**Step 3 — Verification**
The **Bytecode Verifier** checks for illegal bytecode (stack overflow, type safety).

**Step 4 — `main()` Method Invocation**
The JVM looks for a method with the exact signature:

```
public static void main(String[] args)
```

and invokes it, creating a new **stack frame** for it.

**Step 5 — String Object Creation**
The statement `String greeting = "Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme!";` causes the JVM to:
1. Check the **String Constant Pool** for the literal.
2. If absent, create a new `String` object in the **Heap (String Pool)**.
3. Store the reference in local variable `greeting` (on the JVM stack).

**Step 6 — Method Call Resolution**
`System.out` is resolved → returns the static `PrintStream` object.
`.println(greeting)` is dispatched → internally calls `print()` + `newLine()`.

**Step 7 — Output to Console**
The `PrintStream` writes the characters to the underlying file descriptor (`fd 1` in Unix / `STD_OUTPUT_HANDLE` in Windows).

### 📐 Mathematical Model of Console Output

Let the string be denoted as $S$ consisting of $n$ characters:

$$
S = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, \ldots, c_n\}
$$

where each $c_i$ is a Unicode code point. The `println` operation appends a line separator $\ell$:

$$
\text{Output} = S \cup \{\ell\} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n, \ell\}
$$

For `print`, the output is simply:

$$
\text{Output} = S = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}
$$

For `printf` with format `%s`, the JVM substitutes the placeholder with $S$:

$$
\text{Formatted Output} = \text{Format}(S, \text{formatString})
$$

### 🧪 Variant Programs (Increasing Complexity)

**Variant 1 — Print Without Storing in a Variable**
```java
System.out.println("Direct string literal");
```

**Variant 2 — Concatenation Using `+` Operator**
```java
String name = "KTU";
System.out.println("Welcome to " + name + " - 2024 Scheme");
```
*Behind the scenes:* The compiler translates `+` between `String` and any other type into a `StringBuilder.append()` chain for performance.

**Variant 3 — Formatted Output Using `printf`**
```java
String student = "Arjun";
int semester = 4;
System.out.printf("Student: %s, Semester: %d%n", student, semester);
```

**Variant 4 — Multiple Print Statements on Same Line**
```java
System.out.print("Hello");
System.out.print(" ");
System.out.print("World");
System.out.println();  // adds final newline
```
**Output:** `Hello World`

**Variant 5 — Reading and Printing (Using `Scanner`)**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;
public class GreetUser {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter your name: ");
        String name = sc.nextLine();
        System.out.println("Hello, " + name + "!");
    }
}
```

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Mermaid Diagram 1 — Java Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Write Source Code: HelloKTU.java] --> B[Run javac HelloKTU.java]
    B --> C[Bytecode Generated: HelloKTU.class]
    C --> D[Run java HelloKTU]
    D --> E[ClassLoader Loads .class]
    E --> F[Bytecode Verifier Validates]
    F --> G[JVM Finds main method]
    G --> H[Create Stack Frame for main]
    H --> I[Resolve System.out Reference]
    I --> J[Invoke println Method]
    J --> K[Write Characters to stdout]
    K --> L[Display on Console Terminal]
    
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

### 🗺️ Mermaid Diagram 2 — Class Structure of the Print Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[java.lang.System] -->|contains static field| B[public static final PrintStream out]
    B -->|instance of| C[java.io.PrintStream]
    C -->|inherits from| D[java.io.FilterOutputStream]
    D -->|inherits from| E[java.io.OutputStream]
    C -->|provides method| F[println String x]
    C -->|provides method| G[print String x]
    C -->|provides method| H[printf String fmt, Object args]
    
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
```

### 🗺️ Mermaid Diagram 3 — Memory Allocation When Printing a String

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack["JVM Stack - main method frame"]
        V1[Local Variable: greeting]
        V2[Local Variable: args reference]
    end
    
    subgraph Heap["Heap Memory - String Constant Pool"]
        S1[Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme!]
        S2[Hello, Future Engineers!]
    end
    
    subgraph MethodArea["Method Area - Class Metadata"]
        M1[HelloKTU.class bytecode]
        M2[System class metadata]
        M3[PrintStream class metadata]
    end
    
    V1 -.reference.-> S1
    S1 -->|displayed by| Out[System.out.println]
    S2 -->|displayed by| Out
    Out --> Console[Console / Terminal]
    
    style Stack fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#333
    style Heap fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#333
    style MethodArea fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#333
    style Console fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
```

### 🗺️ Mermaid Diagram 4 — Decision Flow: Which Print Method to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Display Output?] --> Q1{Need New Line After?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need Format Specifiers?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use System.out.print]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Use1[Use System.out.printf]
    Q2 -->|No| Use2[Use System.out.println]
    Q3 --> End[Output Displayed]
    Use1 --> End
    Use2 --> End
    
    style Start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style End fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*What is the role of the `main` method in a Java program? Why must it be declared as `public static void`?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The `main` method is the **entry point** of any standalone Java application. The JVM searches for a method with the exact signature `public static void main(String[] args)` to begin execution.
- **`public`** → accessible from anywhere (the JVM is outside the class, so it must be able to call it).
- **`static`** → allows the JVM to invoke it **without creating an object** of the class.
- **`void`** → the method does not return any value to the operating system.
- **`String[] args`** → holds command-line arguments passed at runtime.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Differentiate between `System.out.print()` and `System.out.println()` with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | `System.out.print()` | `System.out.println()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Newline** | Does **not** add a newline | Adds a newline (`\n`) at the end |
| **Cursor Position** | Stays on the same line | Moves to the next line |
| **Use Case** | When you want continued output on one line | When you want output to end a line |
| **Example** | `print("Hi ");` `print("KTU");` → `Hi KTU` | `println("Hi");` `println("KTU");` → `Hi` then `KTU` (separate lines) |

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to print your name, roll number, branch, and semester on separate lines using `System.out.println()`. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step execution of the above program, including compilation, class loading, and output. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

```java
// File: StudentDetails.java
public class StudentDetails {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String name = "Arjun Krishnan";
        String rollNo = "KTU2024BCE0042";
        String branch = "Computer Science and Engineering";
        int semester = 4;
        
        System.out.println("Name      : " + name);
        System.out.println("Roll No   : " + rollNo);
        System.out.println("Branch    : " + branch);
        System.out.println("Semester  : " + semester);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Name      : Arjun Krishnan
Roll No   : KTU2024BCE0042
Branch    : Computer Science and Engineering
Semester  : 4
```

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- Correct class declaration & `main` signature → **1 mark**
- Correct variable declarations with appropriate data types → **2 marks**
- Correct use of `System.out.println()` with concatenation → **2 marks**
- Proper compilation, execution & output → **2 marks**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Source Code Creation:** The programmer writes the code in a file named `StudentDetails.java`. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compilation:** The command `javac StudentDetails.java` is executed. The compiler performs lexical, syntactic, and semantic analysis and produces `StudentDetails.class` containing Java bytecode. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Class Loading:** When `java StudentDetails` is run, the JVM's **ClassLoader** loads `StudentDetails.class` into the method area of JVM memory. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Verification:** The **Bytecode Verifier** validates the bytecode for type safety and security. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — `main()` Invocation:** The JVM creates a new stack frame and invokes `main(String[] args)`. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Execution & Output:** The `println` method is called for each line, the `PrintStream` writes characters to the standard output stream, and the text appears on the console. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** Write a Java program that takes a student's name as a **command-line argument** and prints a personalized greeting. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the difference between the three output methods: `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()`. Provide a single program demonstrating all three. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

```java
// File: GreetStudent.java
public class GreetStudent {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Check if a name was provided
        if (args.length > 0) {
            String name = args[0];
            System.out.println("=================================");
            System.out.println(" Welcome, " + name + "!");
            System.out.println(" KTU 2024 Scheme - OOP Lab");
            System.out.println("=================================");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Usage: java GreetStudent <YourName>");
        }
    }
}
```

**Compilation & Execution:**
```bash
javac GreetStudent.java
java GreetStudent Arjun
```

**Output:**
```
=================================
 Welcome, Arjun!
 KTU 2024 Scheme - OOP Lab
=================================
```

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- Correct `main(String[] args)` signature → **1 mark**
- Accessing `args[0]` correctly → **2 marks**
- Proper validation (`args.length` check) → **2 marks**
- Correct formatted output → **2 marks**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Explanation of the Three Methods:**

1. **`print(String x)`** — Prints the string **without** a newline. The cursor remains at the end of the printed text. *(1 mark)*

2. **`println(String x)`** — Prints the string and **appends a newline** character. The cursor moves to the beginning of the next line. *(1 mark)*

3. **`printf(String format, Object... args)`** — Prints a **formatted** string using format specifiers like `%s` (String), `%d` (integer), `%f` (float). It does **not** add a newline unless `%n` is included. *(1 mark)*

**Demonstration Program:**

```java
public class PrintDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String dept = "CSE";
        int students = 120;
        double avgCgpa = 8.45;
        
        // 1. Using print() - no newline
        System.out.print("Department: ");
        System.out.print(dept);
        System.out.print(" | ");
        
        // 2. Using println() - adds newline
        System.out.println("Total Students: " + students);
        
        // 3. Using printf() - formatted output
        System.out.printf("Department: %s | Students: %d | Avg CGPA: %.2f%n", 
                          dept, students, avgCgpa);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Department: CSE | Total Students: 120
Department: CSE | Students: 120 | Avg CGPA: 8.45
```

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Correct explanation of all 3 methods → **3 marks**
- Correct demonstration program with all three methods used → **2 marks**
- Correct use of format specifiers (`%s`, `%d`, `%f`) → **1 mark**
- Proper output shown → **1 mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Filename Mismatch Trap:** In Java, the **filename MUST match the `public class` name** (e.g., class `HelloKTU` → file `HelloKTU.java`). Otherwise, you get `class HelloKTU is public, should be declared in a file named HelloKTU.java`. **— Deduct 2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Case Sensitivity Mistake:** Writing `system` instead of `System` or `out` instead of `OUT` — Java is **case-sensitive**. **— Deduct 1 mark.**
> 
> 3. **Missing Semicolons:** Every statement in Java ends with `;`. Missing semicolons = compile error. **— Deduct 1 mark per occurrence.**
> 
> 4. **Confusing `print` and `println`:** Examiners often test if you know the difference. If asked to print on separate lines and you use `print`, you lose marks.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting `String[] args`:** Some students write `main()` without parameters — this will NOT be recognized as the entry point by JVM. **— Deduct 2 marks.**
> 
> 6. **Using single quotes for String:** `System.out.println('Hello');` is a **compile error** because single quotes denote `char`, not `String`. Always use **double quotes** for strings.
> 
> 7. **No spaces around `+` in concatenation:** While not an error, improper spacing is considered poor coding style in KTU evaluations.

---

## 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Every Java program must have a `class` and a `public static void main(String[] args)` method.**
- ✅ **Filename must match the public class name** (case-sensitive).
- ✅ **Use `System.out.println()`** to print and move to a new line.
- ✅ **Use `System.out.print()`** to print without moving to a new line.
- ✅ **Use `System.out.printf()`** for formatted output (C-style with `%s`, `%d`, `%f`, `%n`).
- ✅ **Strings must be enclosed in double quotes** `" "`; characters in single quotes `' '`.
- ✅ **The `+` operator concatenates** strings and other types via implicit `toString()`.
- ✅ **`System` is in `java.lang`**, which is auto-imported — no import statement needed.
- ✅ **`System.out` is a `PrintStream` object** connected to standard output (`stdout`).
- ✅ **Compilation:** `javac FileName.java` → produces `.class` bytecode.
- ✅ **Execution:** `java ClassName` → JVM loads, verifies, and executes bytecode.
- ✅ **Escape sequences:** `\n` (newline), `\t` (tab), `\"` (quote), `\\` (backslash).
- ✅ **Common error:** `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if `args[0]` is accessed without checking `args.length`.
- ✅ **KTU tip:** When asked to "explain the output," always mention compilation, class loading, and the role of `System.out` — examiners reward conceptual depth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to find the average of three numbers entered by the user
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to find the average of three numbers entered by the user

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition:**
> A *Java program* is a sequence of instructions written in the Java programming language, compiled into platform-independent *bytecode* and executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**. A program that *finds the average of three numbers entered by the user* is a classic introductory lab exercise that demonstrates **Console-based Input/Output (I/O) handling**, **primitive numeric data type manipulation**, and **arithmetic expression evaluation** in Java.

The *average* (also called the **arithmetic mean**) of three numbers $a$, $b$, and $c$ is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{Average} = \frac{a + b + c}{3}$$

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) terms, this experiment introduces students to:
- **Class declaration** and the mandatory `main(String[] args)` method.
- **Object instantiation** of the `Scanner` class (from `java.util` package).
- **Encapsulation of logic** inside a well-named Java class.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Explanation (The "Bucket" Analogy):**
> Imagine you have **three buckets** of water. Bucket A holds 10 litres, Bucket B holds 20 litres, and Bucket C holds 30 litres. If you pour all the water into a single big container, the total is 60 litres. To find out how much water each bucket would *on average* contain, you simply split the 60 litres equally into 3 buckets — giving **20 litres per bucket**. That "equal share" is the **average**.

In this Java program:
- The **user** is the person pouring water into the buckets (entering numbers).
- The **Scanner object** is the measuring cup that picks up each number.
- The **Java program** is the brain that adds the numbers and divides by 3.

## 1.3 Key Components at a Glance

| Component | Role in Program | Java Class/Keyword |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| User Input Source | Keyboard (Standard Input Stream) | `System.in` |
| Input Reader Object | Reads typed numbers from keyboard | `Scanner` |
| Numeric Storage | Holds each number in memory | `double` / `int` variables |
| Arithmetic Operation | Computes the mean | `+`, `/` operators |
| Output Stream | Displays the result on console | `System.out.println()` |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Always remember to **import** the `Scanner` class using `import java.util.Scanner;` at the top of your file. Programs without this import statement will throw a **compilation error** — a common reason students lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Program Logic

The program can be broken down into **four distinct logical phases**, each corresponding to a core Java concept:

### Phase 1 — Program Entry Point
Every standalone Java application **must** contain a `main` method. This is the JVM's entry point.

- **Why?** The JVM searches for the `public static void main(String[] args)` signature to begin execution. Without it, the program cannot run.
- **How?** Declared as: `public static void main(String[] args) { ... }`

### Phase 2 — Input Acquisition (The `Scanner` Class)
Java does not read keyboard input natively in its core language. The `Scanner` class (introduced in Java 5, part of `java.util`) bridges this gap.

- **Key Methods Used:**
  * `nextDouble()` — reads a `double` value (supports decimals like 5.5, 10.25).
  * `nextInt()` — reads an `int` value (whole numbers only).
  * `nextLine()` — reads an entire line as a `String`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `double` and not `int` for average?**
> The result of dividing two integers in Java performs **integer division** if both operands are `int`. For example, `10 / 3 = 3` (NOT 3.333...). To preserve decimal accuracy, you must cast at least one operand to `double` (e.g., `sum / 3.0`) or declare variables as `double` from the start.

### Phase 3 — Arithmetic Computation
The mean of three numbers is calculated as:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \text{where } n = 3$$

For three numbers specifically:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + x_3}{3}$$

### Phase 4 — Output Display
The result is displayed using `System.out.println()`, which prints to the **standard output stream (stdout)** — typically the console.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Data Type | Common Pitfall |
|---|---------|------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean (3 numbers) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{a+b+c}{3}$ | `double` | Integer division truncation |
| 2 | Sum of n numbers | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | `double`/`int` | Overflow for very large values |
| 3 | Scanner declaration | `Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);` | Object | Forgetting `import` statement |
| 4 | Read double | `sc.nextDouble();` | `double` | Throws `InputMismatchException` on bad input |
| 5 | Read integer | `sc.nextInt();` | `int` | Throws on non-integer input |
| 6 | Print with newline | `System.out.println(result);` | `void` | `print` vs `println` confusion |
| 7 | Resource cleanup | `sc.close();` | `void` | Resource leak warning in IDEs |
| 8 | Division safety | `sum / 3.0` (not `sum / 3`) | `double` | Loses decimal precision |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?**
> Averaging is a **foundational statistical operation** used everywhere:
> - **Civil Engineering:** Averaging multiple concrete strength test samples to determine quality.
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Computing the *DC offset* (average value) of an AC waveform — $V_{DC} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t)\,dt$.
> - **Signal Processing:** *Mean filtering* to reduce noise in sensor data.
> - **Machine Learning:** The mean is the first moment in statistics, foundational to **normalization** and **standardization**.

Understanding how to take input, perform arithmetic, and produce output in Java mirrors the workflow of any real-time **data acquisition system**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Program Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Java Program (Reference Implementation)

Below is the **exam-ready, KTU-accepted** version of the program. Study each line as you would an equation — every character has purpose.

```java
// File: AverageOfThree.java
// Experiment 2: Find the average of three numbers entered by the user

import java.util.Scanner;   // Step 1: Import Scanner class

public class AverageOfThree {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 2: Create a Scanner object to read from keyboard
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Step 3: Prompt and read three numbers from the user
        System.out.print("Enter the first number: ");
        double num1 = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.print("Enter the second number: ");
        double num2 = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.print("Enter the third number: ");
        double num3 = sc.nextDouble();

        // Step 4: Compute the sum
        double sum = num1 + num2 + num3;

        // Step 5: Compute the average (using 3.0 to force double division)
        double average = sum / 3.0;

        // Step 6: Display the result
        System.out.println("The sum of the three numbers is: " + sum);
        System.out.println("The average of the three numbers is: " + average);

        // Step 7: Close the Scanner to release resources
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Program

Let us trace the program's behavior **line-by-line**, mapping each Java statement to its mathematical/logical equivalent.

### Step 1 — Import Statement
```java
import java.util.Scanner;
```
**Logic:** Java organizes classes into *packages* (folders). `Scanner` lives in the `java.util` package. Without this import, the compiler doesn't know where to find `Scanner` and throws:
> *`error: cannot find symbol — class Scanner`*

### Step 2 — Class Declaration
```java
public class AverageOfThree {
```
**Logic:** The filename **must match** the `public class` name. So this file **must** be saved as `AverageOfThree.java`. Mismatched names = compilation error.

### Step 3 — Main Method Signature
```java
public static void main(String[] args) {
```
**Logic:** Breaks down as:
- `public` → accessible to the JVM.
- `static` → can be called without creating an object.
- `void` → returns nothing.
- `main` → reserved method name (entry point).
- `String[] args` → accepts command-line arguments as a string array.

### Step 4 — Scanner Object Creation
```java
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
```
**Logic:**
- `System.in` is a **static final** field of type `InputStream` representing the keyboard.
- `new Scanner(System.in)` creates a new `Scanner` object that wraps the keyboard stream.
- `sc` is the **reference variable** pointing to this object.

### Step 5 — Reading Input
```java
double num1 = sc.nextDouble();
```
**Logic:** The method `nextDouble()`:
1. Skips whitespace.
2. Reads characters until a non-numeric token is encountered.
3. Parses the characters into a `double` primitive.
4. Returns the value, which is stored in `num1`.

### Step 6 — The Core Arithmetic
The crucial mathematical operation is:

$$
\text{average} = \frac{\text{num1} + \text{num2} + \text{num3}}{3.0}
$$

**Why `3.0` instead of `3`?**

If we had written `sum / 3` where `sum` is `double` (e.g., `30.0`), Java would still perform floating-point division because one operand is `double`. But if `sum` was accidentally declared as `int` (a common student mistake), `30 / 3` would yield `10.0` (Java promotes to double in mixed expressions). To be **defensively safe** and **pedagogically explicit**, KTU expects the `3.0` form.

### Step 7 — Concatenation and Output
```java
System.out.println("The average is: " + average);
```
**Logic:** The `+` operator, when one operand is a `String`, triggers **String concatenation**. Java implicitly calls `Double.toString(average)` to convert the numeric value to text.

## 3.3 Numerical Walkthrough — Worked Example

Let us **trace** the program with sample input: `num1 = 10`, `num2 = 20`, `num3 = 30`.

| Line of Code | Variable State | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `double num1 = sc.nextDouble();` | `num1 = 10.0` | User types 10, parsed as double |
| `double num2 = sc.nextDouble();` | `num2 = 20.0` | User types 20, parsed as double |
| `double num3 = sc.nextDouble();` | `num3 = 30.0` | User types 30, parsed as double |
| `double sum = num1 + num2 + num3;` | `sum = 60.0` | $10 + 20 + 30 = 60$ |
| `double average = sum / 3.0;` | `average = 20.0` | $60 \div 3 = 20$ |
| `System.out.println(...)` | Output: `20.0` | Concatenated and printed |

**Final Output on Console:**
```
Enter the first number: 10
Enter the second number: 20
Enter the third number: 30
The sum of the three numbers is: 60.0
The average of the three numbers is: 20.0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative edge case:** If input is `num1 = 5.5`, `num2 = 10.5`, `num3 = 14.0`:
> $$\text{sum} = 5.5 + 10.5 + 14.0 = 30.0$$
> $$\text{average} = \frac{30.0}{3.0} = 10.0$$
> This proves the program correctly handles **decimal inputs**, unlike an `int`-only version.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Program Execution Flowchart

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the complete logical flow of the program, from JVM launch to program termination. This is the exact flow KTU examiners expect students to draw in viva voce or record notebooks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: JVM invokes main] --> B[Import java.util.Scanner]
    B --> C[Declare class AverageOfThree]
    C --> D[Create Scanner object sc = new Scanner System.in]
    D --> E[Display: Enter first number]
    E --> F[Read num1 = sc.nextDouble]
    F --> G[Display: Enter second number]
    G --> H[Read num2 = sc.nextDouble]
    H --> I[Display: Enter third number]
    I --> J[Read num3 = sc.nextDouble]
    J --> K[Compute sum = num1 + num2 + num3]
    K --> L[Compute average = sum divided by 3.0]
    L --> M[Display sum on console]
    M --> N[Display average on console]
    N --> O[Close Scanner object]
    O --> P[End: Program terminates]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:**
> - **Rectangles** (no rounding) represent *process steps*.
> - Each arrow represents a **sequential control flow** — there are no branches in this program (it is strictly linear).
> - The **oval** terminators (`Start`/`End`) are conventional in flowchart notation.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **runtime memory architecture** during program execution, showing how Java's stack and heap interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stack[Call Stack - main method frame]
        A1[num1: double]
        A2[num2: double]
        A3[num3: double]

        A6[sc: Scanner reference]
    end

    subgraph Heap[Java Heap - Object Memory]
        B1[Scanner object]
        B2[Buffer: keyboard input stream]

    end

    subgraph StdOut[Standard Output - Console]
        D1[Monitor display]
    end

    StdIn --> B2

    A6 --> B1
    B1 --> A1

    B1 --> A3
```
