
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU vivas:**
> Examiners often ask: *"Where is the Scanner object stored?"*
> - **Reference variable `sc`** → lives on the **stack** (inside `main`'s frame).
> - **The actual Scanner object** → lives on the **heap** (created via `new`).
> - **`System.in`** → a static field, also references an object on the heap.

## 4.3 Data Type Conversion Reference (Type Promotion Rules)

| Operation | Operands | Result Type | Reason |
|-----------|----------|-------------|--------|
| `int + int` | Both `int` | `int` | No promotion needed |
| `int + double` | Mixed | `double` | Smaller type promoted to larger |
| `int / int` | Both `int` | `int` | **Integer division — fractional part truncated** |
| `double / int` | Mixed | `double` | `int` promoted to `double` |
| `double / double` | Both `double` | `double` | Pure floating-point division |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Explain the role of the `Scanner` class in Java. Why is it necessary to import it from `java.util`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `Scanner` class, defined in the `java.util` package, is used in Java to read formatted input from various sources such as the keyboard (`System.in`), files, or strings. It provides methods like `nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, and `nextLine()` to parse different data types from input streams. Since Java organizes its classes into packages, the `Scanner` class is not automatically available — it must be explicitly imported using the statement `import java.util.Scanner;` placed at the top of the source file. Without this import, the compiler raises a *"cannot find symbol"* error, preventing successful compilation. In a program that finds the average of three numbers, the Scanner enables the application to accept three numeric values from the user via the console. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**What is meant by "integer division truncation" in Java? Show with an example relevant to average calculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*"Integer division truncation"* refers to the behavior in Java where dividing two `int` values discards the fractional (decimal) part, returning only the integer quotient. For example, if we compute the average of `5`, `10`, and `14` using `int` variables, the sum is `29`, and dividing `29 / 3` yields `9` instead of the mathematically correct `9.666...`. This happens because both operands are integers, so Java performs integer arithmetic. To prevent this, at least one operand must be a `double` (e.g., `sum / 3.0` or declaring variables as `double` from the start), which forces floating-point division and preserves the decimal precision. This is why the average program typically uses `double` data types. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **one full question** (both sub-parts) from the choice provided.

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU Model — Module 2, Experiment 2 Type]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to read three numbers from the user and display their sum and average. Use the `Scanner` class for input. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain what happens if the divisor in the average formula is written as the integer `3` instead of `3.0`. Provide a numerical example to justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class SumAverage {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        System.out.print("Enter first number: ");
        double a = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.print("Enter second number: ");
        double b = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.print("Enter third number: ");
        double c = sc.nextDouble();

        double sum = a + b + c;
        double average = sum / 3.0;

        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
        System.out.println("Average = " + average);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```
**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Import statement & class declaration: **1 mark**
- Scanner object creation: **1 mark**
- Reading three inputs correctly: **1.5 marks**
- Sum computation: **1 mark**
- Average computation with `3.0`: **1.5 marks**
- Output formatting: **1 mark**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

If the divisor is written as the integer `3` (e.g., `average = sum / 3;`), and `sum` is of type `double`, Java still performs floating-point division because **at least one operand** is a `double`. The result would be mathematically correct (e.g., `60.0 / 3 = 20.0`).

However, if `sum` is also an `int` (e.g., `int sum = 10 + 20 + 30;`), then the expression `sum / 3` performs **integer division truncation**.

**Numerical Example:**

| Input Numbers | Sum | `sum / 3` (int) | `sum / 3.0` (correct) |
|---------------|-----|-----------------|------------------------|
| 5, 10, 14 | 29 | 9 (❌ wrong) | 9.666... (✅ correct) |
| 10, 20, 30 | 60 | 20 (✅ correct by coincidence) | 20.0 (✅ correct) |

In the first row, integer division `29 / 3` yields `9` because Java truncates the decimal `.666...`. This leads to **loss of precision** — a common bug in numerical programs. The defensive practice is to use `3.0` or cast explicitly: `(double) sum / 3`. **(7 marks)**

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Explanation of `int / int` behavior: **2 marks**
- Numerical example with wrong output: **2 marks**
- Corrective technique (using `3.0` or casting): **2 marks**
- Conclusion / summary: **1 mark**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Write a Java program that accepts three integers from the user, calculates their average, and displays the result rounded to **two decimal places**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `print()` and `println()` methods in Java. Why is `println()` preferred for displaying program output in lab records? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class RoundedAverage {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        System.out.print("Enter first integer: ");
        int a = sc.nextInt();

        System.out.print("Enter second integer: ");
        int b = sc.nextInt();

        System.out.print("Enter third integer: ");
        int c = sc.nextInt();

        double average = (a + b + c) / 3.0;  // cast to double via 3.0

        System.out.printf("The average is: %.2f%n", average);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Key Concept:** The `%.2f` format specifier in `printf()` rounds the value to 2 decimal places.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Program structure: **1 mark**
- Reading three integers: **1.5 marks**
- Safe average computation: **1.5 marks**
- Use of `printf` with `%.2f`: **2 marks**
- Correct output: **1 mark**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

| Feature | `print()` | `println()` |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Cursor movement** | Stays on same line | Moves to next line |
| **Syntax** | `System.out.print("text");` | `System.out.println("text");` |
| **Use case** | When output continues on same line | When each output should appear on a new line |
| **Example output** | `Hello World` (no break) | `Hello` <br> `World` (line break) |

**Why `println()` is preferred in lab records:**
In KTU lab records, outputs are usually recorded **line-by-line** to clearly show each step's result. `println()` automatically appends a newline character (`\n`), ensuring that successive outputs do not merge on the same line. This makes the record cleaner, more readable, and easier for the examiner to verify the program's behavior step by step. **(7 marks)**

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Clear distinction table: **3 marks**
- Examples of both: **2 marks**
- Justification for lab record use: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `import java.util.Scanner;`** — Immediate compilation error. **Loss: 1 mark minimum.**
> 2. **Filename mismatch** — If the file is saved as `Main.java` but the class is `AverageOfThree`, compilation fails. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **Using `int` instead of `double`** — Causes integer division truncation. **Loss: 2–3 marks** for incorrect output on test cases with non-divisible sums.
> 4. **Forgetting `sc.close();`** — Modern IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ) flag this as a *resource leak warning*. While it may not crash the program, KTU's "best practices" rubric deducts marks. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Typo in `nextDouble()`** — Writing `nextdouble()` or `nextDoube()` causes a *"cannot find symbol"* error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Missing semicolons** — Java is strictly terminated by `;`. A missing semicolon is a compilation error. **Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.**
> 7. **Not matching the output format** — KTU sometimes specifies the *exact* output format (e.g., `"Average = X.XX"`). A program that prints `"The result is: X.XX"` may lose marks for **format mismatch**. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Lab Exam:**

- ✅ **Every Java program** needs: `import` statements → `class declaration` → `main` method.
- ✅ **Filename must equal** the public class name (case-sensitive): `AverageOfThree.java`.
- ✅ **Scanner** lives in `java.util` package — must be explicitly imported.
- ✅ **Use `double`** (not `int`) for variables and `3.0` (not `3`) as divisor to avoid truncation.
- ✅ **Average formula:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{a + b + c}{3}$ — memorize the equation symbol-perfect.
- ✅ **`nextDouble()`** reads decimal numbers; **`nextInt()`** reads whole numbers; misuse throws `InputMismatchException`.
- ✅ **`print()` vs `println()`** — only difference is the newline character at the end.
- ✅ **`sc.close()`** is good practice to release the underlying `System.in` resource.
- ✅ **String concatenation:** `"Result: " + value` — the `+` operator joins a `String` with any other type by calling `.toString()`.
- ✅ **Compilation command:** `javac AverageOfThree.java` → **Run command:** `java AverageOfThree`.
- ✅ **Common error message meanings:**
  * `cannot find symbol` → typo or missing import.
  * `class, interface, or enum expected` → mismatched braces.
  * `incompatible types` → wrong data type assignment.
  * `reached end of file while parsing` → missing closing brace `}`.
- ✅ **Viva question to expect:** *"Why is the `main` method declared `static`?"* — Answer: So that the JVM can invoke it **without creating an instance** of the class.
- ✅ **Viva question to expect:** *"What is the difference between `System.in` and `System.out`?"* — Answer: `System.in` is the standard **input** stream (keyboard); `System.out` is the standard **output** stream (console/monitor).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to find the frequency of a given character in a string
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to find the frequency of a given character in a string

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Character Frequency Counting** is a fundamental string processing operation in Java that determines the number of times a specific character (the *target character*) appears in a given sequence of characters (the *string*). Formally, for a string $S = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots, c_n\}$ and a target character $t$, the frequency $f(t, S)$ is defined as:

$$f(t, S) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta(c_i, t) \quad \text{where} \quad \delta(c_i, t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c_i = t \\ 0 & \text{if } c_i \neq t \end{cases}$$

The result is a non-negative integer representing the **occurrence count**. This operation is classified under **String Traversal Algorithms** and forms the basis for advanced operations like anagram checking, palindrome validation, and text compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (PCEOL408):** This experiment validates **CO1: Apply object-oriented programming concepts using Java to develop solutions to real-world problems** and **CO2: Implement Java programs utilizing classes, objects, and string handling mechanisms**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine you have a **transparent jar full of mixed colored marbles** — red, blue, green, and yellow. Your friend asks: *"How many blue marbles are in this jar?"* You don't have a machine. So you **pick up the jar, look inside, and count one marble at a time**, keeping a tally on your fingers every time you see a blue one. At the end, you announce the total.

**Translating to Java:**
- The **jar** = your `String` (a sequence of characters).
- **Blue marbles** = the target character you want to count.
- **Your eyes scanning** = a `for` loop iterating character by character.
- **Tally on fingers** = the integer counter variable.
- **Announcing the total** = printing the frequency.

The algorithm is **deterministic, finite, and linear** — for a string of length $n$, you will always check exactly $n$ positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A common exam pitfall is confusing **String length()** (a method) with **Array length** (a property). In Java, `string.length()` uses parentheses, while `array.length` does not. Examiners often test this distinction.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition | Java Example |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **String** | An immutable sequence of characters in Java | `"Hello World"` |
| **char** | A primitive 16-bit Unicode data type | `'a'`, `'5'`, `'@'` |
| **Index** | 0-based position of a character | `H` is at index `0` in `"Hello"` |
| **Frequency** | The total count of occurrences | `'l'` appears **3** times in `"Hello World"` |
| **Case Sensitivity** | Distinction between `'A'` and `'a'` | `'A' ≠ 'a'` in Java |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Logic Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The character frequency problem is decomposed into the following logical pipeline:

1. **Input Acquisition** — Read the source string from the user via the `Scanner` class or pass it as an argument.
2. **Target Identification** — Read or accept the character whose frequency needs to be determined.
3. **Initialization** — Declare a counter variable initialized to **0**.
4. **Iterative Traversal** — Loop from index `0` to `string.length() - 1`, examining each character using `charAt(i)`.
5. **Conditional Comparison** — Inside the loop, compare each character with the target character using the equality operator `==` (valid for primitive `char` types).
6. **Counter Increment** — If the comparison returns `true`, increment the counter by 1.
7. **Termination & Output** — Once the loop completes, print the final count.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why initialize to 0?** A counter must start at a known neutral state. The identity element for addition is **0**, so any increment adds correctly.
- **Why use `charAt(i)` instead of indexing with `[]`?** In Java, strings are **immutable objects**, not arrays. Direct bracket indexing (`str[i]`) is illegal. `charAt(i)` is the canonical accessor method.
- **Why use `==` for char comparison?** The `char` is a **primitive type** in Java. For primitives, `==` compares actual values, not references. For `String` objects, however, `==` would compare references — a classic interview trap.
- **Why iterate until `length() - 1`?** Java uses **zero-based indexing**. A string of length `n` has valid indices `[0, n-1]`.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Logic Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Approach | Method Used | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | KTU Frequency |
|----------|-------------|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Brute Force** | `charAt(i)` with `for` loop | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | ★★★★★ (Most Common) |
| **Character Array** | `toCharArray()` + enhanced `for` | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ★★★★☆ |
| **Stream API (Java 8+)** | `chars().filter().count()` | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | ★★★☆☆ |
| **HashMap Frequency Map** | Loop with `HashMap<Character, Integer>` | $O(n)$ | $O(k)$ where $k$ = unique chars | ★★★★☆ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** For Lab examinations, the **brute force `charAt()` approach** is the expected canonical solution. Examiners award full marks for clear loop logic, proper variable initialization, and correct use of `Scanner` for input.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Character frequency counting is the **foundational building block** for:
- **Cryptography** — Frequency analysis is used to break classical ciphers like the Caesar cipher.
- **Data Compression** — Huffman coding builds binary trees based on character frequency.
- **Plagiarism Detection** — Document fingerprinting uses character n-gram frequencies.
- **Bioinformatics** — Counting nucleotide frequencies (A, T, G, C) in DNA sequences.
- **Search Engines** — Term frequency (TF) is a core component of the TF-IDF ranking algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Algorithm Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Algorithm Derivation — Formal Pseudocode

We now derive the algorithm formally. Let:
- $S$ = the input string of length $n$.
- $t$ = the target character to count.
- $f$ = the frequency counter (initially 0).

**Pseudocode Block (BEGIN ALGORITHM):**

```
ALGORITHM: CountCharacterFrequency(S, t)
INPUT:  A string S of length n, a character t
OUTPUT: Integer f representing the frequency of t in S

BEGIN
    f ← 0
    FOR i ← 0 TO n - 1 DO
        IF S[i] == t THEN
            f ← f + 1
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN f
END
```

### 3.2 Worked Example — Hand Trace

Let us hand-trace the algorithm with **S = `"Programming"`** and **t = `'g'`**.

| Iteration `i` | Character `S.charAt(i)` | Comparison with `'g'` | Counter `f` | State |
|:-------------:|:-----------------------:|:--------------------:|:-----------:|:------|
| 0 | `'P'` | `'P' == 'g'` → **false** | 0 | No match |
| 1 | `'r'` | `'r' == 'g'` → **false** | 0 | No match |
| 2 | `'o'` | `'o' == 'g'` → **false** | 0 | No match |
| 3 | `'g'` | `'g' == 'g'` → **true** | 1 | **MATCH** |
| 4 | `'r'` | `'r' == 'g'` → **false** | 1 | No match |
| 5 | `'a'` | `'a' == 'g'` → **false** | 1 | No match |
| 6 | `'m'` | `'m' == 'g'` → **false** | 1 | No match |
| 7 | `'m'` | `'m' == 'g'` → **false** | 1 | No match |
| 8 | `'i'` | `'i' == 'g'` → **false** | 1 | No match |
| 9 | `'n'` | `'n' == 'g'` → **false** | 1 | No match |
| 10 | `'g'` | `'g' == 'g'` → **true** | 2 | **MATCH** |

**Final Output:** `Frequency of 'g' in "Programming" = 2`

### 3.3 Mathematical Verification of Output Count

The string `"Programming"` has length $n = 11$. The character `'g'` appears at indices $3$ and $10$.

$$f('g', \text{"Programming"}) = \sum_{i=0}^{10} \delta(c_i, 'g') = \delta(c_3, 'g') + \delta(c_{10}, 'g') = 1 + 1 = 2$$

This matches our trace. The algorithm is **mathematically correct** and **logically sound**.

### 3.4 Complete Java Reference Implementation (KTU Board Standard)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class CharacterFrequency {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Create Scanner object for input
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        // Step 2: Read the input string
        System.out.print("Enter a string: ");
        String input = scanner.nextLine();
        
        // Step 3: Read the target character
        System.out.print("Enter the character to find frequency: ");
        char target = scanner.next().charAt(0);
        
        // Step 4: Initialize the counter
        int frequency = 0;
        
        // Step 5: Traverse the string character by character
        for (int i = 0; i < input.length(); i++) {
            // Step 6: Compare each character with the target
            if (input.charAt(i) == target) {
                frequency++;  // Step 7: Increment on match
            }
        }
        
        // Step 8: Display the result
        System.out.println("Frequency of '" + target + "' in \"" + input 
                           + "\" = " + frequency);
        
        // Step 9: Close the scanner (good practice)
        scanner.close();
    }
}
```

### 3.5 Alternative Implementation — Using `toCharArray()`

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class CharacterFrequencyArray {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        System.out.print("Enter a string: ");
        String input = scanner.nextLine();
        
        System.out.print("Enter a character: ");
        char target = scanner.next().charAt(0);
        
        int frequency = 0;
        
        // Convert string to character array and use enhanced for-loop
        for (char ch : input.toCharArray()) {
            if (ch == target) {
                frequency++;
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("Frequency = " + frequency);
        scanner.close();
    }
}
```

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | `charAt()` Approach | `toCharArray()` Approach | Stream API |
|---------|:-------------------:|:------------------------:|:----------:|
| **Code Length** | Moderate | Concise | Concise |
| **Memory Footprint** | $O(1)$ — no extra copy | $O(n)$ — creates a copy | $O(1)$ |
| **Readability for Beginners** | High ★★★★★ | High ★★★★☆ | Low ★★☆☆☆ |
| **KTU Expected Answer** | **YES** | Acceptable | Not in syllabus |
| **Handles Unicode Properly** | Yes | Yes | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommendation for KTU Lab:** Use the `charAt()` approach. It demonstrates explicit understanding of string traversal, loop bounds, and primitive comparison — exactly what examiners look for in the **Algorithm/Logic** section of the mark distribution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the complete control flow of the character frequency algorithm, from program entry to result output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Import java.util.Scanner]
    B --> C[Create Scanner Object]
    C --> D[Read Input String S]
    D --> E[Read Target Character t]
    E --> F[Initialize frequency = 0]
    F --> G[Set i = 0]
    G --> H{i less than S.length?}
    H -- No --> M[Print Final Frequency]
    H -- Yes --> I[Get S.charAt i]
    I --> J{charAt i == t?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Increment frequency]
    J -- No --> L[Skip Increment]
    K --> N[Increment i by 1]
    L --> N
    N --> H
    M --> O[Close Scanner]
    O --> P[Program End]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Diamond shapes** represent decision points (e.g., `i < length`, `charAt(i) == t`).
- **Rectangles** represent processing actions (e.g., increment, read).
- **Rounded rectangles** represent start/end terminators.
- The loop between `H` and `N` is the **traversal phase**, executed exactly $n$ times.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram maps the interaction between Java components during program execution, illustrating the **separation of concerns** essential in OOP design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        IN1[Keyboard Buffer]
    end
    
    subgraph ApplicationLayer[Java Application - main method]
        SC[Scanner Object]
        STR[String Variable]
        CHR[char Target Variable]
        CTR[Integer Counter]
        LOOP[For Loop Controller]
    end
    
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        OUT[Console stdout]
    end
    
    IN1 --> SC
    SC --> STR
    SC --> CHR
    STR --> LOOP
    CHR --> LOOP
    LOOP --> CTR
    CTR --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Memory State Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack[Stack Memory]
        REF[input reference: 0x4A2F]
        TGT[target: g]
        FRQ[frequency: 2]
        IDX[i: 11 - exit loop]
    end
    
    subgraph Heap[Heap Memory - String Pool]
        STR[Programming - 11 chars]
        IDX0["0: P"]
        IDX1["1: r"]
        IDX2["2: o"]
        IDX3["3: g"]
        IDX10["10: g"]
    end
    
    REF -.points to.-> STR
    STR --- IDX0
    STR --- IDX1
    STR --- IDX2
    STR --- IDX3
    STR --- IDX10
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Point:** Notice how the `String` is stored in the **Heap (String Pool)** because strings are objects in Java, while primitive variables (`target`, `frequency`, `i`) are stored in the **Stack**. This memory distinction is a frequently tested concept in KTU theory exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Explain the difference between `==` operator and `.equals()` method in Java with reference to String comparison. Why is `==` valid for comparing `char` data type in this experiment?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `==` Operator | `.equals()` Method |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Compares memory addresses (references) for objects; compares actual values for primitives | Compares the content/character sequence of two objects |
| **Use Case for `char`** | ✅ Valid — compares primitive values directly | ❌ Cannot be used — `char` is not an object |
| **Use Case for `String`** | ❌ Risky — may return `false` even for equal content | ✅ Recommended |

**Why `==` works for `char`:** The `char` data type in Java is a **primitive type** (16-bit Unicode), not an object. For primitives, the `==` operator compares the actual stored values, making it both safe and efficient for character comparison. **(1 Mark)**
**Memory Concept:** Strings are objects stored in heap memory; `==` checks reference equality, which can fail for strings with identical content but different memory locations (e.g., one from String pool, one from `new String()`). **(1 Mark)**
**Conclusion:** In our character frequency program, we use `==` because we are comparing `char` primitives extracted via `charAt()`, not `String` objects. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**What is the significance of the `charAt(int index)` method in Java? What exception is thrown if the index is out of bounds?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **`charAt(int index)`** is a built-in method of the `java.lang.String` class that returns the character located at the specified zero-based index. It has the signature: `public char charAt(int index)`. **(1 Mark)**
- **Significance:** It is the **primary mechanism** for character-by-character traversal of a string in Java. Since strings are immutable and bracket-indexing (`str[i]`) is not permitted, `charAt()` is the canonical accessor. **(1 Mark)**
- **Exception:** If `index` is negative or greater than or equal to `string.length()`, the method throws a **`StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`**, which is a subclass of `IndexOutOfBoundsException`, which in turn extends `RuntimeException`. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Sample Paper]`

**A. (a)** Write a Java program to find the frequency of a given character in a string using `Scanner` for input. Include proper comments and explain the logic. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class FreqChar {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);    // Create Scanner
        
        System.out.print("Enter string: ");
        String str = sc.nextLine();             // Read full line input
        
        System.out.print("Enter character: ");
        char ch = sc.next().charAt(0);          // Read first char of token
        
        int count = 0;                          // Initialize counter
        
        for (int i = 0; i < str.length(); i++) {  // Traverse
            if (str.charAt(i) == ch) {             // Compare
                count++;
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("Frequency of '" + ch + "' = " + count);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Correct class structure and `main` method signature: **1 Mark**
- Proper `Scanner` import and instantiation: **1 Mark**
- Correct reading of string and character: **1 Mark**
- Loop initialization and boundary condition (`i < str.length()`): **1 Mark**
- Correct use of `charAt(i)` and `==` comparison: **1 Mark**
- Counter increment logic: **1 Mark**
- Output statement and resource cleanup: **1 Mark**

---

**A. (b)** Modify the above program to find the frequency of **all characters** in the string and display the result. Use a `HashMap`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class FreqAllChar {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter string: ");
        String str = sc.nextLine().toLowerCase();  // Normalize to lowercase
        
        HashMap<Character, Integer> freqMap = new HashMap<>();
        
        for (int i = 0; i < str.length(); i++) {
            char ch = str.charAt(i);
            if (ch != ' ') {  // Ignore spaces
                freqMap.put(ch, freqMap.getOrDefault(ch, 0) + 1);
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("Character frequencies:");
        for (char key : freqMap.keySet()) {
            System.out.println("'" + key + "' : " + freqMap.get(key));
        }
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Correct `HashMap` declaration with `Character, Integer` generics: **1 Mark**
- Loop traversal of input string: **1 Mark**
- Correct use of `getOrDefault()` for handling new keys: **2 Marks**
- Space filtering (optional but professional): **1 Mark**
- Iteration over `keySet()` to display results: **1 Mark**
- Output formatting and compilation: **1 Mark**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**B. (a)** Explain the concept of **immutability of Strings** in Java. How does it impact the character frequency counting program? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Immutability:** In Java, a `String` object is **immutable**, meaning once a String object is created in memory, its content cannot be changed. Any operation that appears to modify a string (like concatenation or replacement) actually creates a **new String object** in memory. **(2 Marks)**

**How it works internally:** Strings in Java are backed by a `private final char[] value` array inside the `String` class. The `final` keyword ensures the array reference cannot be reassigned, and the array itself is private with no mutator methods exposed. **(2 Marks)**

**Impact on Character Frequency Program:**
1. **Memory Efficiency:** When we read a string using `scanner.nextLine()`, the string is stored in the **String Constant Pool**. Reusing identical literals does not create new objects.
2. **Thread Safety:** Immutability makes strings inherently thread-safe, which is beneficial in multi-threaded applications.
3. **Security:** Strings are used for passwords, file paths, and network connections. Immutability prevents malicious modification.
4. **No In-Place Modification:** Our program correctly uses `charAt(i)` which returns a copy of the character, not a reference to the internal array. We never attempt to modify the string — we only read and count. **(2 Marks)**

**Conclusion:** Immutability is a design choice that prioritizes safety and predictability. Our frequency counting program is fully compatible with this design because it only performs read operations. **(1 Mark)**

---

**B. (b)** Write a Java program to find the **most frequent character** in a given string. If multiple characters have the same highest frequency, display all of them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class MostFrequentChar {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter string: ");
        String str = sc.nextLine().toLowerCase().replace(" ", "");
        
        HashMap<Character, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
        
        // Build frequency map
        for (int i = 0; i < str.length(); i++) {
            char ch = str.charAt(i);
            map.put(ch, map.getOrDefault(ch, 0) + 1);
        }
        
        // Find maximum frequency
        int maxFreq = 0;
        for (int freq : map.values()) {
            if (freq > maxFreq) {
                maxFreq = freq;
            }
        }
        
        // Display all characters with maximum frequency
        System.out.println("Maximum frequency = " + maxFreq);
        System.out.print("Most frequent character(s): ");
        for (char key : map.keySet()) {
            if (map.get(key) == maxFreq) {
                System.out.print("'" + key + "' ");
            }
        }
        System.out.println();
        
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Correct input reading and preprocessing (lowercase, trim spaces): **1 Mark**
- `HashMap` population logic with `getOrDefault`: **2 Marks**
- Finding the maximum frequency value: **1 Mark**
- Iterating to find all characters matching max frequency: **2 Marks**
- Output formatting displaying all tied characters: **1 Mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Off-by-One Error:** Students often write `i <= str.length()` instead of `i < str.length()`, causing a `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using `==` for String Comparison:** Comparing `str.charAt(i) == "g"` (String literal) instead of `'g'` (char literal) causes a **compilation error**. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Not Closing Scanner:** Failing to call `sc.close()` is considered poor practice. **Lose 0.5 mark** in strict valuation.
> 4. **Forgetting `import java.util.Scanner`:** This results in a compilation error. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing `next()` with `nextLine()`:** Using `next()` only reads up to whitespace, which truncates multi-word strings. For full-line input, **always use `nextLine()`**. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 6. **Integer Overflow:** Extremely rare but possible for strings with length approaching `Integer.MAX_VALUE` (≈ 2.1 billion). Use `long` for safety in production code.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Canonical Solution:** Use `for` loop + `charAt(i) + ==` for char comparison.
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — single pass through the string.
- ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — only a counter variable, no auxiliary data structures.
- ✅ **Immutability:** Java Strings cannot be modified after creation; traversal is read-only.
- ✅ **Case Sensitivity:** `'A'` and `'a'` are **different** characters (Unicode values 65 vs 97).
- ✅ **Whitespace Handling:** `charAt()` counts spaces, tabs, and newlines too — filter if needed.
- ✅ **Edge Cases to Test:** Empty string (`""`) → frequency is 0; character not present → frequency is 0.
- ✅ **Scanner Best Practice:** Close the `Scanner` object to prevent resource leaks.
- ✅ **Compiler Requirement:** Always include `import java.util.Scanner;` at the top of the file.
- ✅ **Mark Allocation Reminder:** KTU lab records typically allocate 60% weightage to **Algorithm/Logic** and 40% to **Output/Viva** — focus on explaining your loop boundary and comparison logic clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to multiply two given matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to multiply two given matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Multiplication (KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Definition):**
> Matrix multiplication is a binary operation that produces a resultant matrix **C** by computing the dot product of the rows of the first matrix **A** with the columns of the second matrix **B**. For two matrices **A** of order *m × n* and **B** of order *n × p*, the product **C = A × B** is defined only when the number of columns of **A** equals the number of rows of **B**, producing a resultant matrix **C** of order *m × p*.

In the context of the KTU **PCEOL408 – Object Oriented Programming (Java) Lab**, this experiment is designed to evaluate the following **Course Outcomes (COs)** under the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** framework:

| Bloom's Level | Cognitive Activity | KTU Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 3: Apply** | Implement matrix multiplication using nested `for` loops in Java | CO2 – Apply OOP concepts to solve real-world computational problems |
| **Level 4: Analyze** | Trace loop iterations and validate boundary conditions | CO3 – Debug and test Java programs using standard test data |
| **Level 5: Evaluate** | Verify matrix dimension compatibility and compute correctly | CO4 – Develop modular Java solutions with proper validation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Coffee Shop Bill Calculator" Analogy** ☕
> Imagine a coffee shop chain. There are **3 outlets** (rows of matrix A), each selling **4 different items** (columns of A). The quantity of each item sold is recorded in matrix **A**. The price of each item is given as a column vector **B** (4 rows, 1 column) listing the unit price of each of the 4 items. To compute the total revenue at each outlet, we don't just multiply the matrices directly — we **multiply each row of A with the column of B and sum the products** (this is the dot product). The result is a **3 × 1 matrix** showing the revenue at each of the 3 outlets. This "row-times-column-and-sum" pattern is the **soul of matrix multiplication**.

## 1.3 Matrix Compatibility Rule (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!WARNING]
> **The Golden Rule of Matrix Multiplication:**
> If **A** is *m × n* and **B** is *n × p*, then **C = A × B** exists and is of order *m × p*.
> The **inner dimensions must match**: *n == n*. If they don't match, the program MUST throw an error (typically an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` or a custom message). KTU examiners *deliberately* test cases with mismatched dimensions to check whether students have implemented the **dimension check logic**.

### The Dimension Compatibility Test:

$$A_{(m \times n)} \times B_{(n \times p)} = C_{(m \times p)} \text{ — Only if columns of A = rows of B}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

Matrix multiplication is **NOT** an element-by-element operation (that would be the Hadamard product). Instead, each element $C_{ij}$ of the product matrix is computed as the **scalar dot product** of the *i-th row* of **A** with the *j-th column* of **B**.

### Element-wise Formula:

$$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A_{ik} \times B_{kj}$$

Where:
- **i** = row index of the resultant matrix (from 0 to m-1)
- **j** = column index of the resultant matrix (from 0 to p-1)
- **k** = summation index ranging from 0 to n-1
- **n** = common dimension (columns of A = rows of B)

### Why Three Nested Loops?

The triple-loop structure maps directly to the mathematical formula:

| Loop Level | Index | Purpose | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outer Loop** | `i` (0 to m-1) | Selects the current **row** of A | Fixes the row in the result |
| **Middle Loop** | `j` (0 to p-1) | Selects the current **column** of B | Fixes the column in the result |
| **Inner Loop** | `k` (0 to n-1) | Walks through the common dimension | Performs the summation $\sum$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Complexity Insight:**
> The straightforward matrix multiplication algorithm using nested loops has a time complexity of **O(m × n × p)**. For square matrices of size *n × n*, this simplifies to **O(n³)**. KTU viva questions often ask students to state this complexity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiplication Condition** | $\text{cols}(A) = \text{rows}(B)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Mandatory prerequisite check |
| **Resultant Matrix Order** | $A_{(m \times n)} \times B_{(n \times p)} = C_{(m \times p)}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Asked in viva |
| **Element Computation** | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A_{ik} \cdot B_{kj}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Core derivation |
| **Time Complexity (Square)** | $O(n^3)$ | ⭐⭐ Viva favorite |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(m \times p)$ for result | ⭐ Lab record entry |
| **Non-Commutativity** | $A \times B \neq B \times A$ in general | ⭐⭐ Conceptual question |
| **Identity Element** | $A \times I = I \times A = A$ | ⭐ Conceptual clarity |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Matrix Multiplication Powers the World:**
> 1. **Computer Graphics** – 3D transformations (rotation, scaling, translation) use matrix multiplication on vertex coordinates.
> 2. **Machine Learning** – Neural network forward passes are essentially repeated matrix multiplications.
> 3. **Signal Processing** – Convolution operations in digital filters use matrix algebra.
> 4. **Structural Engineering** – Finite Element Analysis (FEA) solves systems using matrix multiplication.
> 5. **Cryptography** – Hill cipher encryption is a direct application of modular matrix multiplication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Required Before Code)

Let's manually compute the product of two small matrices to understand the algorithm's behavior. Suppose:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}_{(2 \times 3)} \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 8 \\ 9 & 10 \\ 11 & 12 \end{bmatrix}_{(3 \times 2)}$$

Here, **A** is 2 × 3 and **B** is 3 × 2. Since **cols(A) = 3 = rows(B)**, the multiplication is **valid**. The resultant matrix **C** will be of order **2 × 2**.

### Step 1: Compute $C_{00}$ (Row 0 of A, Column 0 of B)

$$C_{00} = A_{00} \times B_{00} + A_{01} \times B_{10} + A_{02} \times B_{20}$$

$$C_{00} = (1 \times 7) + (2 \times 9) + (3 \times 11)$$

$$C_{00} = 7 + 18 + 33 = 58$$

### Step 2: Compute $C_{01}$ (Row 0 of A, Column 1 of B)

$$C_{01} = A_{00} \times B_{01} + A_{01} \times B_{11} + A_{02} \times B_{21}$$

$$C_{01} = (1 \times 8) + (2 \times 10) + (3 \times 12)$$

$$C_{01} = 8 + 20 + 36 = 64$$

### Step 3: Compute $C_{10}$ (Row 1 of A, Column 0 of B)

$$C_{10} = A_{10} \times B_{00} + A_{11} \times B_{10} + A_{12} \times B_{20}$$

$$C_{10} = (4 \times 7) + (5 \times 9) + (6 \times 11)$$

$$C_{10} = 28 + 45 + 66 = 139$$

### Step 4: Compute $C_{11}$ (Row 1 of A, Column 1 of B)

$$C_{11} = A_{10} \times B_{01} + A_{11} \times B_{11} + A_{12} \times B_{21}$$

$$C_{11} = (4 \times 8) + (5 \times 10) + (6 \times 12)$$

$$C_{11} = 32 + 50 + 72 = 154$$

### Final Resultant Matrix:

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 64 \\ 139 & 154 \end{bmatrix}_{(2 \times 2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Trick:** Notice that $C_{00} + C_{11} = 58 + 154 = 212$ and $C_{01} + C_{10} = 64 + 139 = 203$. The trace and the anti-diagonal sums help cross-verify calculations manually before running the program.

## 3.2 Complete Java 17 Implementation

The following is the **board-exam-ready, KTU-compliant** Java program. Every line is annotated for lab record submission.

```java
// File: MatrixMultiplication.java
// Experiment 2: Multiply two given matrices
// KTU 2024 Scheme - PCEOL408 OOP (Java) Lab

import java.util.Scanner;

public class MatrixMultiplication {

    // -------------------- METHOD: Read a matrix from the user --------------------
    public static void readMatrix(int[][] matrix, int rows, int cols, Scanner sc) {
        System.out.println("Enter " + (rows * cols) + " elements row-wise:");
        for (int i = 0; i < rows; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < cols; j++) {
                matrix[i][j] = sc.nextInt();
            }
        }
    }

    // -------------------- METHOD: Display a matrix in grid format --------------------
    public static void displayMatrix(int[][] matrix, int rows, int cols) {
        System.out.println("The matrix is:");
        for (int i = 0; i < rows; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < cols; j++) {
                System.out.print(matrix[i][j] + "\t");
            }
            System.out.println();
        }
    }

    // -------------------- METHOD: Multiply two matrices --------------------
    public static int[][] multiplyMatrices(int[][] A, int[][] B, int m, int n, int p) {
        int[][] C = new int[m][p]; // Resultant matrix of order m x p
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {          // i -> row of A and C
            for (int j = 0; j < p; j++) {      // j -> column of B and C
                C[i][j] = 0;                   // Initialize accumulator
                for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) {  // k -> walks through common dim
                    C[i][j] += A[i][k] * B[k][j];
                }
            }
        }
        return C;
    }

    // -------------------- MAIN METHOD --------------------
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Input dimensions of first matrix A
        System.out.print("Enter rows and columns of Matrix A: ");
        int m = sc.nextInt();
        int n = sc.nextInt();

        int[][] A = new int[m][n];
        readMatrix(A, m, n, sc);

        // Input dimensions of second matrix B
        System.out.print("Enter rows and columns of Matrix B: ");
        int n2 = sc.nextInt();
        int p = sc.nextInt();

        // ----------- KTU MANDATORY DIMENSION CHECK -----------
        if (n != n2) {
            System.out.println("\n[ERROR] Matrix multiplication is NOT possible!");
            System.out.println("Columns of A (" + n + ") must equal rows of B (" + n2 + ").");
            System.out.println("Program terminated.");
            return; // Exit gracefully
        }

        int[][] B = new int[n2][p];
        readMatrix(B, n2, p, sc);

        // Display input matrices
        System.out.println("\n--- Matrix A ---");
        displayMatrix(A, m, n);
        System.out.println("\n--- Matrix B ---");
        displayMatrix(B, n2, p);

        // Compute and display product
        int[][] C = multiplyMatrices(A, B, m, n, p);
        System.out.println("\n--- Resultant Matrix C = A x B ---");
        displayMatrix(C, m, p);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    inputA --> fillA[/Read elements of Matrix A/]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**State and explain the condition for multiplying two matrices. Why is matrix multiplication said to be non-commutative?**

**Model Answer:**
For two matrices **A** of order *m × n* and **B** of order *n × p* to be multiplied, the **number of columns of A must equal the number of rows of B** (the inner dimensions must match). If this holds, the product **A × B** exists and is of order *m × p*.

Matrix multiplication is **non-commutative** because in general **A × B ≠ B × A**. This is because:
- Even if both products exist, they may have different resultant orders.
- Example: If A is 2 × 3 and B is 3 × 2, then A × B is 2 × 2, but B × A is 3 × 3.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the time complexity of the standard matrix multiplication algorithm using three nested loops? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**
The time complexity of the standard matrix multiplication algorithm is **O(n³)** for square matrices (or **O(m × n × p)** for general rectangular matrices). Justification: there are three nested loops — the outer runs *m* times, the middle runs *p* times, and the inner runs *n* times. The body of the innermost loop performs a constant-time multiplication and addition. Hence, the total number of elementary operations is proportional to the product *m × n × p*, which for square matrices simplifies to *n × n × n = n³*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the algorithm to multiply two matrices with a suitable example. State the prerequisite condition for matrix multiplication. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Read the order of the first matrix **A** as *m × n*.
2. Read the order of the second matrix **B** as *n2 × p*.
3. **Check the compatibility condition:** If *n ≠ n2*, print an error message and terminate.
4. Read the elements of both matrices **A** and **B**.
5. Create a resultant matrix **C** of order *m × p*, initialized to 0.
6. Use three nested loops:
   - Outer loop: `i` from 0 to *m − 1*
   - Middle loop: `j` from 0 to *p − 1*
   - Inner loop: `k` from 0 to *n − 1*
   - Update: $C_{ij} = C_{ij} + A_{ik} \times B_{kj}$
7. Display the resultant matrix **C**.

**Example (as derived in Section 3.1):**
A is 2 × 3 and B is 3 × 2. Inner dimension *n = 3 = n2*, so multiplication is valid. After computing, the result is $C = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 64 \\ 139 & 154 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Prerequisite Condition:** The number of columns of the first matrix must equal the number of rows of the second matrix.

---

**(b)** Write a complete Java program to read two matrices from the user, check their dimensional compatibility, multiply them if valid, and display the resultant matrix. Use methods for reading, displaying, and multiplying the matrices. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:** *(Refer to the complete program provided in Section 3.2 of these notes.)*

**Key Implementation Highlights:**
- Three static methods: `readMatrix`, `displayMatrix`, `multiplyMatrices`.
- Mandatory dimension check using `if (n != n2)` before multiplication.
- The product matrix `C` is declared inside `multiplyMatrices` and returned to `main`.
- Proper `Scanner` initialization and closure to avoid resource leaks.
- The inner accumulator is reset to 0 at the start of every $(i, j)$ pair.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the Hadamard (element-wise) product and the standard matrix product. Provide the formula for the standard product element $C_{ij}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Hadamard Product (A ⊙ B) | Standard Matrix Product (A × B) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operation** | Multiplies corresponding elements | Computes row-by-column dot product |
| **Formula** | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} \times B_{ij}$ | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k} A_{ik} \times B_{kj}$ |
| **Dimension Req.** | Both matrices must have **same order** *m × n* | Columns of A must equal rows of B |
| **Result Order** | Same as inputs *m × n* | *m × p* (may differ) |
| **Commutativity** | Yes, A ⊙ B = B ⊙ A | No, A × B ≠ B × A generally |

**Standard Product Formula:**

$$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A_{ik} \cdot B_{kj}$$

This represents the dot product of the *i-th row* of A and the *j-th column* of B.

---

**(b)** Write a Java program to multiply two 3 × 3 matrices entered by the user. Validate the result by computing the sum of all elements of the resultant matrix and printing it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Matrix3x3 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        int[][] A = new int[3][3];
        int[][] B = new int[3][3];
        int[][] C = new int[3][3];
        int sum = 0;

        System.out.println("Enter 9 elements of Matrix A (3x3):");
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++)
            for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++)
                A[i][j] = sc.nextInt();

        System.out.println("Enter 9 elements of Matrix B (3x3):");
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++)
            for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++)
                B[i][j] = sc.nextInt();

        // Standard matrix multiplication - dimensions match automatically
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
                C[i][j] = 0;
                for (int k = 0; k < 3; k++) {
                    C[i][j] += A[i][k] * B[k][j];
                }
                sum += C[i][j];
            }
        }

        System.out.println("Resultant Matrix C:");
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
                System.out.print(C[i][j] + "\t");
            }
            System.out.println();
        }
        System.out.println("Sum of all elements of C: " + sum);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to initialize `C[i][j] = 0` before the inner loop** — In Java, `int` arrays are zero-initialized by default, so it *happens* to work, but if you reuse the array (e.g., reading the result into a pre-filled matrix), this will cause wrong answers. **Always explicitly reset to 0.** Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missing in the lab record.
> 2. **Confusing row and column indexing** — `A[i][k] * B[k][j]` is correct; `A[i][k] * B[j][k]` is a common wrong answer that gives the Hadamard-like wrong result. Pay attention to the position of `j`.
> 3. **Skipping the dimension check** — KTU lab record requires explicit validation. A program that crashes with `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` for mismatched dimensions loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not closing the Scanner** — Minor but examiners in stricter valuation cycles deduct a mark.
> 5. **Hardcoding the dimensions** — Use variables `m`, `n`, `p` instead of magic numbers like `3`. This shows proper OOP thinking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Matrix Multiplication in Java**
>
> ✅ **Mathematical Rule:** $A_{(m \times n)} \times B_{(n \times p)} = C_{(m \times p)}$ — Inner dimensions MUST match.
>
> ✅ **Element Formula:** $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} A_{ik} \times B_{kj}$ — always write this on the board before coding.
>
> ✅ **Three Nested Loops** — `i` for rows, `j` for columns, `k` for the common dimension. Mnemonic: **"I-J-K, sum and multiply"**.
>
> ✅ **Initialization:** Always set `C[i][j] = 0` before the inner `k` loop starts to accumulate.
>
> ✅ **Dimension Check is MANDATORY** — Implement `if (colsA != rowsB)` before the multiplication. Print a friendly error and `return` from `main`.
>
> ✅ **Method Modularity** — Use at least three methods: `readMatrix`, `displayMatrix`, `multiplyMatrices`. Static methods are preferred for a lab program.
>
> ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(m \times n \times p)$ general, $O(n^3)$ for square matrices.
>
> ✅ **Non-Commutativity:** $A \times B \neq B \times A$ in general. The product order is NOT symmetric.
>
> ✅ **Edge Cases to Test:** (1) 2×3 × 3×2 → 2×2 result. (2) 3×3 × 3×3 → 3×3 result. (3) 2×2 × 3×2 → ERROR. (4) Multiplication with zero matrix → zero result.
>
> ✅ **Java-Specific Tip:** Use `"\t"` (tab) in `print` for clean matrix alignment in the console output.
>
> ✅ **Scanner Hygiene:** Always `sc.close()` at the end of `main` to prevent resource leak warnings.
>
> ✅ **Lab Record Format:** Include Aim, Algorithm, Source Code, Output Screenshot, Result, and Viva Questions in the prescribed KTU lab manual format.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a menu-driven Java program for vector operations: create a vector of 5 student names, insert an element at the third position, insert 3 elements at the end, delete an element from the second position, and delete all elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> The `java.util.Vector` class implements a **growable array of objects**. It is a legacy class (available since JDK 1.0) that implements the `List<E>` interface and stores elements in **contiguous memory** with **automatic capacity expansion**. It is **synchronized** (thread-safe) by default, distinguishing it from `ArrayList`.

The experiment requires the use of the following **Vector class methods** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme OOP-Java Lab syllabus:

- `addElement(E obj)` — appends an element at the end
- `add(int index, E element)` — inserts an element at the specified position
- `remove(int index)` — deletes the element at the given index
- `removeAllElements()` / `clear()` — deletes every element
- `size()` — returns the number of elements currently stored
- `capacity()` — returns the current storage capacity (default **10**, doubles on overflow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL408):**
> The student is expected to use a `Vector<String>` (generic, type-safe) and drive the operations through a `do-while` loop with a `switch` statement (menu-driven structure). Index positions in this experiment follow **0-based indexing**:
> - *Third position* = index `2`
> - *Second position* = index `1`

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Class Register That Grows":**
> Imagine a **physical attendance register** used by a class teacher. Initially, it has empty rows. As students are admitted, the teacher writes their names in sequence. The register has two special features:
>
> 1. **It auto-grows** — when all pages are filled, the teacher can paste additional blank pages (this is what *capacity expansion* does in Vector — by default, it **doubles** its size when full).
> 2. **Rows can be inserted/deleted** — to insert a new student in the **3rd row**, the teacher shifts everyone from row 3 onwards downward by one. Similarly, deleting the **2nd row** shifts everyone upward.
>
> A `Vector<String>` behaves exactly the same: it stores student names, expands automatically, and supports **indexed insertion and deletion** with automatic shifting of subsequent elements.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary for the Experiment

| Term | Meaning in this Experiment |
|---|---|
| **Vector** | Dynamic, resizable array (`java.util.Vector`) |
| **Menu-driven program** | Program that displays a list of choices; user picks one via `switch` |
| **Element** | A single student name (a `String` object) |
| **Position vs Index** | "Position" is human-friendly (1, 2, 3…); "index" is Java-internal (0, 1, 2…) |
| **Capacity** | Total slots allocated (initial default = **10**) |
| **Size** | Number of slots currently occupied |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Students often confuse *position* with *index*. If the question says "insert at the **3rd position**", you must use `v.add(2, element)` because the 1st position is `index 0`, 2nd is `index 1`, 3rd is `index 2`. Examiners will deduct marks for off-by-one errors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Vector Method

### Step 1 — `addElement(E obj)` / `add(E obj)`
- Appends the object to the **end** of the vector.
- Increments `size` by 1.
- If `size` exceeds `capacity`, the vector **doubles its capacity** (growth policy: `capacity = 2 × old capacity`).
- **Time complexity:** amortized **O(1)**.

### Step 2 — `add(int index, E element)`
- Inserts `element` at the given `index`.
- All elements currently at `index` and beyond are **shifted one position to the right** to make room.
- Throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` if `index < 0` or `index > size`.
- **Time complexity:** **O(n)** due to shifting.

### Step 3 — `remove(int index)`
- Removes the element at the given `index`.
- All elements beyond `index` are **shifted one position to the left**.
- Returns the removed element.
- Throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` if `index < 0` or `index >= size`.
- **Time complexity:** **O(n)**.

### Step 4 — `removeAllElements()` / `clear()`
- Removes **all elements** from the vector.
- `size` becomes `0`, but `capacity` remains unchanged.
- **Time complexity:** **O(n)**.

### Step 5 — `size()` and `capacity()`
- `size()` → number of elements currently stored.
- `capacity()` → total slots available before expansion is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Vectors underpin dynamic data structures used in **compiler symbol tables, network packet buffers, real-time scheduling queues, and GUI event queues**. Understanding the cost of insertion/deletion (`O(n)`) versus access (`O(1)`) is fundamental to choosing the right data structure for performance-critical systems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Vector Mechanics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Vector Reference Table for Lab Viva & ESE**

| Operation | Method Signature | Returns | Index Range Allowed | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Add at end | `boolean add(E e)` | `true` | — | amortized **O(1)** |
| Legacy add at end | `void addElement(E obj)` | `void` | — | amortized **O(1)** |
| Insert at index | `void add(int i, E e)` | `void` | `0 ≤ i ≤ size` | **O(n)** |
| Get element | `E get(int i)` | element | `0 ≤ i < size` | **O(1)** |
| Remove by index | `E remove(int i)` | removed element | `0 ≤ i < size` | **O(n)** |
| Remove first occurrence | `boolean remove(Object o)` | `true/false` | — | **O(n)** |
| Remove all | `void removeAllElements()` | `void` | — | **O(n)** |
| Remove all (modern) | `void clear()` | `void` | — | **O(n)** |
| Count elements | `int size()` | count | — | **O(1)** |
| Total slots | `int capacity()` | slots | — | **O(1)** |

### Capacity Growth Rule

$$C_{new} = \begin{cases} C_{initial} = 10 & \text{if first overflow} \\ 2 \cdot C_{old} & \text{on every subsequent overflow} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The doubling growth policy ensures that the **amortized cost** of `add` remains `O(1)`, even though individual operations occasionally cost `O(n)`. This is the same principle used in `ArrayList`, `HashMap` (bucket arrays), and dynamic memory allocators in operating systems.

## 2.3 Vector vs ArrayList — Examiner's Quick Check

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *"Differentiate between Vector and ArrayList."* Memorize this:

| Feature | `Vector` | `ArrayList` |
|---|---|---|
| Introduced in | JDK **1.0** (legacy) | JDK **1.2** |
| Thread-safety | **Synchronized** (thread-safe) | **Not synchronized** (faster) |
| Performance | Slower (sync overhead) | Faster |
| Growth policy | Doubles (2×) | Grows by **1.5×** |
| Iterator | `Enumeration` + `Iterator` | `Iterator` + `ListIterator` only |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In modern KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the use of `Enumeration` for iteration is **acceptable** in Vector-specific questions but `Iterator`/`for-each` is preferred. Avoid mixing `Enumeration` with `ArrayList` — it is illegal and will cause compilation errors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Program Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete, Production-Ready Java Program

```java
// Experiment 2: Menu-driven Vector Operations on Student Names
// KTU 2024 Scheme | PCEOL408 | OOP-Java Lab

import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.Vector;

public class VectorMenu {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        Vector<String> v = new Vector<String>();   // creates empty vector
        int choice;

        do {
            // ---------- MENU DISPLAY ----------
            System.out.println("\n========== VECTOR OPERATIONS MENU ==========");
            System.out.println("1. Create a vector of 5 student names");
            System.out.println("2. Insert an element at the 3rd position");
            System.out.println("3. Insert 3 elements at the end");
            System.out.println("4. Delete an element from the 2nd position");
            System.out.println("5. Delete all elements");
            System.out.println("6. Display vector (with size & capacity)");
            System.out.println("7. Exit");
            System.out.print("Enter your choice: ");
            choice = sc.nextInt();
            sc.nextLine();    // consume trailing newline

            // ---------- SWITCH DRIVER ----------
            switch (choice) {
                case 1:
                    v.clear();   // ensure fresh start
                    System.out.println("Enter 5 student names:");
                    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
                        System.out.print("  Name " + (i + 1) + ": ");
                        v.add(sc.nextLine());
                    }
                    System.out.println("Vector created successfully.");
                    break;

                case 2:
                    System.out.print("Enter the name to insert at 3rd position: ");
                    String elem = sc.nextLine();
                    v.add(2, elem);  // index 2 = 3rd position
                    System.out.println("Element inserted at index 2.");
                    break;

                case 3:
                    System.out.println("Enter 3 student names to append:");
                    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
                        System.out.print("  Name " + (i + 1) + ": ");
                        v.add(sc.nextLine());
                    }
                    System.out.println("3 elements appended to the end.");
                    break;

                case 4:
                    if (v.size() >= 2) {
                        String removed = v.remove(1);   // index 1 = 2nd position
                        System.out.println("Removed: " + removed);
                    } else {
                        System.out.println("Vector has fewer than 2 elements.");
                    }
                    break;

                case 5:
                    v.removeAllElements();   // (or v.clear();)
                    System.out.println("All elements deleted.");
                    break;

                case 6:
                    System.out.println("Vector contents : " + v);
                    System.out.println("Current size    : " + v.size());
                    System.out.println("Current capacity: " + v.capacity());
                    break;

                case 7:
                    System.out.println("Exiting program. Goodbye!");
                    break;

                default:
                    System.out.println("Invalid choice. Please try again.");
            }
        } while (choice != 7);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Sample Dry-Run Execution Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **The following trace demonstrates every menu option in sequence.** This is exactly the kind of output KTU examiners expect students to show in the **observation** section of the lab record.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Position → Index Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping table for the KTU lab record (Viva-favourite):**

| Menu Operation | User says | Index used in `v.method(index, …)` | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Insert at 3rd position | "3rd position" | `2` | Positions are 1-based: pos 3 = idx 2 |
| Insert 3 elements at end | "at the end" | no index | `v.add(x)` appends |
| Delete from 2nd position | "2nd position" | `1` | Positions are 1-based: pos 2 = idx 1 |
| Delete all elements | "delete all" | no index | `v.clear()` / `removeAllElements()` |
| Display | — | — | `System.out.println(v)` |

## 3.4 Underlying Memory Mechanics (For Theory Backs)

$$v = [e_0, e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4] \quad (\text{size}=5,\ \text{capacity}=10)$$

**After `v.add(2, e_{new})`:**

$$v = [e_0, e_1, e_{new}, e_2, e_3, e_4] \quad (\text{size}=6)$$

**After three `v.add(x)`:**

$$v = [e_0, e_1, e_{new}, e_2, e_3, e_4, x_1, x_2, x_3] \quad (\text{size}=9)$$

**After `v.remove(1)`:**

$$v = [e_0, e_{new}, e_2, e_3, e_4, x_1, x_2, x_3] \quad (\text{size}=8)$$

**After `v.removeAllElements()`:**

$$v = [] \quad (\text{size}=0,\ \text{capacity}=10)$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual Recall)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEOL408]**

### Q1. Define the `Vector` class in Java. Mention any **two** key methods.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `java.util.Vector` class is a **legacy, resizable-array** implementation of the `List` interface, introduced in JDK 1.0. It stores elements in a **dynamic array** that grows automatically (default capacity **10**, doubled on overflow) and is **synchronized** for thread-safety.
Two key methods:
1. `addElement(E obj)` — appends element at the end.
2. `removeAllElements()` — removes every element from the vector.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCEOL408]**

### Q2. Differentiate between `Vector` and `ArrayList` in Java.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Vector | ArrayList |
|---|---|---|
| Thread-safe | Yes (synchronized) | No (not synchronized) |
| Performance | Slower | Faster |
| Growth | Doubles (2×) | 1.5× growth |
| Legacy? | Yes (JDK 1.0) | No (JDK 1.2) |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Program / Analytical — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, OR a single 14-mark program. The student attempts **ONE of the two choices** (A or B).

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEOL408]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to create a `Vector` of 5 student names, accept them from the user, and display the vector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to insert an element at the **3rd position** and append **3 more elements** to the end of the same vector, displaying the vector after each operation. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Program (7 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class VectorCreate {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        Vector<String> v = new Vector<String>();

        System.out.println("Enter 5 student names:");
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
            v.add(sc.nextLine());
        }

        System.out.println("Vector elements: " + v);
        System.out.println("Size = " + v.size() + ", Capacity = " + v.capacity());
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

*Evaluation breakdown (7 marks):*
- Class & imports: 1 mark
- `Vector<String>` declaration: 1 mark
- Loop to read input: 2 marks
- Display: 1 mark
- Size/capacity: 1 mark
- Output/compilation: 1 mark

**(b) Program (7 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class VectorInsert {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        Vector<String> v = new Vector<String>();

        // Pre-populate or read 5 names (as in part a)
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) v.add(sc.nextLine());
        System.out.println("Initial: " + v);

        // Insert at 3rd position
        System.out.print("Enter name to insert at 3rd position: ");
        v.add(2, sc.nextLine());
        System.out.println("After insert at 3rd: " + v);

        // Append 3 elements
        System.out.println("Enter 3 names to append:");
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) v.add(sc.nextLine());
        System.out.println("After appending 3: " + v);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

*Evaluation breakdown (7 marks):*
- Index 2 explanation: 1 mark
- `add(2, x)` usage: 2 marks
- `add(x)` for appending: 1 mark
- Loop for 3 appends: 1 mark
- Display after each: 1 mark
- Compilation/output: 1 mark

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCEOL408]**

**(a)** Write a Java program to **delete an element from the 2nd position** of a pre-existing vector of student names and display the updated vector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to **delete all elements** of the vector using both `removeAllElements()` and `clear()` methods, and explain the difference. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Program (7 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class VectorDelete {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        Vector<String> v = new Vector<String>();

        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) v.add(sc.nextLine());
        System.out.println("Before delete: " + v);

        // 2nd position = index 1
        String removed = v.remove(1);
        System.out.println("Removed element: " + removed);
        System.out.println("After delete: " + v);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**(b) Program + Explanation (7 marks):**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class VectorClear {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vector<String> v = new Vector<String>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) v.add("Student" + i);
        System.out.println("Size before: " + v.size() + ", Capacity: " + v.capacity());

        v.removeAllElements();   // legacy method
        System.out.println("After removeAllElements -> size: " + v.size());

        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) v.add("X" + i);
        v.clear();               // modern method (Collection framework)
        System.out.println("After clear -> size: " + v.size() + ", Capacity: " + v.capacity());
    }
}
```

**Explanation (for 7 marks):**
- `removeAllElements()` is the **legacy method** (since JDK 1.0) — it removes all elements, sets `size = 0`, but `capacity` is **unchanged**.
- `clear()` is the **modern method** (from `Collection` interface, JDK 1.2+) — it does the same job as `removeAllElements()` and is the **preferred** style.
- Both are **functionally equivalent**; the choice is a matter of style and API consistency.

*Evaluation breakdown:*
- Correct method usage: 2 marks
- Functional difference: 2 marks
- Capacity behavior: 1 mark
- Display statements: 1 mark
- Compilation/output: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error:** Writing `v.add(3, x)` for "3rd position" — this inserts at *4th* position. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Using `ArrayList` instead of `Vector`:** The question specifically demands `Vector`. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting `sc.nextLine()` after `sc.nextInt()`:** Causes a `String` input to be skipped. **Cost: 1 mark (program "fails" one test case).**
> 4. **Not closing the `Scanner`:** Minor but penalized in best-practice rubrics. **Cost: 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing `removeAllElements()` with `removeAll(Collection)`.** They are different. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Not displaying the vector after each operation** — examiners expect observable evidence of every operation. **Cost: 1 mark per missed display.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU viva & ESE:**

- ✅ `Vector` is in the package **`java.util`** — always `import java.util.Vector;`
- ✅ Use **generics** `Vector<String>` to make it type-safe (KTU 2024 mandates this).
- ✅ **Default initial capacity = 10.** Growth is by **doubling** (`capacity = 2 × old`).
- ✅ **Position-to-index rule:** pos `n` → index `n − 1`.
  - 3rd position → `index 2`
  - 2nd position → `index 1`
- ✅ **Insertion** (`add(int, E)`) and **deletion** (`remove(int)`) cause **shifting** → both are **O(n)**.
- ✅ **Appending** (`add(E)`) is amortized **O(1)**.
- ✅ To **delete all** elements, use `v.clear()` (modern) or `v.removeAllElements()` (legacy) — both set `size = 0` but **do not shrink capacity**.
- ✅ `Vector` is **synchronized** (thread-safe); `ArrayList` is **not** (faster).
- ✅ For iteration, prefer the enhanced `for` loop or `Iterator`. Avoid concurrent modification during iteration.
- ✅ Always `close()` your `Scanner` to free the resource.
- ✅ Display the vector **after every operation** in the lab record to demonstrate correctness.
- ✅ Exception to remember: `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` for invalid indices.
- ✅ Common Vector constructors: `new Vector<>()` (cap 10), `new Vector<>(int initialCapacity)`, `new Vector<>(int initialCapacity, int capacityIncrement)`, `new Vector<>(Collection<? extends E> c)`.
