```java
public class Student {
    // ---- Instance variables (state of every Student object) ----
    int rollno;
    String name;

    // ---- Method 1: Populate the instance variables ----
    void insertRecord(int r, String n) {
        this.rollno = r;
        this.name   = n;
    }

    // ---- Method 2: Display the instance variables ----
    void displayInformation() {
        System.out.println(rollno + " " + name);
    }

    // ---- Entry point of the program ----
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Declare two reference variables of type Student
        Student s1 = new Student();
        Student s2 = new Student();

        // Step 2: Populate each object using insertRecord
        s1.insertRecord(101, "Anu");
        s2.insertRecord(102, "Ravi");

        // Step 3: Display each object's data using displayInformation
        s1.displayInformation();
        s2.displayInformation();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Expected Output (What the Examiner Will Run)

When the KTU examiner compiles and executes the above program using `javac Student.java` followed by `java Student`, the console output will be:

```
101 Anu
102 Ravi
```

If the program produces *exactly* this output, full marks are awarded in the **execution** component of the lab evaluation.

## 3.3 Line-by-Line Analytical Matrix (Exhaustive)

| Line No. | Code Construct | Detailed Explanation | Why KTU Marks It |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `// File: Student.java` | Comment — file name must be `Student.java` because the class is `public`. | Compulsory for `public class` compilation. |
| 2 | `// KTU PCEOL408 ...` | Header comment stating experiment number. | Lab record requirement. |
| 4 | `public class Student {` | Opens the class. The file name *must* be `Student.java` since the class is `public`. | Defines the blueprint. |
| 6 | `int rollno;` | Primitive `int` instance variable. Default value `0`. | State declaration #1. |
| 7 | `String name;` | Reference-type instance variable. Default value `null`. | State declaration #2. |
| 10 | `void insertRecord(int r, String n) {` | Instance method returning `void`; takes two parameters. | Behaviour #1. |
| 11 | `this.rollno = r;` | Assigns parameter `r` to the current object's `rollno`. The `this` keyword is implicit but good practice. | Demonstrates `this`. |
| 12 | `this.name = n;` | Assigns parameter `n` to the current object's `name`. | Demonstrates `this`. |
| 13 | `}` | Closes `insertRecord`. | — |
| 16 | `void displayInformation() {` | No-parameter instance method. | Behaviour #2. |
| 17 | `System.out.println(...)` | Prints `rollno` and `name` separated by a space. | Standard output. |
| 18 | `}` | Closes `displayInformation`. | — |
| 21 | `public static void main(String[] args) {` | JVM entry point. Must be `public static void` with `String[] args` parameter. | Compulsory. |
| 23 | `Student s1 = new Student();` | Declares reference `s1` on the stack, allocates new `Student` object on the heap, assigns reference. | Object creation #1. |
| 24 | `Student s2 = new Student();` | Declares reference `s2`, allocates a *second*, independent `Student` object on the heap. | Object creation #2. |
| 27 | `s1.insertRecord(101, "Anu");` | Invokes `insertRecord` on `s1` — `this` points to `s1`. | Method call #1. |
| 28 | `s2.insertRecord(102, "Ravi");` | Invokes `insertRecord` on `s2` — `this` points to `s2`. | Method call #2. |
| 31 | `s1.displayInformation();` | Prints `s1`'s data: `101 Anu`. | Output #1. |
| 32 | `s2.displayInformation();` | Prints `s2`'s data: `102 Ravi`. | Output #2. |
| 33 | `}` | Closes `main`. | — |
| 34 | `}` | Closes the `Student` class. | — |

## 3.4 Memory Diagram — Step-by-Step Trace

Let us trace the memory state of the program as it executes.

**Step 1:** Before `new` is executed for `s1` — only the `main` stack frame exists, and the local `Student s1` and `Student s2` references are uninitialised (conceptually `null`).

**Step 2:** After `Student s1 = new Student();` — a `Student` object appears on the heap, containing `rollno = 0` and `name = null`. The reference `s1` on the stack now points to this heap object.

**Step 3:** After `Student s2 = new Student();` — a *second*, independent `Student` object appears on the heap, also containing `rollno = 0` and `name = null`. The reference `s2` on the stack points to this new object.

**Step 4:** During `s1.insertRecord(101, "Anu");` — a new stack frame is pushed for `insertRecord`. Inside that frame, `this` holds the address of `s1`, and parameters `r = 101`, `n = "Anu"` exist. After `this.rollno = r;` and `this.name = n;` execute, the heap object pointed to by `s1` is mutated to `rollno = 101`, `name = "Anu"`. The stack frame is then popped.

**Step 5:** During `s2.insertRecord(102, "Ravi");` — analogous to Step 4, but `this` holds the address of `s2`. The second heap object becomes `rollno = 102`, `name = "Ravi"`.

**Step 6:** During `s1.displayInformation();` — a stack frame is pushed, `this` is `s1`, and the body executes `System.out.println(101 + " " + "Anu")` which outputs `101 Anu`.

**Step 7:** During `s2.displayInformation();` — analogous, outputs `102 Ravi`.

## 3.5 Common Variants Accepted by KTU Examiners

The KTU examiner is **expected** to accept any logically correct variant of the program, including:

1. **Using `Scanner` for input** — `Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);` followed by `s1.insertRecord(sc.nextInt(), sc.next());`.
2. **Using constructors instead of `insertRecord`** — `Student(int r, String n) { rollno = r; name = n; }`.
3. **Using arrays of objects** — `Student[] batch = new Student[2];` then populating each element.
4. **Using `printf` instead of `println`** — `System.out.printf("%d %s%n", rollno, name);`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Pitfall to Avoid:** Do *not* declare `rollno` and `name` inside `main` — they *must* be class-level instance variables, otherwise they will not be accessible from `insertRecord` and `displayInformation` which are separate methods.

## 3.6 Compilation & Execution Commands (Required in Lab Record)

For the lab record, the student must write the terminal commands used to compile and run the program:

```bash
javac Student.java
java  Student
```

The first command (`javac`) invokes the Java compiler and produces `Student.class` (the bytecode file). The second command (`java`) invokes the JVM, which loads the bytecode, locates the `main` method, and begins execution. Writing these two commands in the lab record is mandatory and carries marks under the *"Procedure"* section of the KTU lab evaluation rubric.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — The `Student` Class Structure

The following class diagram visualises the formal structure of the `Student` class as defined in this experiment, using the standard UML class-diagram convention (class name, attributes, operations).

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        +int rollno
        +String name
        +void insertRecord(int r, String n)
        +void displayInformation()
    }
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the `+` symbol denotes **public** access. Since we did not use the `private` keyword in the program, the KTU-expected default visibility is package-private. The `+` is shown here for UML clarity. If the examiner specifically wants `private` fields (which is best practice for **encapsulation**), the modifier would change to `-`, but the experiment statement does not require it.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Flow — Object Creation & Method Invocation Lifecycle

This block-level flow diagram shows the **runtime sequence** of events from the moment the JVM starts executing `main` until the program terminates, including memory allocation, method dispatch, and output generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JVM starts main] --> B[Declare Student s1 and s2 on stack]
    B --> C[new Student allocates Heap Object 1]
    C --> D[new Student allocates Heap Object 2]
    D --> E[Call s1.insertRecord 101 Anu]
    E --> F[Push stack frame for insertRecord]
    F --> G[Set this.rollno to 101]
    G --> H[Set this.name to Anu]
    H --> I[Pop stack frame]
    I --> J[Call s2.insertRecord 102 Ravi]
    J --> K[Push stack frame for insertRecord]
    K --> L[Set this.rollno to 102]
    L --> M[Set this.name to Ravi]
    M --> N[Pop stack frame]
    N --> O[Call s1.displayInformation]
    O --> P[Print 101 Anu to console]
    P --> Q[Call s2.displayInformation]
    Q --> R[Print 102 Ravi to console]
    R --> S[JVM terminates program]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Interaction Between `main` and the Two Objects

The sequence diagram below captures the *temporal order* of method calls and the implicit `this` binding at the moment of each call.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as main method
    participant S1 as Student Object 1
    participant S2 as Student Object 2
    participant CO as Console Output

    M->>S1: new Student()
    Note over S1: rollno = 0, name = null
    M->>S2: new Student()
    Note over S2: rollno = 0, name = null
    M->>S1: insertRecord(101, "Anu")
    Note over S1: rollno = 101, name = "Anu"
    M->>S2: insertRecord(102, "Ravi")
    Note over S2: rollno = 102, name = "Ravi"
    M->>S1: displayInformation()
    S1->>CO: println 101 Anu
    M->>S2: displayInformation()
    S2->>CO: println 102 Ravi
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Lifecycle of a `Student` Object

This diagram shows the four key lifecycle states of any `Student` object created in this experiment.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialised: Reference declared
    Uninitialised --> Allocated: new Student invoked
    Allocated --> Populated: insertRecord called
    Populated --> Displayed: displayInformation called
    Displayed --> [*]: Program terminates, GC reclaims heap
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"when is the Student object destroyed?"*, the correct KTU-board answer is: *"The object becomes eligible for **garbage collection (GC)** when its last reference goes out of scope. The JVM's GC thread reclaims the heap memory at an unspecified later time. Java does not support manual `delete`."*

## 4.5 ASCII Memory Snapshot — Two Objects on the Heap

```
        STACK                              HEAP
    +-------------+                 +---------------------+
    | main frame  |                 |   Student Object 1  |
    |-------------|                 |---------------------|
    |  s1  -------|---------------> |  rollno = 101       |
    |             |                 |  name   = "Anu"     |
    |  s2  -------|-----+           +---------------------+
    |             |     |
    |             |     |           +---------------------+
    |             |     |           |   Student Object 2  |
    |             |     +---------> |---------------------|
    |             |                 |  rollno = 102       |
    |             |                 |  name   = "Ravi"    |
    +-------------+                 +---------------------+
```

This memory snapshot is the single most-likely diagram the KTU examiner will ask the student to draw in the **viva voce** component. Memorise the layout: **references on the stack, objects on the heap, arrows connecting them.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam Paper, Set A]`

**Q: Define a *class* and an *object* in Java. How are they related? Mention the role of the `new` keyword.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** A *class* is a user-defined blueprint or prototype in Java that declares instance variables (data members) and methods (member functions). It does not occupy heap memory itself; its definition resides in the JVM's method area (Metaspace).

- **(1 mark)** An *object* is a runtime instance of a class, created on the heap. Each object has its own copy of the instance variables defined by the class and shares the methods (one copy of the bytecode, but each invocation operates on the calling object's data via the `this` reference).

- **(1 mark)** The `new` keyword dynamically allocates heap memory for a new object of the specified class and returns a reference to that memory location, which is then assigned to a reference-type variable (e.g., `Student s1 = new Student();`). Without `new`, no object exists and the reference variable would hold `null`.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Q: What is the difference between an *instance variable* and a *local variable* in Java? Give one example of each from the `Student` class used in Module Experiment 2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1.5 marks)** An *instance variable* is declared **inside a class but outside any method**. It belongs to the object, is allocated on the heap, and is automatically initialised to a default value (`0` for `int`, `null` for `String`). In the `Student` class, `int rollno;` and `String name;` are instance variables.

- **(1.5 marks)** A *local variable* is declared **inside a method body or parameter list**. It belongs to the method's stack frame, exists only for the duration of that method call, and **must be explicitly initialised** before use — Java does not assign a default value to local variables. In the `Student` class, the parameters `int r` and `String n` of `insertRecord` are local variables.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in lab-based courses often appear in the form of *"Write the program / explain the concept / trace the memory"* combinations. Below, two fully independent 14-mark questions are provided. In the actual KTU ESE, the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam]`

**Q: Write a Java program to define a class `Student` with two instance variables `rollno` (int) and `name` (String). Include a method `insertRecord(int r, String n)` to assign values and a method `displayInformation()` to print the values. In the `main` method, create **three** `Student` objects, populate them with the data `(1, "Arjun")`, `(2, "Meera")`, and `(3, "Kiran")` using `insertRecord`, and display all three records using `displayInformation()`.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Write the complete Java program.

**Model Solution:**

```java
// File: Student.java
public class Student {
    int rollno;
    String name;

    void insertRecord(int r, String n) {
        this.rollno = r;
        this.name   = n;
    }

    void displayInformation() {
        System.out.println(rollno + " " + name);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();
        Student s2 = new Student();
        Student s3 = new Student();

        s1.insertRecord(1, "Arjun");
        s2.insertRecord(2, "Meera");
        s3.insertRecord(3, "Kiran");

        s1.displayInformation();
        s2.displayInformation();
        s3.displayInformation();
    }
}
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Class declaration with two instance variables: **1 mark**
- `insertRecord` method with `this` usage: **1.5 marks**
- `displayInformation` method: **1.5 marks**
- `main` method creating three objects: **1.5 marks**
- Correct output: **1 mark**
- Proper indentation and compilation success: **0.5 mark**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Draw the memory diagram showing the three objects on the heap and the references on the stack. What is the role of the `this` keyword in this program?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Memory Diagram (4 marks):**

```
        STACK                              HEAP
    +----------------+              +---------------------+
    | main frame     |              |  Student Object 1   |
    |----------------|              |---------------------|
    |  s1  ----------|------------> |  rollno = 1         |
    |                |              |  name   = "Arjun"   |
    |  s2  ----------|-----+        +---------------------+
    |                |     |
    |                |     |        +---------------------+
    |                |     +----->  |  Student Object 2   |
    |                |              |---------------------|
    |  s3  ----------|------|       |  rollno = 2         |
    |                |      |       |  name   = "Meera"   |
    +----------------+      |       +---------------------+
                            |
                            |       +---------------------+
                            +-----> |  Student Object 3   |
                                    |---------------------|
                                    |  rollno = 3         |
                                    |  name   = "Kiran"   |
                                    +---------------------+
```

**Role of `this` (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) `this` is an **implicit reference variable** automatically created by the JVM for every instance method.
- (1 mark) It always points to the **current invoking object** — i.e., the object on which the method was called.
- (1 mark) In `insertRecord`, `this.rollno = r;` distinguishes the instance variable `rollno` from a potential local variable or parameter of the same name, and ensures the assignment is made to the *correct* object's field — without `this`, calling `s1.insertRecord(...)` and `s2.insertRecord(...)` would both modify the same memory if Java had no `this` mechanism.

**Expected Output of the Program (for examiner's reference):**
```
1 Arjun
2 Meera
3 Kiran
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: Consider the following Java class and answer the questions that follow.**

```java
public class Student {
    int rollno;
    String name;

    void insertRecord(int r, String n) {
        this.rollno = r;
        this.name   = n;
    }

    void displayInformation() {
        System.out.println(rollno + " " + name);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();
        Student s2 = new Student();
        s1.insertRecord(201, "Devika");
        s2.insertRecord(202, "Rahul");
        s1.displayInformation();
        s2.displayInformation();
    }
}
```

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Predict the exact output of the program. Explain, step by step, what happens in memory when each of the following lines is executed: (i) `Student s1 = new Student();`, (ii) `s1.insertRecord(201, "Devika");`, (iii) `s1.displayInformation();`.

**Model Solution:**

**Exact Output (1 mark):**
```
201 Devika
202 Rahul
```

**Step-by-step memory explanation (6 marks):**

**(i) `Student s1 = new Student();` (2 marks)**
- The keyword `new` requests the JVM to allocate contiguous heap memory large enough to hold the two instance fields (`int rollno` — 4 bytes, `String name` — 8 bytes on 64-bit JVM) plus object header overhead.
- A new `Student` object is created on the heap with default values: `rollno = 0` and `name = null`.
- The `new` operator returns the memory address of this new object, which is stored in the stack-local reference variable `s1`.
- The reference `s2` at this point remains uninitialised (effectively `null`).

**(ii) `s1.insertRecord(201, "Devika");` (2 marks)**
- A new stack frame is pushed for the `insertRecord` method.
- The implicit `this` reference inside the new frame is bound to the address of the `s1` heap object.
- The formal parameters `r` and `n` are local variables in this frame, holding `201` and `"Devika"` respectively.
- The statement `this.rollno = r;` writes the value `201` into the `rollno` field of the object pointed to by `this` (i.e., `s1`).
- The statement `this.name = n;` writes the reference to the String literal `"Devika"` into the `name` field of the same object.
- The stack frame is popped; the parameters and `this` are destroyed, but the heap object retains the updated values.

**(iii) `s1.displayInformation();` (2 marks)**
- A new stack frame is pushed for `displayInformation`, with `this` bound to `s1`.
- The body executes `System.out.println(rollno + " " + name)`. The expression `rollno` resolves via `this.rollno` to the value `201`, and `name` resolves to the String reference `"Devika"`.
- The `+` operator, when one operand is a String, performs string concatenation, producing `"201 Devika"`, which is sent to `System.out` and printed to the console followed by a newline.
- The stack frame is popped.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: What will happen if the `this` keyword is removed from `insertRecord` (i.e., if we write `rollno = r;` and `name = n;` instead of `this.rollno = r;` and `this.name = n;`)? Will the program still compile and produce the same output? Justify your answer with reference to Java's variable scoping rules. Also explain: what is the output if we accidentally swap the calls `s1.insertRecord(201, "Devika");` and `s2.insertRecord(202, "Rahul");`? Does the final output change?

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Removing `this` (4 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) **Yes, the program will still compile and produce the same output.** The `this` keyword is not mandatory in this case because the parameter names (`r` and `n`) are **lexically different** from the instance variable names (`rollno` and `name`). Java's scope resolution rule searches from the innermost scope outward: when `rollno` appears on the left side of the assignment, the compiler looks first inside the method body, finds no local variable named `rollno`, then looks at the enclosing class scope, and binds it to the instance field. Thus `rollno = r;` and `this.rollno = r;` are semantically equivalent in this exact program.
- (1.5 marks) **However, the KTU Board recommends using `this`** because if the parameter were renamed to `rollno` (i.e., `void insertRecord(int rollno, String name)`), then `rollno = rollno;` would be **self-assigning** — both `rollno`s would refer to the parameter, and the instance field would remain unchanged. Using `this.rollno = rollno;` would correctly resolve the ambiguity.
- (1 mark) **Best-practice verdict:** Always use `this` when assigning parameters to instance variables to make the intent explicit and to prevent scope-shadowing bugs. This is what the KTU model answer key expects.

**Part 2 — Swapping the calls (3 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) The lines `s1.insertRecord(201, "Devika");` and `s2.insertRecord(202, "Rahul");` are **independent method calls** on independent objects. The JVM executes them in the order they appear in `main`, but since each call mutates a different heap object, the order of these two calls does not affect the final state of either object.
- (1.5 marks) The final output **remains identical**:
```
201 Devika
202 Rahul
```
- (Bonus 0.5 mark, if mentioned) However, the lines `s1.displayInformation();` and `s2.displayInformation();` **do** depend on order — if swapped, the output would become `202 Rahul` followed by `201 Devika`. This subtle distinction is a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this experiment (Common Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **File-name mismatch** — Writing `public class student` (lowercase) in a file named `Student.java` causes a compilation error. Java is case-sensitive. **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting `String[] args`** — The main method signature must be exactly `public static void main(String[] args)`. Missing `String[]` causes `Main method not found in class Student`. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Declaring `rollno` and `name` inside `main`** — They are then local variables and cannot be accessed from `insertRecord` or `displayInformation`. The compiler gives *"cannot find symbol"*. **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Using `void insertRecord(int rollno, String name)` without `this`** — Causes the instance fields to remain at default values (`0` and `null`), and the program prints `0 null` twice. **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Misspelling `System.out.println`** — Case-sensitive. `system.out.println` or `System.Out.println` will not compile. **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Forgetting semicolons** — Java requires `;` at the end of every statement. The most common miss is at the end of `int rollno` or `String name`. **−1 mark per occurrence**.
> 7. **Saving the file with `.java.txt` extension** (Windows users) — The compiler will not find the file. **−1 mark**.
> 8. **Not showing terminal output in the lab record** — A lab record without the screenshot or handwritten copy of the output is treated as incomplete. **−1 mark**.
> 9. **Writing `displayinformation()` instead of `displayInformation()`** — Case-sensitive method names. **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorise Before Entering the Lab:**

- A **class** is a blueprint; an **object** is an instance of that blueprint, living on the heap.
- The file name **must** exactly match the `public class` name (case-sensitive), and the extension **must** be `.java`.
- Instance variables are declared **at class scope**, outside all methods, and receive **default values** automatically.
- Local variables (including method parameters) are declared **inside methods**, and have **no default value** — they must be initialised before use.
- The `new` keyword allocates heap memory and returns a reference; without `new`, the reference is `null` and dereferencing it causes a `NullPointerException`.
- The dot operator `.` is used to invoke a method on an object: `objectReference.methodName(arguments)`.
- The `this` keyword is an implicit reference to the current object, available in every instance method (but **not** in `static` methods).
- Methods that return nothing declare their return type as `void`.
- The main method signature is **exactly**: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- `System.out.println(...)` prints to the standard console, followed by a newline.
- Default values: `int` → `0`, `double` → `0.0`, `boolean` → `false`, object references → `null`.
- Each object has its **own copy** of the instance variables, but methods are **shared** (only one copy of bytecode exists in the method area).
- When `s1.insertRecord(...)` and `s2.insertRecord(...)` are called, the `this` pointer binds to `s1` and `s2` respectively, ensuring each call mutates the correct object.
- Memory layout: **references on the stack, objects on the heap, arrows connecting them.**
- Compilation: `javac FileName.java` produces `FileName.class`; Execution: `java FileName` invokes the JVM.
- The order of independent method calls on **different** objects does not affect the final state, but the order of `displayInformation()` calls **does** affect the printed output order.
- Java is **case-sensitive** for keywords, class names, method names, and file names.
- All statements end with `;`. The only exceptions are class, method, and block declarations, which end with `{` `}`.
- `this` is optional **only** when there is no name collision between the parameter and the instance variable; **best practice is to always use it**.
- A Java program can have **only one `public class`** per file, and the file name must match.
- The KTU Board expects the **expected output** to be written in the lab record, even if the program runs successfully on the examiner's machine.
- Common viva questions: *"Where is the object stored?"* → **Heap.** *"Where is the reference stored?"* → **Stack.** *"What is garbage collection?"* → **JVM reclaiming heap memory of unreferenced objects.** *"Why `static` for `main`?"* → **So that the JVM can call it without creating an object first.**

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Class = template, Object = product, `new` = factory, `this` = self-pointer, dot = call, stack = reference, heap = object, void = no return, `String[] args` = command-line parameters.* If you remember these ten mappings, this experiment is locked in.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to create a Student class with instance variables id and name, and initialize two student objects using a parameterized constructor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to create a Student class with instance variables id and name, and initialize two student objects using a parameterized constructor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) using Java, a **class** is a blueprint or template that defines the **state** (instance variables) and **behavior** (methods) common to all objects of that type. An **object** is a runtime instance of a class that occupies actual memory in the **heap area** of the JVM.

A **parameterized constructor** is a special member method of a class that:

- Bears the **exact same name** as the class.
- Has **no return type** (not even `void`).
- Accepts one or more **formal parameters** used to initialize the instance variables of the newly created object.
- Is invoked **implicitly** at the moment of object creation using the `new` keyword.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO1 – K2 Level):** This experiment maps to Course Outcome **CO1**: *"Apply object-oriented concepts such as classes, objects, constructors, and methods to develop elementary Java programs."* It is assessed at **Bloom's Level 2 (Understand)** and **Level 3 (Apply)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **class** as the *architectural blueprint* of a house. The blueprint itself is **not** a house — it merely describes what the house will look like (number of rooms, doors, color). Each **house built from that blueprint** is an **object**, and the **construction process** that customizes each house (giving it a specific owner and paint color) is the **constructor**.

For our experiment:

- **Blueprint (Class)**: `Student` — defines that every student *will have* an `id` and a `name`.
- **House 1 (Object s1)**: A real student with `id = 101` and `name = "Arun"`.
- **House 2 (Object s2)**: A real student with `id = 102` and `name = "Meera"`.
- **Construction Crew (Parameterized Constructor)**: The team that takes the values `101, "Arun"` and *plugs them into* the blueprint while building House 1.

### 1.3 Terminology Glossary (KTU Board-Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions for Exam Writing**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Instance Variable** | A non-static data member declared inside a class but outside any method. Each object gets its **own copy**. |
| **Local Variable** | A variable declared inside a method, constructor, or block. Scope is limited to that block. |
| **Constructor** | A special method-like block used to initialize a new object. Has the same name as the class and no return type. |
| **Parameterized Constructor** | A constructor that accepts arguments to initialize object state with caller-supplied values. |
| **Object** | A runtime entity that encapsulates state (fields) and behavior (methods) and resides in heap memory. |
| **`new` Keyword** | JVM operator that allocates heap memory for a new object and invokes the matching constructor. |
| **Reference Variable** | A variable (e.g., `s1`) that stores the *memory address* of an object, not the object itself. |

### 1.4 Physical / Language Constants Referenced

- **Java Class File Naming Rule**: The **public** class name **must match** the `.java` file name. If a file contains a public class `StudentDemo`, the file **must** be saved as `StudentDemo.java`.
- **JVM Memory Regions Used**: **Stack** (holds reference variables `s1`, `s2`) and **Heap** (holds the actual `Student` objects).
- **Default Constructor Rule**: If **no** constructor is explicitly written, Java provides a **no-argument default constructor**. As soon as **any** constructor is declared, this default is **removed automatically**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Parameterized Constructor Works

The execution of the statement:

```java
Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");
```

proceeds in **three atomic JVM steps**, in strict order:

1. **Declaration Phase** — The reference variable `s1` of type `Student` is created in the **stack frame** of `main()`. At this instant, `s1` is uninitialized and **cannot** be used.
2. **Memory Allocation Phase** — The `new` operator requests the JVM to allocate a contiguous block of **heap memory** large enough to hold all instance variables (`int id` → 4 bytes, `String name` → 1 reference pointer). Memory is initialized to default values: `id = 0`, `name = null`.
3. **Constructor Invocation Phase** — The `new` operator then passes the reference of the newly allocated heap object to the parameterized constructor `Student(int i, String n)`. The constructor body executes, assigns `id = 101` and `name = "Arun"`, and returns. The address of the initialized object is finally stored in `s1`.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why pass parameters?** Because two different students must hold two different `id` and `name` values. A no-argument constructor would force identical (or zero/default) data into every object, defeating the purpose of having multiple objects.
- **Why use `this` keyword (alternative style)?** Java allows `id = i;` (parameter name differs from field name) **or** `this.id = id;` (parameter shadows the field — `this` disambiguates). Both are **valid** and **commonly accepted** in KTU evaluations.
- **Why `void display()` method?** To demonstrate **encapsulation of behavior** alongside state. The object is responsible for *printing its own* data, mirroring real-world OOP design.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Syntax Table — Memorize Before Exam**

| Construct | Exact Syntax | Purpose / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Class declaration | `class ClassName { }` | Blueprint definition. |
| Instance variable | `accessModifier dataType varName;` | Per-object field stored on the heap. |
| Parameterized constructor | `ClassName(param1, param2) { /* body */ }` | Initializes fields with caller values. |
| Object creation | `ClassName refVar = new ClassName(arg1, arg2);` | Allocates heap memory + invokes constructor. |
| `this` keyword | `this.fieldName = parameterName;` | Resolves name conflict between parameter and field. |
| Method definition | `returnType methodName() { /* body */ }` | Defines object behavior. |
| Console output | `System.out.println("Text " + variable);` | Concatenates string and value using `+`. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The *parameterized-constructor + multiple-object* pattern is the foundation of nearly every enterprise Java system — from creating user records in a database-backed Spring Boot application, to instantiating sensor nodes in an IoT pipeline, to constructing transaction objects in a banking system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Program Construction & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Mandate Followed:** Every line of the program is derived step-by-step with explicit rationale. No placeholders, no skipped lines.

### 3.1 Program Objective (Verbatim from KTU Record)

Create a Java program containing a class named `Student` with two **instance variables** `id` (int) and `name` (String). Provide a **parameterized constructor** to initialize both fields. In the `main` method (placed in a separate public class), create **two `Student` objects** with distinct values and display their details.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Program Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the class skeleton.**
We start with the smallest valid Java class. The class must be named `Student` to match the experimental specification.

```java
class Student {

}
```

*Rationale:* A class is the container for both state (variables) and behavior (methods). Without a class, no object can be created.

**Step 2 — Declare the two instance variables inside the class.**
These are placed *outside* any method so that every object of `Student` owns its own copy.

```java
class Student {
    int id;
    String name;
}
```

*Rationale:* `id` is an integer roll number; `name` is a textual identifier. Both are non-static, so they live in heap memory per object. Default values are `0` and `null` respectively.

**Step 3 — Add the parameterized constructor.**
The constructor's name **must** be identical to the class name (`Student`). It accepts two parameters and assigns them to the instance variables. We deliberately use parameter names `i` and `n` to avoid name collision with the fields `id` and `name`.

```java
class Student {
    int id;
    String name;

    Student(int i, String n) {
        id = i;
        name = n;
    }
}
```

*Rationale:* The assignment `id = i` reads as "set this object's `id` field equal to the value passed in parameter `i`". If we had named the parameter `id`, we would have needed the `this` keyword: `this.id = id;`. Both forms are **equally correct** in KTU 2024 evaluations.

**Step 4 — Add a `display()` method to print object data.**
This demonstrates that the object not only *stores* state but also *acts on* it.

```java
class Student {
    int id;
    String name;

    Student(int i, String n) {
        id = i;
        name = n;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Student ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }
}
```

*Rationale:* Using `+` with a String performs concatenation in Java, converting `id` (int) to its textual form automatically.

**Step 5 — Create a separate public class `StudentDemo` containing `main()`.**
A Java program needs exactly **one** `public` class with a `main` method as its entry point. Since `main` cannot live inside `Student` if we want to keep `Student` reusable, we place it in a sibling public class.

```java
class Student {
    int id;
    String name;

    Student(int i, String n) {
        id = i;
        name = n;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Student ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }
}

public class StudentDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");
        Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera");
        s1.display();
        s2.display();
    }
}
```

*Rationale:* The file **must** be saved as `StudentDemo.java` because the public class is `StudentDemo`. Each `new Student(...)` call allocates a **new** heap object and runs the parameterized constructor.

### 3.3 Final Complete Program (Lab-Ready Version)

```java
// File: StudentDemo.java
class Student {
    int id;
    String name;

    // Parameterized constructor
    Student(int i, String n) {
        id = i;
        name = n;
    }

    // Method to display student details
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Student ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }
}

public class StudentDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Creating two Student objects using parameterized constructor
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");
        Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera");

        // Displaying details of both students
        s1.display();
        s2.display();
    }
}
```

### 3.4 Expected Output

```text
Student ID: 101, Name: Arun
Student ID: 102, Name: Meera
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Trace of Execution

| Step | Statement | Stack State | Heap Object `s1` | Heap Object `s2` |
|------|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arun");` — `new` invoked | `s1` declared (uninitialized) | Memory allocated; `id=0`, `name=null` | — |
| 2 | Constructor body executes | — | `id=101`, `name="Arun"` | — |
| 3 | Address returned to `s1` | `s1` → 0xA1 | `id=101`, `name="Arun"` | — |
| 4 | `Student s2 = new Student(102, "Meera");` | `s2` declared | `id=101`, `name="Arun"` | Memory allocated; `id=0`, `name=null` |
| 5 | Constructor body executes for `s2` | `s1` → 0xA1 | `id=101`, `name="Arun"` | `id=102`, `name="Meera"` |
| 6 | `s1.display();` | `s1` → 0xA1 | Prints: `ID: 101, Name: Arun` | — |
| 7 | `s2.display();` | `s2` → 0xB2 | — | Prints: `ID: 102, Name: Meera` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        +int id
        +String name
        +Student(int i, String n)
        +void display()
    }

    class StudentDemo {
        +main(String[] args)$ void$
    }

    StudentDemo ..> Student : creates instances of
```

*Interpretation:* The diagram shows the `Student` class with its two instance fields, the parameterized constructor, and the `display()` method. The `StudentDemo` class depends on `Student` (dashed arrow) because it instantiates two objects of that type inside `main`.

### 4.2 Mermaid Object-Creation Flow (JVM Execution Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[main method begins] --> B[Declare reference: Student s1]
    B --> C[Execute new Student 101 Arun]
    C --> D[Allocate heap memory for new object]
    D --> E[Default init: id=0, name=null]
    E --> F[Invoke parameterized constructor]
    F --> G[Assign id=101, name equals Arun]
    G --> H[Return heap address to s1]
    H --> I[Declare reference: Student s2]
    I --> J[Execute new Student 102 Meera]
    J --> K[Allocate separate heap block]
    K --> L[Default init: id=0, name=null]
    L --> M[Invoke parameterized constructor for s2]
    M --> N[Assign id=102, name equals Meera]
    N --> O[Return heap address to s2]
    O --> P[Call s1 dot display]
    O --> Q[Call s2 dot display]
    P --> R[Print line 1]
    Q --> S[Print line 2]
    R --> T[main method ends]
    S --> T
```

*Interpretation:* This flowchart traces the exact order in which the JVM performs allocation, initialization, and method invocation. Note that `s1` and `s2` receive **two independent heap blocks** — modifying one never affects the other.

### 4.3 Mermaid Memory Layout Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STACK["STACK MEMORY - main method frame"]
        S1["s1 --> 0xA1"]
        S2["s2 --> 0xB2"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP1["HEAP - Object 1 at 0xA1"]
        F1["id = 101"]
        F2["name = Arun"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP2["HEAP - Object 2 at 0xB2"]
        F3["id = 102"]
        F4["name = Meera"]
    end

    S1 -.points to.-> HEAP1
    S2 -.points to.-> HEAP2
```

*Interpretation:* The stack contains only the **reference variables** `s1` and `s2` (which hold memory addresses). The actual `Student` data — `id` and `name` — lives in two **separate** heap blocks. This separation is what enables true object independence in Java.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a default constructor and a parameterized constructor in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Default Constructor:** A constructor that takes **no arguments**. If the programmer does not define any constructor, Java automatically provides a no-argument constructor that initializes fields to their default values.
- **Parameterized Constructor:** A constructor that accepts **one or more arguments**, used to initialize an object with caller-supplied values at the time of creation.
- **Example — Default:** `Student() { id = 0; name = "Unknown"; }`
- **Example — Parameterized:** `Student(int i, String n) { id = i; name = n; }`
- **Key Difference:** A default constructor produces objects with **identical** initial state, whereas a parameterized constructor allows each object to be created with **distinct** data in a single line of code. *(1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for each example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2. What is the role of the `new` keyword in Java? Explain with respect to object creation and constructor invocation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `new` keyword performs **three sequential operations** during object creation:

1. **Heap Allocation:** It requests the JVM to allocate a contiguous block of heap memory large enough to hold all instance variables of the class.
2. **Default Initialization:** All fields are initialized to their default values (numeric types → `0`, objects → `null`, booleans → `false`).
3. **Constructor Invocation:** It then invokes the matching constructor (here, the parameterized `Student(int, String)`) and passes the heap reference into it. The constructor's assignments overwrite the default values.

After these three steps, the `new` expression **returns the memory address** of the initialized object, which is then stored in the reference variable on the left-hand side. *(1 mark per step.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **class**, an **object**, and an **instance variable** in Java. How are they related? Illustrate with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program to define a class `Employee` with instance variables `empId` (int) and `empName` (String). Provide a **parameterized constructor** to initialize both fields, a method `show()` to display the values, and create **three** `Employee` objects in `main` with different data. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **characteristics and types of constructors** in Java. Explain the rules governing constructor overloading with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program with a class `Book` having instance variables `title` (String) and `price` (double). Use a **parameterized constructor** to assign values, a `display()` method to print details, and demonstrate creation of two `Book` objects in `main`. Show the **expected output** as well. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

- A **class** is a user-defined blueprint or prototype from which objects are created. It declares the **attributes** (instance variables) and **behaviors** (methods) common to all objects of its type. *(1 mark)*
- An **object** is a runtime instance of a class. It is created using the `new` keyword, resides in heap memory, and contains its own copy of every instance variable. *(1 mark)*
- An **instance variable** is a non-static data member declared inside a class but outside any method. It belongs to the object, not to the class, and is initialized to a default value if not explicitly set. *(1 mark)*
- **Relationship:** The class acts as a *template*; the object is a *concrete realization* of that template; the instance variables are the *data slots* allocated within each object. *(1 mark)*
- **Diagram (text-based for KTU records):** *(3 marks)*

```text
        Class: Student              Objects (Heap)
        ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
        │ int id          │   -->   │ id    = 101     │
        │ String name     │         │ name  = "Arun"  │
        │ display()       │         └─────────────────┘
        └─────────────────┘         ┌─────────────────┐
                                     │ id    = 102     │
                                     │ name  = "Meera" │
                                     └─────────────────┘
```

**Part (b) — Program (7 marks):**

```java
// File: EmployeeDemo.java
class Employee {
    int empId;
    String empName;

    Employee(int i, String n) {
        empId = i;
        empName = n;
    }

    void show() {
        System.out.println("Employee ID: " + empId + ", Name: " + empName);
    }
}

public class EmployeeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee(201, "Rahul");
        Employee e2 = new Employee(202, "Priya");
        Employee e3 = new Employee(203, "Kiran");

        e1.show();
        e2.show();
        e3.show();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```text
Employee ID: 201, Name: Rahul
Employee ID: 202, Name: Priya
Employee ID: 203, Name: Kiran
```

*Mark Distribution: Class + variables + constructor (3 marks), show() method (1 mark), main with 3 objects (2 marks), correct output (1 mark).*

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

Java constructors possess the following **characteristics**: *(3 marks)*

1. Same name as the class.
2. No return type (not even `void`).
3. Invoked automatically when an object is created via `new`.
4. Cannot be `abstract`, `static`, `final`, or `synchronized`.

**Types of constructors:** *(2 marks)*

- **Default (No-arg) constructor:** Takes no parameters.
- **Parameterized constructor:** Takes one or more parameters.
- **Copy constructor** (user-defined, not Java-built-in): Initializes an object using another object of the same class.

**Constructor Overloading Rules:** *(2 marks)*

- A class can have **multiple constructors** provided their **parameter lists differ** in number, type, or order.
- The compiler differentiates them by **signature**, not by name (all share the class name).
- At object creation, the compiler matches the argument list to the correct constructor.
- **Example:**

```java
class Box {
    int side;
    Box() { side = 1; }                          // No-arg
    Box(int s) { side = s; }                     // Parameterized
    Box(Box b) { side = b.side; }                // Copy
}
```

**Part (b) — Program (7 marks):**

```java
// File: BookDemo.java
class Book {
    String title;
    double price;

    Book(String t, double p) {
        title = t;
        price = p;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Title: " + title + ", Price: Rs. " + price);
    }
}

public class BookDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Book b1 = new Book("Java Programming", 499.50);
        Book b2 = new Book("Data Structures", 550.00);

        b1.display();
        b2.display();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```text
Title: Java Programming, Price: Rs. 499.5
Title: Data Structures, Price: Rs. 550.0
```

*Mark Distribution: Class + fields (2 marks), constructor (2 marks), display (1 mark), main + 2 objects (1 mark), output (1 mark).*

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Wrong file name:** Saving the file as `student.java` (lowercase) when the public class is `StudentDemo` causes a **compilation error** and **0 marks** for output. Always match the public class name exactly.
> 2. **Including a return type in the constructor:** Writing `void Student(int i, String n)` makes it a **regular method**, not a constructor. The compiler will not call it during `new Student(...)`. **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the `new` keyword:** Writing `Student s1 = Student(101, "Arun");` is a **syntax error** in Java. Always use `new` for heap allocation. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Printing instance variables directly from `main`** without a display method: This works but is **considered poor OOP design** by KTU examiners. Always encapsulate printing inside the class. **−0.5 to −1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing local and instance variables:** Declaring `int id` inside the constructor shadows the field. The assignment then modifies only the local copy. Always use distinct names **or** the `this` keyword.
> 6. **Missing the public class:** If `main` is inside the `Student` class itself (which is allowed), remember to add `public static` and accept `String[] args` exactly. Any deviation causes a runtime entry-point error.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master Before Exam**
>
> - A **class** is a blueprint; an **object** is its real memory-resident instance. *(L1)*
> - **Instance variables** are declared *inside the class* but *outside any method*. Each object has its own copy. *(L2)*
> - A **constructor** has the same name as the class, has **no return type**, and is called **automatically** by `new`. *(L1)*
> - A **parameterized constructor** receives arguments and uses them to initialize instance variables of the new object. *(L2)*
> - The `new` keyword does **three things**: (1) allocates heap memory, (2) default-initializes fields, (3) invokes the matching constructor. *(L3)*
> - The **filename must match** the public class name, including capitalization. *(L1)*
> - If **any** constructor is defined, Java **stops** providing the default no-arg constructor automatically. *(L3 — often tested as a trick question.)*
> - The **`this` keyword** resolves the ambiguity when a constructor parameter has the same name as an instance variable. *(L3)*
> - **Reference variables** live on the **stack**; **objects** live on the **heap**. The reference holds only the memory address. *(L2)*
> - Two objects created from the same class are **independent** — changing one does **not** affect the other. *(L2)*
> - A constructor **cannot** be `abstract`, `static`, `final`, or `synchronized`. *(L1)*
> - A class can have **multiple constructors** (overloading) provided their **parameter signatures differ**. *(L3)*
> - The `main` method signature is **fixed**: `public static void main(String[] args)`. *(L1)*
> - `System.out.println` with `+` operator **concatenates** strings and **auto-converts** primitive values to text. *(L2)*
> - **Encapsulation principle:** Let the object *display its own data* via a method like `display()` or `show()` instead of accessing fields from `main` directly. *(L3 — fetches extra appreciation from KTU examiners.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to calculate the area of circle, rectangle, and triangle using the concept of method overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to calculate the area of circle, rectangle, and triangle using the concept of method overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Method Overloading** (also called *Compile-time Polymorphism* or *Static Binding*) is a core feature of Java (and OOP) that allows a class to declare **multiple methods with the same name** within the same class, provided their **method signatures** differ. The method signature consists of the **method name** combined with the **parameter list (number, type, or order of parameters)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Method overloading is the mechanism of defining two or more methods within the same class that share the same name but differ in the number, type, or order of their parameters. The compiler differentiates these methods at compile time using the JVM's Method Resolution Mechanism, making it an example of static polymorphism.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Method Overloading (Rule of Difference)

For two methods to be considered *overloaded*, they MUST differ in at least ONE of the following:

| Sl. No. | Differentiating Factor | Example |
|:-:|:-|:-:|
| **1** | **Number of parameters** | `area(int r)` vs `area(int l, int w)` |
| **2** | **Data type of parameters** | `area(int a, int b)` vs `area(double a, double b)` |
| **3** | **Order of parameters** | `area(int a, double b)` vs `area(double a, int b)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Restriction:** Changing ONLY the return type of methods is **NOT** considered valid method overloading. For example, `int area(int r)` and `double area(int r)` together in the same class will cause a **compilation error**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "ATM Machine" Intuition

Imagine you walk up to an **ATM machine**. The action you want to perform is **"Withdraw Money"** — this is the **method name**.

However, the ATM accepts *different ways* to perform this action:
- Insert card + Enter amount → withdraws from your **savings account**
- Insert card + Enter amount + Enter loan ID → withdraws from a **loan account**
- Insert card + Enter amount + Enter beneficiary details → withdraws as a **transfer**

The label on the button is the **same** ("Withdraw"), but the **inputs you provide** (number/type of parameters) determine which internal logic runs.

> **Mapping this back to Java:**
> The button label = `area(...)` method name
> The inputs you provide = Parameters (radius, length, width, base, height)
> The internal logic = The body of the specific `area()` method chosen

This is exactly what **method overloading** does in Java — the **same method name** performs **different computational tasks** based on the **arguments passed**.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Use-Case

In a **CAD (Computer-Aided Design) software** like AutoCAD, the developer writes **one method called `drawShape()`** that can draw a circle, rectangle, or triangle. The user simply calls `drawShape()` with the appropriate geometric inputs, and the compiler routes the call to the correct implementation. This makes the API **cleaner, more readable, and intuitive**.

---

## 1.5 Why This Experiment is in the KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment satisfies **Course Outcome CO2** of PCEOL408: *"Implement object-oriented programming features such as inheritance, polymorphism, interfaces, and packages in Java."* Specifically, it demonstrates **Polymorphism at compile-time**, which is a foundational building block for understanding overriding (Module 3) and advanced OOP design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Method Resolution Mechanism (How Compiler Decides)

When you call an overloaded method, the **Java compiler** uses a strict, deterministic procedure called **Method Resolution**. Understanding this is **critical** for KTU lab viva questions.

**Step-by-step resolution:**

1. **Phase 1 — Exact Match:** The compiler searches for an overloaded method whose parameter types match **exactly** with the arguments passed.

2. **Phase 2 — Widening Primitive Conversion:** If no exact match is found, the compiler applies *type promotion* in this fixed order:
   - `byte` → `short` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double`

3. **Phase 3 — Autoboxing / Unboxing:** The compiler tries to convert between primitives and their wrapper classes (e.g., `int` ↔ `Integer`).

4. **Phase 4 — Varargs Match:** As a last resort, the compiler matches against variable-argument (`...`) methods.

> [!WARNING]
> **Phase 2 Trap (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva):** If you call `area('5')` (a `char`), it will match `area(int)` and NOT `area(double)` directly, because `char → int` (widening) takes precedence over `char → double`. This is the **"most specific method"** rule.

---

## 2.2 Geometric Area Formulas Used in the Program

> [!NOTE]
> The following three formulas must be **memorized** for the lab record and viva. They are the *core computational logic* of this experiment.

### Formula Sheet

| Shape | Geometric Inputs | Area Formula (LaTeX) | Unit of Area (SI) |
|:-|:-|:-:|:-:|
| **Circle** | radius $r$ | $A_{\text{circle}} = \pi \cdot r^{2}$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ |
| **Rectangle** | length $l$, width $w$ | $A_{\text{rect}} = l \cdot w$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ |
| **Triangle** | base $b$, height $h$ | $A_{\text{tri}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot h$ | $\text{m}^{2}$ |

**Where:**
- $\pi$ (pi) is a **mathematical constant** approximately equal to **3.141592653589793**
- In Java, we access the highly precise value of $\pi$ via the built-in constant **`Math.PI`** (uppercase PI, no parentheses — it is a `static final` field, not a method).

---

## 2.3 Why `Math.PI` Instead of `3.14`?

| Property | Hardcoded `3.14` | `Math.PI` |
|:-|:-:|:-:|
| Precision | 3 significant digits | ~15 significant digits |
| Readability | Magic number (poor practice) | Self-documenting |
| KTU Recommendation | ❌ Not preferred | ✅ **Always preferred** |

> **Engineering Utility:** In civil/structural engineering software (e.g., calculating the cross-sectional area of a cylindrical pillar), even a 0.001 error in $\pi$ accumulates over thousands of calculations. Hence, KTU evaluators expect `Math.PI`.

---

## 2.4 Signature Design Strategy for This Experiment

To overload `area()` for three shapes, we have multiple valid KTU-accepted strategies:

| Strategy | Method 1 (Circle) | Method 2 (Rectangle) | Method 3 (Triangle) | Differentiator |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **A — Count-based** | `area(double r)` | `area(double l, double w)` | `area(double b, double h, int dummy)` | Number of params |
| **B — Type-based** | `area(int r)` | `area(int l, int w)` | `area(double b, double h)` | Data type of params |
| **C — Mixed** | `area(int r)` | `area(int l, int w)` | `area(int b, int h, boolean isTriangle)` | Mixed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** Strategy **B** (Type-based) is **most commonly evaluated** in KTU lab records because it elegantly demonstrates the *"type-based overloading"* rule. We will use **Strategy B** in our program.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Java Program & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete, KTU-Evaluator-Approved Java Source Code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The program below is **fully compilable** on JDK 8+ and follows **KTU lab record conventions** (class name in PascalCase, proper indentation, Scanner usage, and comments).

```java
// File: AreaCalculator.java
// Experiment 2: Method Overloading for Area Calculation
// KTU 2024 Scheme - PCEOL408

import java.util.Scanner;   // Importing Scanner class for user input

// Class containing overloaded area() methods
class AreaCalculator {

    // Overloaded Method 1: Area of Circle (Takes 1 'int' parameter)
    double area(int radius) {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }

    // Overloaded Method 2: Area of Rectangle (Takes 2 'int' parameters)
    double area(int length, int width) {
        return length * width;
    }

    // Overloaded Method 3: Area of Triangle (Takes 2 'double' parameters)
    // NOTE: Differentiated by DATA TYPE (double vs int) from Method 2
    double area(double base, double height) {
        return 0.5 * base * height;
    }
}

// Main class to demonstrate method overloading
public class ShapeAreaDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // Create Scanner object to read user input
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Create object of AreaCalculator class
        AreaCalculator obj = new AreaCalculator();

        // -------- 1. Circle --------
        System.out.print("Enter radius of circle: ");
        int r = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Area of Circle = " + obj.area(r));

        // -------- 2. Rectangle --------
        System.out.print("Enter length and width of rectangle: ");
        int l = sc.nextInt();
        int w = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Area of Rectangle = " + obj.area(l, w));

        // -------- 3. Triangle --------
        System.out.print("Enter base and height of triangle: ");
        double b = sc.nextDouble();
        double h = sc.nextDouble();
        System.out.println("Area of Triangle = " + obj.area(b, h));

        // Close the Scanner
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Line-by-Line Analytical Walkthrough

| Line(s) | Code Segment | Explanation | KTU Concept Tested |
|:-:|:-|:-:|:-:|
| 5 | `import java.util.Scanner;` | Imports `Scanner` for runtime input | Java Packages |
| 8 | `class AreaCalculator` | Helper class containing overloaded methods | Class Definition |
| 11 | `double area(int radius)` | **1st** overload — circle | Number of params = 1 |
| 12 | `Math.PI * radius * radius` | Computes $\pi \cdot r^2$ using built-in constant | `Math` class |
| 16 | `double area(int length, int width)` | **2nd** overload — rectangle | Number of params = 2 (int) |
| 21 | `double area(double base, double height)` | **3rd** overload — triangle | Same param count as rect, but **different type** |
| 22 | `0.5 * base * height` | Computes $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot h$ | Arithmetic Expression |
| 32 | `AreaCalculator obj = new AreaCalculator();` | Object instantiation | Class & Object |
| 36 | `obj.area(r)` | Compiler routes to **Method 1** (1 int param) | Compile-time Binding |
| 41 | `obj.area(l, w)` | Compiler routes to **Method 2** (2 int params) | Compile-time Binding |
| 47 | `obj.area(b, h)` | Compiler routes to **Method 3** (2 double params) | Compile-time Binding |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Sample Execution

**Input given by user:**
- Circle radius $r = 7$
- Rectangle length $l = 10$, width $w = 5$
- Triangle base $b = 8.0$, height $h = 6.0$

**Step 1: Circle area computation**

$$A_{\text{circle}} = \pi \cdot r^{2} = \pi \cdot 7^{2} = 49\pi \approx 153.938 \, \text{m}^{2}$$

The Java statement `Math.PI * 7 * 7` evaluates as: `3.141592653589793 * 49 = 153.93804...`

**Step 2: Rectangle area computation**

$$A_{\text{rect}} = l \cdot w = 10 \cdot 5 = 50 \, \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Triangle area computation**

$$A_{\text{tri}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 8.0 \cdot 6.0 = 24.0 \, \text{m}^{2}$$

**Expected Console Output:**

```
Enter radius of circle: 7
Area of Circle = 153.93804002589985
Enter length and width of rectangle: 10 5
Area of Rectangle = 50.0
Enter base and height of triangle: 8.0 6.0
Area of Triangle = 24.0
```

---

## 3.4 Common Compilation Errors to Pre-empt

| Error Message | Cause | Fix |
|:-|:-|:-|
| `method area(int) is already defined` | Duplicate signature (same name + same params) | Change number/type of parameters |
| `cannot resolve method area(int, int, int)` | No matching overload found | Verify parameter types |
| `incompatible types: possible lossy conversion` | Assigning `double` to `int` | Use explicit cast or change variable type |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT** declare two methods like `int area(int r)` and `double area(int r)` together. The compiler treats only the **parameter list** as the signature, not the return type. This is a **favourite viva trick question** at KTU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Method Overloading — Compile-Time Resolution Flow

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates how the Java compiler resolves which `area()` method to invoke during the execution of `obj.area(...)`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Program begins in main] --> B[Create AreaCalculator object obj]
    B --> C{Read user input via Scanner}
    C --> D1[Input: radius of type int]
    C --> D2[Input: length, width of type int]
    C --> D3[Input: base, height of type double]

    D1 --> E1[Compiler checks signatures]
    E1 --> F1{Match found?}
    F1 -->|Yes, 1 int param| G1[Call area int radius]
    G1 --> H1[Compute: PI x radius x radius]
    H1 --> I1[Return area of circle]

    D2 --> E2[Compiler checks signatures]
    E2 --> F2{Match found?}
    F2 -->|Yes, 2 int params| G2[Call area int length, int width]
    G2 --> H2[Compute: length x width]
    H2 --> I2[Return area of rectangle]

    D3 --> E3[Compiler checks signatures]
    E3 --> F3{Match found?}
    F3 -->|Yes, 2 double params| G3[Call area double base, double height]
    G3 --> H3[Compute: 0.5 x base x height]
    H3 --> I3[Return area of triangle]

    I1 --> J[Display all three results]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[End of program]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Program

This block diagram maps the **interactions between classes, methods, and the compiler's resolution engine**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MainClass[ShapeAreaDemo - main method]
        M1[Scanner Input]
        M2[Object Creation: obj]
        M3[Display Output]
    end

    subgraph HelperClass[AreaCalculator - Overloaded Methods]
        O1[Method 1: area int r]
        O2[Method 2: area int l, int w]
        O3[Method 3: area double b, double h]
    end

    subgraph CompilerEngine[Java Compiler - Method Resolution]
        R1[Phase 1: Exact Match]
        R2[Phase 2: Type Widening]
        R3[Phase 3: Autoboxing]
    end

    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> CompilerEngine
    CompilerEngine -->|Routes to| O1
    CompilerEngine -->|Routes to| O2
    CompilerEngine -->|Routes to| O3
    O1 --> M3
    O2 --> M3
    O3 --> M3

    CompilerEngine --> R1
    CompilerEngine --> R2
    CompilerEngine --> R3
```

---

## 4.3 Overloading Differentiation Table (Visual Summary)

| Shape | Method Signature | Parameter Count | Parameter Type(s) | Differentiator |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Circle | `area(int r)` | 1 | `int` | — |
| Rectangle | `area(int l, int w)` | 2 | `int, int` | **Count** ↑ |
| Triangle | `area(double b, double h)` | 2 | `double, double` | **Type** ↔ |

> **Read this row-by-row:** From Circle → Rectangle, the count changes. From Rectangle → Triangle, the count stays at 2 but the **type** changes from `int` to `double`. Hence, the compiler can unambiguously resolve all three calls — this is the *essence* of method overloading.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define method overloading. List the three ways in which two methods can be overloaded in Java.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Method overloading is a feature in Java that allows a class to define **multiple methods with the same name** but **different parameter lists** within the same class. It is an example of **compile-time polymorphism** (static binding).

**Three ways to overload (2 Marks):**

1. **Changing the number of parameters** — e.g., `area(int r)` vs `area(int l, int w)`
2. **Changing the data type of parameters** — e.g., `area(int a, int b)` vs `area(double a, double b)`
3. **Changing the order of parameters** — e.g., `area(int a, double b)` vs `area(double a, int b)`

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is changing only the return type NOT considered valid method overloading in Java? Give an example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Reason (2 Marks):**
The Java compiler uses the **method signature** (method name + parameter list) to differentiate between overloaded methods. The **return type is NOT part of the method signature**. Therefore, if two methods have the same name and same parameter list but differ only in return type, the compiler cannot decide which method the programmer intends to call, leading to an **ambiguity error**.

**Example (1 Mark):**

```java
class Demo {
    int  compute(int x) { return x; }        // Line A
    double compute(int x) { return x * 1.0; } // Line B → COMPILATION ERROR
}
```

The above code **will not compile** and produces: *"method compute(int) is already defined in class Demo"*.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain the concept of **compile-time polymorphism** in Java. How does the Java compiler resolve calls to overloaded methods? Illustrate with a suitable example.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Write a complete Java program to calculate the **area of a square, a cube's surface, and a cylinder's curved surface** using the concept of method overloading. Use the formulas:
- Square: $A = s^{2}$
- Cube Surface: $A = 6 s^{2}$
- Cylinder Curved Surface: $A = 2 \pi r h$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Compile-Time Polymorphism Explanation

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Compile-time polymorphism, also known as **static binding** or **early binding**, is the type of polymorphism where the compiler determines which method to invoke **at compile time** based on the method signature. Method overloading is the most common example.

**Resolution Steps (3 Marks):**
The Java compiler follows a 4-phase method resolution mechanism:

| Phase | What the compiler does |
|:-:|:-|
| **1** | Searches for an **exact match** of parameter types |
| **2** | Applies **widening primitive conversion** (e.g., `int` → `long` → `double`) |
| **3** | Performs **autoboxing/unboxing** (e.g., `int` ↔ `Integer`) |
| **4** | Matches against **varargs** methods as the last resort |

**Illustration (2 Marks):**

```java
class PolyDemo {
    void show(int a)    { System.out.println("int: " + a); }
    void show(double a) { System.out.println("double: " + a); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PolyDemo().show(10);   // Exact match → calls show(int)
        new PolyDemo().show(10.5); // Exact match → calls show(double)
    }
}
```

### Part (b) — Java Program for Areas

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

class SurfaceCalculator {

    // 1. Area of Square: A = s^2
    double area(int side) {
        return side * side;
    }

    // 2. Surface area of Cube: A = 6 * s^2
    double area(int side, int dummy) {  // dummy differentiates from Method 1
        return 6 * side * side;
    }

    // 3. Curved surface area of Cylinder: A = 2 * PI * r * h
    double area(double radius, double height) {  // type-differentiated
        return 2 * Math.PI * radius * height;
    }
}

public class SurfaceAreaDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        SurfaceCalculator obj = new SurfaceCalculator();

        System.out.print("Enter side of square (and cube): ");
        int s = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Area of Square   = " + obj.area(s));
        System.out.println("Surface of Cube  = " + obj.area(s, 0));

        System.out.print("Enter radius and height of cylinder: ");
        double r = sc.nextDouble();
        double h = sc.nextDouble();
        System.out.println("CSA of Cylinder  = " + obj.area(r, h));

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Output Verification:**

$$A_{\text{square}} = 5^{2} = 25 \, \text{m}^{2}$$
$$A_{\text{cube}} = 6 \cdot 5^{2} = 150 \, \text{m}^{2}$$
$$A_{\text{cyl}} = 2 \pi \cdot 3 \cdot 10 = 60\pi \approx 188.496 \, \text{m}^{2}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Differentiate between **method overloading** and **method overriding** in Java. Mention at least **five** points of distinction.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Write a Java program with a class `Calculator` that has overloaded methods named `compute()` to perform:
1. Addition of two integers
2. Multiplication of three doubles
3. Power calculation (base raised to exponent, both integers)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Method Overloading vs Method Overriding

| Sl. No. | Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | **Definition** | Same method name, **different parameters** in **same class** | Same method name, **same parameters** in **parent and child class** |
| 2 | **Polymorphism type** | **Compile-time** (static binding) | **Run-time** (dynamic binding) |
| 3 | **Class requirement** | Occurs **within a single class** | Requires **inheritance** (two classes) |
| 4 | **Method signature** | Must differ | Must be **identical** |
| 5 | **Return type** | Can be same or different | Must be **same** (or covariant) |
| 6 | **Access modifier** | Can be anything | Cannot be more restrictive than parent |
| 7 | **Performance** | Faster (resolved at compile time) | Slightly slower (resolved at runtime) |

### Part (b) — Calculator Program

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

class Calculator {

    // 1. Addition of two integers
    int compute(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // 2. Multiplication of three doubles
    double compute(double a, double b, double c) {
        return a * b * c;
    }

    // 3. Power calculation (base ^ exponent)
    double compute(int base, int exponent, char mode) {  // 'mode' differentiates
        return Math.pow(base, exponent);
    }
}

public class CalculatorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        Calculator calc = new Calculator();

        // Addition
        System.out.print("Enter two integers to add: ");
        int x = sc.nextInt();
        int y = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Sum = " + calc.compute(x, y));

        // Multiplication
        System.out.print("Enter three doubles to multiply: ");
        double d1 = sc.nextDouble();
        double d2 = sc.nextDouble();
        double d3 = sc.nextDouble();
        System.out.println("Product = " + calc.compute(d1, d2, d3));

        // Power
        System.out.print("Enter base and exponent: ");
        int b = sc.nextInt();
        int e = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Power = " + calc.compute(b, e, 'p'));

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Trace:**

| Operation | Input | Internal Call | Output |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Addition | `4 7` | `compute(int, int)` | `Sum = 11` |
| Multiplication | `2.0 3.0 4.0` | `compute(double, double, double)` | `Product = 24.0` |
| Power | `2 5` | `compute(int, int, char)` | `Power = 32.0` |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks (Lab Exam & Viva):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `Math.PI` and hardcoding `3.14`** → Lose 0.5–1 mark; KTU expects the constant.
> 2. **Declaring two methods with same name AND same parameters** → Compilation error; full program fails.
> 3. **Trying to differentiate methods using ONLY return type** → Compilation error. This is the **#1 viva trap**.
> 4. **Not closing the `Scanner` object** → Loses 0.5 mark in code-quality assessment.
> 5. **Not providing comments** → Lab record explicitly requires comments; without them, expect 1–2 mark deduction.
> 6. **Confusing Method Overloading with Method Overriding** → Conceptually different. Overloading is in **one class**; Overriding is across **parent-child** classes.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist right before entering the lab exam hall:**

- [x] **Method overloading** = same name, different parameter list (count / type / order)
- [x] It is an example of **compile-time polymorphism** (static binding)
- [x] **Return type alone CANNOT** differentiate overloaded methods
- [x] Use `Math.PI` (≈ 3.141592653589793) — never hardcode `3.14`
- [x] Area of circle: $A = \pi r^{2}$
- [x] Area of rectangle: $A = l \cdot w$
- [x] Area of triangle: $A = \tfrac{1}{2} b \cdot h$
- [x] Method signature = **method name + parameter types** (return type excluded)
- [x] The compiler resolves overloaded calls at **compile time**, not runtime
- [x] Type promotion order: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`
- [x] Method overloading **does NOT require inheritance** — it occurs within a single class
- [x] Program must have proper `import`, `Scanner` input handling, and `sc.close()` to satisfy KTU quality standards
- [x] Two methods differing only by `static` keyword are **NOT** considered overloaded
- [x] Constructor overloading follows the **same rules** as method overloading
- [x] **Always include comments** in the lab record — KTU evaluators check for documentation
