> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU lab viva questions often test the **difference between overloading and overriding** — memorize the 5–7 row table given in Part B (Question A) above. This is a **guaranteed question** across all KTU 2024 lab exam cycles.

Write two Java classes Employee and Engineer where Engineer inherits from Employee, with display() and calcSalary() methods demonstrating single object instantiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write two Java classes Employee and Engineer where Engineer inherits from Employee, with display() and calcSalary() methods demonstrating single object instantiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inheritance** is a fundamental pillar of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in which a new class (called the *subclass*, *derived class*, or *child class*) is created from an existing class (called the *superclass*, *base class*, or *parent class*), thereby acquiring — and optionally specializing — its members. In the Java language, this relationship is declared using the **`extends`** keyword, which enforces **single class inheritance** at any given depth of the hierarchy (a class may directly extend only one parent, although it may implement multiple interfaces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *"Inheritance is the mechanism by which one class (subclass) is created from another class (superclass), inheriting its attributes and methods, enabling code reuse, extensibility, and runtime polymorphism."*

### 1.2 Concept of "Single Object Instantiation"

**Single object instantiation** refers to the practice of creating **only one object** in memory using the `new` keyword — and yet, through inheritance, accessing the data members and methods of **both** the child and the parent class through that single reference. This is the practical embodiment of inheritance: one object of the subclass implicitly contains a hidden *parent portion* in its memory layout, and this is what allows a single `Engineer` object to invoke `super.display()` and `super.calcSalary()`.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree

Imagine a real-world **Employee Card** issued by a company:

| Real-World Element | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A blank Employee ID card template with fields for *Name* and *Basic Pay* | `Employee` class |
| A specialized *Engineer ID card* that **already has** Name & Basic Pay **plus** an extra *Specialization* and *Bonus* | `Engineer` class (extends Employee) |
| Issuing **one** physical Engineer card in the HR office | `new Engineer(...)` — single object instantiation |
| The Engineer holding the card can show "Name/Basic Pay" (inherited) **and** "Specialization/Bonus" (own) | Method overriding + `super.display()` call |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Lab Viva:** When you instantiate `Engineer eng = new Engineer(...)`, Java allocates **one contiguous object** in the heap. The first part of the object holds the inherited `Employee` fields (`name`, `basicPay`); the second part holds the Engineer-specific fields (`specialization`, `bonus`). You never need a separate `Employee` object — the parent lives *inside* the child.

## 1.4 Why "Single Object Instantiation" Matters

1. **Memory Efficiency** — Only one heap allocation, not two.
2. **Logical Cohesion** — A single Engineer *is-an* Employee; we don't want two disconnected objects.
3. **Polymorphism Readiness** — The single object reference can later be upcast to `Employee` (the IS-A relationship), enabling runtime polymorphism.

## 1.5 Core Terminology Checklist (Exam Favorite)

| Term | Meaning | KTU 2024 Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **`extends`** | Java keyword establishing inheritance | Must appear in child class declaration |
| **`super`** | Reference to immediate parent class | Used to call parent constructor & methods |
| **Method Overriding** | Child redefines parent's method with same signature | Achieved via `@Override` annotation |
| **IS-A Relationship** | Engineer IS-A Employee (semantic inheritance) | Tested in viva |
| **HAS-A Relationship** | Composition (NOT inheritance) | Often confused in exams |
| **Dynamic Method Dispatch** | JVM resolves overridden method at runtime | Why `eng.display()` calls Engineer's version |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Inheritance Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

When the statement `Engineer eng = new Engineer("Rahul", 50000, "Software", 10000);` is executed, the JVM performs the following sequence:

1. **Class Loading** — The ClassLoader loads `Engineer.class` into method area; this triggers loading of its parent `Employee.class` (transitive closure).
2. **Heap Allocation** — The JVM computes the total object size: `sizeof(Engineer) + sizeof(inherited Employee portion) + 8 bytes (object header)`. A single contiguous block is allocated on the heap.
3. **Default Initialization** — All instance variables (parent + child) are set to default values (`null`, `0`, `0.0`, `false`).
4. **Constructor Chaining** — Java implicitly inserts `super();` as the **first statement** of `Engineer(...)` constructor. Control jumps to the `Employee(String, double)` constructor.
5. **Parent Initialization** — `Employee` constructor initializes `this.name` and `this.basicPay` using `this.fieldName = value`.
6. **Return to Child** — After `Employee` constructor finishes, control returns to `Engineer` constructor, which initializes `this.specialization` and `this.bonus`.
7. **Reference Binding** — The local reference `eng` is assigned the heap address of the freshly constructed object.
8. **Method Resolution** — Subsequent calls like `eng.display()` are dispatched via the **vtable (virtual method table)** at runtime. The JVM looks up `display()` in `Engineer`'s vtable first; if found (overridden), it executes that version. If not, it walks up to `Employee`'s vtable.

## 2.2 The Role of `super` — Two Distinct Uses

| Usage | Syntax | Purpose | When Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constructor Call** | `super(args)` | Invokes parent class constructor | Must be the **first statement** of child constructor (explicit) — otherwise compiler inserts no-arg `super()` implicitly |
| **Method/Field Access** | `super.methodName()` or `super.fieldName` | Accesses parent's version (bypasses overriding) | When child overrides a method but still wants to reuse parent's logic (extension pattern) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** If the parent class has **only** a parameterized constructor and **no** default (no-arg) constructor, then the child class **must** explicitly write `super(args)` as the first line. Otherwise, the compiler error is: *"Implicit super constructor Employee() is undefined. Must explicitly invoke another constructor"*.

## 2.3 Method Overriding — The "Same Signature" Rule

For a method in the child class to truly **override** a parent method, the following must match exactly:

| Element | Must Match? | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Method name | ✅ Yes | `display` = `display` |
| Parameter list (types, order, count) | ✅ Yes | `()` = `()` |
| Return type | ✅ Co-variant (same or subclass) | `void` = `void` |
| Access modifier | ✅ Same or **broader** (not narrower) | `public` overriding `protected` is allowed |
| Exception list | ✅ Same, fewer, or narrower checked exceptions | Throwing fewer exceptions is allowed |

> [!NOTE]
> The `@Override` annotation is **not mandatory** syntactically, but KTU examiners reward its use because it triggers a compile-time check — preventing accidental *overloading* (which would silently create a new method instead of overriding).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Inheritance & Polymorphism Mechanics

| # | Concept | Rule / Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Inheritance | `class B extends A` | One parent only |
| 2 | Multilevel | `class C extends B extends A` | A → B → C chain |
| 3 | Hierarchical | Multiple children, one parent | A → B, A → C |
| 4 | Multiple (NOT in Java classes) | `class C extends A, B` ❌ | Use interfaces instead |
| 5 | `super()` invocation | First line of child constructor | Mandatory if parent has only parameterized constructor |
| 6 | Method resolution order | Child vtable → Parent vtable | Dynamic dispatch |
| 7 | Type-casting (Upcast) | `Employee e = new Engineer();` | Always safe (IS-A) |
| 8 | Type-casting (Downcast) | `Engineer e2 = (Engineer) emp;` | Risky — needs `instanceof` check |
| 9 | Object size | `Size = Σ(all inherited fields) + Σ(child fields) + 8 bytes` | Memory layout |
| 10 | Polymorphic call | `e.display()` invokes `Engineer.display()` | Late binding |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Payroll Software** — A single Engineer object, with role-specific bonuses, processed uniformly through the generic `calcSalary()` interface.
- **GUI Frameworks** — Swing/AWT use deep inheritance trees (`JFrame → Frame → Window → Container → Component`).
- **JDBC API** — All drivers inherit from `java.sql.Driver`; the application uses the generic reference.
- **Android SDK** — `Activity`, `Service`, `BroadcastReceiver` are parent classes; apps extend them.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Program Construction & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Complete Java Source Code (Lab-Ready, KTU-Compliant)

```java
// File: EmployeeEngineerDemo.java
// Aim: Demonstrate single inheritance & single object instantiation

// ===== PARENT CLASS =====
class Employee {
    // Step 1: Protected/Public data members for inheritance access
    protected String name;
    protected double basicPay;

    // Step 2: Parameterized constructor (no default; forces super() in child)
    public Employee(String name, double basicPay) {
        this.name = name;
        this.basicPay = basicPay;
        System.out.println("[Employee] Constructor invoked for: " + name);
    }

    // Step 3: Method to display parent-specific details
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("--- Employee Details ---");
        System.out.println("Name      : " + this.name);
        System.out.println("Basic Pay : Rs. " + this.basicPay);
    }

    // Step 4: Base salary calculation
    public double calcSalary() {
        return this.basicPay;
    }
}

// ===== CHILD CLASS =====
class Engineer extends Employee {
    // Step 5: Child-specific data members
    private String specialization;
    private double bonus;

    // Step 6: Constructor chaining via super()
    public Engineer(String name, double basicPay, String specialization, double bonus) {
        super(name, basicPay);  // MUST be first line
        this.specialization = specialization;
        this.bonus = bonus;
        System.out.println("[Engineer] Constructor invoked for: " + name);
    }

    // Step 7: Overriding display() — extends parent behavior
    @Override
    public void display() {
        super.display();   // Reuse parent's display logic
        System.out.println("Specialization : " + this.specialization);
        System.out.println("Bonus         : Rs. " + this.bonus);
    }

    // Step 8: Overriding calcSalary() — adds bonus to base
    @Override
    public double calcSalary() {
        return super.calcSalary() + this.bonus;
    }
}

// ===== DRIVER CLASS =====
public class EmployeeEngineerDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // === SINGLE OBJECT INSTANTIATION ===
        Engineer eng = new Engineer("Rahul Krishnan", 50000.0, "Software Engineering", 10000.0);

        // Calls overridden method — executes Engineer's display()
        eng.display();

        // Calls overridden method — returns 50000 + 10000 = 60000
        System.out.println("\nTotal Salary : Rs. " + eng.calcSalary());
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Expected Output (Verbatim)

```
[Employee] Constructor invoked for: Rahul Krishnan
[Engineer] Constructor invoked for: Rahul Krishnan
--- Employee Details ---
Name      : Rahul Krishnan
Basic Pay : Rs. 50000.0
Specialization : Software Engineering
Bonus         : Rs. 10000.0

Total Salary : Rs. 60000.0
```

## 3.3 Exhaustive Execution Trace (Row-by-Row)

| Line | Statement | Action | Heap State After |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Engineer eng = new Engineer(...)` | ClassLoader loads `Engineer.class` → triggers loading of `Employee.class` | — |
| 2 | `new Engineer("Rahul", 50000, "Software", 10000)` | Allocates **one** object: `{name, basicPay, specialization, bonus}` + 8-byte header | Object memory reserved |
| 3 | Implicit `super(name, basicPay)` inserted | Jumps to `Employee(String, double)` | `name = null`, `basicPay = 0.0` |
| 4 | `this.name = name; this.basicPay = basicPay;` | Parent fields populated | `name = "Rahul"`, `basicPay = 50000.0` |
| 5 | Returns to `Engineer` constructor | `super()` call completed | Same object |
| 6 | `this.specialization = specialization; this.bonus = bonus;` | Child fields populated | `specialization = "Software"`, `bonus = 10000.0` |
| 7 | `eng.display()` | JVM checks vtable → finds `Engineer.display()` | — |
| 8 | `super.display()` inside `display()` | Executes `Employee.display()` (prints name & basicPay) | — |
| 9 | Continues in `Engineer.display()` | Prints specialization & bonus | — |
| 10 | `eng.calcSalary()` | vtable → `Engineer.calcSalary()` → calls `super.calcSalary()` → returns 50000.0 + 10000.0 | — |

## 3.4 Common Compile-Time / Runtime Errors (KTU Lab Record Must Include)

| # | Error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `cannot find symbol: method display()` (in `Employee` ref) | Accidentally downcasting & missing method | Cast to `Engineer` first |
| 2 | `Implicit super constructor Employee() is undefined` | Parent has no default constructor | Add explicit `super(args)` |
| 3 | `attempting to assign weaker access privileges; was public` | Overriding `public` with `private` | Keep access same or wider |
| 4 | `method does not override or implement a method from a supertype` | Signature mismatch (overload ≠ override) | Match parameter list exactly |
| 5 | `incompatible types: Engineer cannot be converted to Employee` | Wrong direction cast | Engineer IS-A Employee (upcast is safe) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Class Diagram)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Employee {
        +String name
        +double basicPay
        +Employee(String name, double basicPay)
        +void display()
        +double calcSalary()
    }
    class Engineer {
        -String specialization
        -double bonus
        +Engineer(String name, double basicPay, String specialization, double bonus)
        +void display()
        +double calcSalary()
    }
    Employee <|-- Engineer : extends
```

## 4.2 Object Instantiation & Memory Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[main: Engineer eng = new Engineer...] --> B[ClassLoader: Load Engineer.class]
    B --> C[Transitive: Load Employee.class]
    C --> D[Heap: Allocate Single Object]
    D --> E[Object Header: 8 bytes]
    E --> F[Inherited Employee Portion: name, basicPay]
    F --> G[Engineer Portion: specialization, bonus]
    G --> H[Implicit super call: Employee constructor]
    H --> I[Initialize name, basicPay]
    I --> J[Return to Engineer constructor]
    J --> K[Initialize specialization, bonus]
    K --> L[Bind reference 'eng' to heap address]
```

## 4.3 Method Call Resolution Flow (Dynamic Dispatch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[eng.display called] --> Lookup{VTable Lookup: Engineer.display}
    Lookup -->|Found - Overridden| ExecEng[Execute Engineer.display]
    ExecEng --> SuperCall[super.display invoked]
    SuperCall --> ExecParent[Execute Employee.display]
    ExecParent --> PrintParent[Print Name and Basic Pay]
    PrintParent --> ContinueEng[Return to Engineer.display]
    ContinueEng --> PrintChild[Print Specialization and Bonus]
    PrintChild --> End[Return to main]
```

## 4.4 Constructor Chaining Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main()
    participant Eng as Engineer(...)
    participant Emp as Employee(...)
    Main->>Eng: new Engineer("Rahul", 50000, "Software", 10000)
    activate Eng
    Eng->>Emp: super("Rahul", 50000)
    activate Emp
    Emp->>Emp: this.name = "Rahul"
    Emp->>Emp: this.basicPay = 50000.0
    Emp-->>Eng: return (parent initialized)
    deactivate Emp
    Eng->>Eng: this.specialization = "Software"
    Eng->>Eng: this.bonus = 10000.0
    Eng-->>Main: object reference returned
    deactivate Eng
```

## 4.5 Single vs. Multiple Object Instantiation — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Multi[Multiple Object Approach - NOT used here]
        E1[Employee Object]:::bad
        EN1[Engineer Object]:::bad
        E1 -.->|has-a / composition| EN1
    end
    subgraph Single[Single Object Approach - THIS experiment]
        ENGOBJ[Engineer Object contains: name, basicPay, specialization, bonus]:::good
    end
    classDef bad fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef good fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Mermaid:** Arrows (`-->`) represent flow of execution. Dotted arrows (`-.->`) represent weak/cross-class relationships. Solid arrows (`-->`) in class diagrams denote inheritance (`<|--`).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Define inheritance in Java. How is it implemented using the `extends` keyword? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Inheritance is an OOP mechanism that allows a new class (subclass) to acquire the properties and methods of an existing class (superclass), promoting code reuse and establishing an *is-a* relationship. In Java, it is implemented using the **`extends`** keyword, as in:

```java
class Engineer extends Employee { ... }
```

This means `Engineer` inherits all non-private members of `Employee`. Java supports single class inheritance — a class can extend only one parent directly. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is method overriding? State any two rules that must be followed while overriding a method in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its parent class, using the **same name, same return type, and same parameter list**.

**Two Rules:**
1. The access modifier in the child cannot be more restrictive than the parent's (e.g., overriding `public` with `private` is illegal).
2. The parameter list must be **identical**; otherwise, it becomes *overloading*, not *overriding*.
3. (Optional third) Checked exceptions thrown can be the same, fewer, or narrower, but not broader. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptor Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer either OR choice.

---

### **Question A — Option I (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of single inheritance in Java with a suitable diagram. Write a Java program to create a class `Employee` with data members `name` and `basicPay`, and methods `display()` and `calcSalary()`. Create a subclass `Engineer` that adds `specialization` and `bonus`, and overrides both methods to include the bonus in salary calculation.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Concept Explanation (2 marks):**
Single inheritance means a class inherits from **exactly one** direct parent. In Java, this is enforced because the language does not allow `class C extends A, B`. The `Engineer → Employee` relationship represents an *is-a* link: every engineer *is* an employee.

**Java Program (5 marks):**

```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    double basicPay;
    public Employee(String name, double basicPay) {
        this.name = name;
        this.basicPay = basicPay;
    }
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Basic Pay: " + basicPay);
    }
    public double calcSalary() {
        return basicPay;
    }
}

class Engineer extends Employee {
    String specialization;
    double bonus;
    public Engineer(String n, double b, String s, double bn) {
        super(n, b);
        this.specialization = s;
        this.bonus = bn;
    }
    @Override
    public void display() {
        super.display();
        System.out.println("Specialization: " + specialization + ", Bonus: " + bonus);
    }
    @Override
    public double calcSalary() {
        return super.calcSalary() + bonus;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Engineer e = new Engineer("Anu", 45000, "Civil", 8000);
        e.display();
        System.out.println("Total Salary = Rs. " + e.calcSalary());
    }
}
```

**Output:** Name, Basic Pay, Specialization, Bonus displayed, then `Total Salary = Rs. 53000.0`. **(7 marks total)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the role of the `super` keyword in Java? Explain its two uses with examples from the Employee-Engineer program.

**Model Solution (b):**

The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It serves two distinct purposes:

1. **`super(args)` to call parent constructor (3 marks):** Used to initialize the inherited portion of the child object. In `Engineer` constructor, `super(name, basicPay)` invokes `Employee(String, double)`, ensuring `name` and `basicPay` are set. If omitted, the compiler inserts `super()` (no-arg), which fails when the parent has no default constructor.

2. **`super.method()` to call parent's overridden method (3 marks):** When `Engineer.display()` calls `super.display()`, it executes the *parent's* `display()` version, avoiding infinite recursion. Similarly, `super.calcSalary()` returns the base pay, to which `bonus` is added.

**Why this matters (1 mark):** Without `super`, the child cannot access hidden private fields of the parent, and method calls would resolve to the child itself, causing logical errors.

---

### **Question B — Option II (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding. Show how overriding is used in the `Engineer` class to redefine `calcSalary()` so that total salary = basicPay + bonus.

**Model Solution (a):**

| Aspect | Overloading | Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Same method name, **different** parameter list | Same method name, **same** parameter list |
| Class scope | Within the same class | Across parent and child (inheritance) |
| Binding | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| `@Override` annotation | Optional (typically omitted) | Strongly recommended |
| Purpose | Increases readability | Provides specific behavior |

**Code for Overriding `calcSalary()` (4 marks):**

```java
@Override
double calcSalary() {
    return super.calcSalary() + this.bonus;  // 50000 + 10000 = 60000
}
```

Here, `super.calcSalary()` returns the parent's computation (`basicPay`), and the child adds `bonus`. **(7 marks total)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain dynamic method dispatch with a diagram. What will be the output of invoking `display()` on an `Engineer` reference vs. an `Employee` reference pointing to the same Engineer object?

**Model Solution (b):**

**Dynamic Method Dispatch (3 marks):** It is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved **at runtime** based on the **actual object type**, not the reference type. The JVM uses the vtable (virtual method table) for this.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Employee ref = new Engineer();
        ref.display();
              ↓
   [Runtime check: actual object is Engineer]
              ↓
   [vtable lookup: Engineer.display found]
              ↓
   [Execute Engineer.display() → calls super.display() inside]
```

**Output Analysis (2 marks):**

| Reference Declaration | `display()` Output |
|---|---|
| `Engineer eng = new Engineer(...); eng.display();` | Full output: Name, Basic Pay, Specialization, Bonus |
| `Employee emp = new Engineer(...); emp.display();` | **Same** full output — because of dynamic dispatch, Engineer's version runs |
| `Engineer eng2 = (Engineer) new Employee(...);` | ❌ `ClassCastException` at runtime (Engineer is not a parent) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting `super(...)` as first line** in child constructor → 1 mark deducted.
> 2. **Omitting `@Override` annotation** → 0.5 mark deducted (best practice expected in 2024 scheme).
> 3. **Creating two objects** (`Employee e = new Employee(); Engineer eng = new Engineer();`) when the question asks for *single object instantiation* → 2 marks deducted — examiners explicitly test whether you understood the IS-A relationship.
> 4. **Confusing overriding with overloading** (changing parameter list instead of matching it) → 0 marks for that sub-question; the method silently becomes a *new* method, not an override.
> 5. **Not invoking `super.display()`** in the child's overridden `display()` → parent fields not printed → output mismatch → 1.5 marks lost.
> 6. **Wrong access modifier** (e.g., `private` instead of `public` when overriding `public` parent method) → compile error → 0 marks for execution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Inheritance** = `extends` keyword; **single** class inheritance only in Java.
- ✅ **Single object instantiation** = one `new` call creates the *entire* parent + child object in one heap block.
- ✅ **`super(args)`** must be the **first** statement of the child constructor.
- ✅ **Method overriding** requires **identical** signature; use **`@Override`** annotation.
- ✅ **`super.method()`** calls the *parent's* version of an overridden method (avoids infinite recursion).
- ✅ **Dynamic method dispatch** = runtime resolution based on actual object type, not reference type.
- ✅ **Upcasting** (`Employee e = new Engineer();`) is always safe; **downcasting** needs `instanceof` check.
- ✅ Child's overridden method **cannot** have a more restrictive access modifier than parent's.
- ✅ `@Override` is **optional** syntactically but **mandatory by best practice** in KTU 2024 lab evaluation.
- ✅ Memory layout = `{object header 8B} + {inherited fields} + {child fields}` — all in one object.
- ✅ IS-A = inheritance; HAS-A = composition — do not confuse in viva questions.
- ✅ Without `super` in child constructor, compiler error: *"constructor Employee in class Employee cannot be applied to given types"*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to demonstrate the usage of try, catch, throws, and finally
Write a Java program to demonstrate thread synchronization
Write a Java program implementing a multi-threaded application with two threads: one displaying "Java is simple" every 1 second and another displaying "Java is robust" every 2 seconds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program implementing a multi-threaded application with two threads: one displaying "Java is simple" every 1 second and another displaying "Java is robust" every 2 seconds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multithreading** in Java is a concurrent execution feature of the Java Runtime Environment (JRE) that allows a single program (process) to execute **two or more threads** independently, each handling a distinct task, while sharing the same process resources such as heap memory and file handles.

As per the **KTU 2024 B.Tech (CSE) OOP-Java Lab syllabus (PCEOL408)**, this experiment falls under the competency area of *"Implementing Concurrency using the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` interface"*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A **Thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. In Java, every program has at least one thread — the *main thread* — spawned automatically by the `JVM` (Java Virtual Machine) when `main()` is invoked. Additional user-defined threads run **concurrently** with the main thread, governed by the underlying OS thread scheduler.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
> 
> Imagine a single chef (single thread) trying to cook an entire banquet alone — boil rice, fry chicken, bake bread, and serve soup — **one dish at a time**. Customers wait forever.
> 
> Now imagine **four chefs** in the same kitchen, sharing the same stove, oven, and pantry. Each chef works on a separate dish **simultaneously**. The kitchen (program) finishes all dishes much faster. Each chef is a **Thread**, and the shared kitchen resources are the **process heap memory**.
> 
> In our experiment:
> - **Chef 1** shouts *"Java is simple"* every **1 second** (faster chef).
> - **Chef 2** shouts *"Java is robust"* every **2 seconds** (slower chef).
> - Both chefs work **concurrently** inside the same kitchen (same `JVM` process).

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Thread** | Lightweight sub-process; the basic unit of CPU utilization. |
| **`Thread` class** | Concrete class in `java.lang` used to create threads. |
| **`Runnable` interface** | Functional interface (single method `run()`) used to define thread task. |
| **`start()`** | Method that creates a new call stack and invokes `run()`. |
| **`run()`** | Entry point of the thread's task; **never called directly**. |
| **`sleep(ms)`** | Static method that pauses the current thread for `ms` milliseconds. |
| **`InterruptedException`** | Checked exception thrown when a sleeping thread is interrupted. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Fact:** `sleep()` is a **static** method of the `Thread` class. It pauses the **currently executing** thread, not the thread on which it is invoked. This is a frequently tested point.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Default Values)

- **Default Time Unit for `sleep()`:** **milliseconds** → `1000 ms = 1 second`.
- **Thread Priority Range:** `1` (MIN_PRIORITY) to `10` (MAX_PRIORITY); default is `5` (NORM_PRIORITY).
- **Number of Threads in this Experiment:** **2 user threads + 1 main thread = 3 threads** in the JVM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Ways to Create a Thread in Java

Java provides **two standard approaches** defined in the KTU syllabus:

### Approach A — Extending the `Thread` Class
A class inherits from `java.lang.Thread` and overrides the `public void run()` method. This approach is used in this experiment.

### Approach B — Implementing the `Runnable` Interface
A class implements `java.lang.Runnable` and provides the `run()` method. The object is then passed to a `Thread` constructor. **Preferred** in professional code because Java does not support multiple class inheritance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which approach is better?"*, the official answer is: **"Implementing the `Runnable` interface is preferred because it allows the class to extend another class and separates the task from the thread object."**

## 2.2 Lifecycle of a Thread (KTU High-Yield Diagram Concept)

A thread transitions through **5 states** during its lifetime:

1. **New** — Thread object is created but `start()` not yet called.
2. **Runnable** — `start()` invoked; thread is ready, waiting for CPU.
3. **Running** — Thread scheduler picks the thread; `run()` executes.
4. **Blocked / Waiting / Timed-Waiting** — Thread paused (e.g., `sleep()`, `wait()`).
5. **Terminated (Dead)** — `run()` method completes; thread cannot be restarted.

> [!NOTE]
> **`sleep()` causes a Timed-Waiting state**, NOT a Dead state. After the timer expires, the thread becomes Runnable again and competes for CPU.

## 2.3 How `sleep()` Works Internally

When `Thread.sleep(1000)` is executed:

1. The current thread releases the CPU.
2. The thread is placed in the **Timed-Waiting** state for exactly **1000 milliseconds** (subject to OS timer accuracy).
3. The JVM thread scheduler may schedule **other threads** during this gap → this is what makes our two messages appear interleaved.
4. After 1 second, the thread transitions back to **Runnable**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | KTU Board Note |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Import statement | `import java.lang.*;` (optional — auto-imported) | `java.lang` is auto-imported. |
| Extend Thread | `class MyThread extends Thread { public void run() { ... } }` | `run()` must be `public`. |
| Start thread | `t1.start();` | Never call `t1.run()` directly. |
| Sleep for 1 s | `Thread.sleep(1000);` | Argument is in **milliseconds**. |
| Handle exception | `try { ... } catch (InterruptedException e) { ... }` | `InterruptedException` is **checked**. |
| Thread yield | `Thread.yield();` | Hints scheduler to give chance to other threads. |
| Time conversion | $1 \text{ second} = 1000 \text{ milliseconds}$ | Used universally in `sleep()`. |
| Threads in experiment | **2 user threads** (T1, T2) | Plus the implicit **main thread** = 3 total. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Each HTTP request handled by a separate thread.
- **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX):** Event Dispatch Thread prevents UI freezing.
- **Banking Software:** Concurrent transactions processed in parallel.
- **Real-Time Systems:** Sensor data acquisition + actuator control running in parallel threads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this lab experiment, the engineer is learning the **fundamental building block** of every server-side Java technology — Servlet containers, JDBC connection pools, and Java NIO all rely on thread management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Code Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm Design (Step-by-Step Logic)

The experiment requires two threads operating at different periodic intervals. The algorithm is:

1. **Define** two classes: `SimpleThread` and `RobustThread`, both extending `Thread`.
2. **Override** the `run()` method in each class.
3. **Inside `run()`**: place an infinite `while(true)` loop.
4. **Print** the required message.
5. **Call** `Thread.sleep()` with the required delay — `1000` ms for Simple, `2000` ms for Robust.
6. **Wrap** the `sleep()` call in a `try-catch` block for `InterruptedException`.
7. **In `main()`**: create two `Thread` objects and invoke `start()` on each.
8. The **main thread** also calls `Thread.sleep()` to keep the program alive long enough for the output to be visible.

## 3.2 Complete Java Program (Lab-Ready Code)

```java
// File: MultiThreadDemo.java
// Lab: PCEOL408 - Module Experiment 2
// Description: Two-thread application with different sleep intervals.

class SimpleThread extends Thread {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (true) {
                System.out.println("Java is simple");
                Thread.sleep(1000); // 1 second delay
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("SimpleThread interrupted: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}

class RobustThread extends Thread {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (true) {
                System.out.println("Java is robust");
                Thread.sleep(2000); // 2 second delay
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("RobustThread interrupted: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}

public class MultiThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create two thread objects
        SimpleThread t1 = new SimpleThread();
        RobustThread t2 = new RobustThread();

        // Start both threads
        t1.start();
        t2.start();

        // Keep main thread alive for 10 seconds to observe output
        try {
            Thread.sleep(10000);
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Main thread interrupted.");
        }

        System.out.println("Main thread exiting. Program terminated.");
    }
}
```

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Execution Walkthrough

| Step | Line of Code | Internal Action | Resulting State |
|------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | `SimpleThread t1 = new SimpleThread();` | Object created on heap; thread is **New**. | T1 = New |
| 2 | `RobustThread t2 = new RobustThread();` | Object created on heap; thread is **New**. | T2 = New |
| 3 | `t1.start();` | JVM creates new call stack; `t1.run()` invoked. | T1 = Runnable/Running |
| 4 | `t2.start();` | JVM creates new call stack; `t2.run()` invoked. | T2 = Runnable/Running |
| 5 | Inside `t1.run()`: `println("Java is simple");` | Output printed. | — |
| 6 | `Thread.sleep(1000);` | T1 enters **Timed-Waiting** for 1000 ms. | T1 = Timed-Waiting |
| 7 | Meanwhile, scheduler may pick T2 → prints "Java is robust" → sleeps 2000 ms. | T2 = Timed-Waiting | Output interleaved |
| 8 | After 1 s, T1 wakes → prints again → sleeps. | Loop continues. | — |
| 9 | After 10 s, main thread resumes → prints final line → program ends. | All threads terminate. | Dead |

## 3.4 Mathematical Timing Analysis (Output Sequence)

Let $T$ denote the time (in seconds) elapsed since program start. The output sequence follows this pattern:

$$
\text{Output}(T) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{``Java is simple''} & \text{if } T \bmod 1 = 0 \text{ (approx.) and T1 is scheduled} \\
\text{``Java is robust''} & \text{if } T \bmod 2 = 0 \text{ (approx.) and T2 is scheduled}
\end{cases}
$$

**Sample Output Trace (first 6 seconds):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T = 0 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is simple} \\
T = 0 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is robust} \\
T = 1 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is simple} \\
T = 2 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is simple} \\
T = 2 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is robust} \\
T = 3 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is simple} \\
T = 4 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is simple} \\
T = 4 \text{ s} &: \quad \text{Java is robust} \\
T &= \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact ordering between the two threads is **non-deterministic** because the OS thread scheduler is platform-dependent. KTU examiners accept any valid interleaving as long as the **time intervals are correct**.

## 3.5 Why `start()` and NOT `run()`?

| Call | What Happens | Is it a Thread? |
|------|--------------|-----------------|
| `t1.run();` | Executes `run()` in the **current** (main) call stack. | ❌ No — just a normal method call. |
| `t1.start();` | Creates a **new call stack**, then invokes `run()`. | ✅ Yes — true multithreading. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most Asked Question:** *"What is the difference between `start()` and `run()` methods?"* — Always write that `start()` registers the thread with the thread scheduler and creates a new stack, while `run()` is just a normal method invocation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Thread Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start: main method invoked] --> B[Create SimpleThread object t1]
    A --> C[Create RobustThread object t2]
    B --> D{t1.start called}
    C --> E{t2.start called}
    D --> F[New call stack created for T1]
    E --> G[New call stack created for T2]
    F --> H[T1 enters run method]
    G --> I[T2 enters run method]
    H --> J[Print Java is simple]
    I --> K[Print Java is robust]
    J --> L[Thread.sleep 1000 ms]
    K --> M[Thread.sleep 2000 ms]
    L --> N{Timer expired}
    M --> O{Timer expired}
    N -->|Yes| H
    O -->|Yes| I
    N -->|Interrupted| P[Print InterruptedException message]
    O -->|Interrupted| P
    P --> Q[Thread Terminated]
    H --> Q
    I --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Output Generation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Thread
    participant T1 as SimpleThread
    participant T2 as RobustThread
    participant JVM as JVM Scheduler

    Main->>T1: t1.start()
    Main->>T2: t2.start()
    Main->>Main: Thread.sleep(10000)

    activate T1
    activate T2

    loop Every 1 second
        T1->>JVM: Request CPU
        JVM-->>T1: Allocated
        T1->>Console: print "Java is simple"
        T1->>JVM: Thread.sleep(1000)
    end

    loop Every 2 seconds
        T2->>JVM: Request CPU
        JVM-->>T2: Allocated
        T2->>Console: print "Java is robust"
        T2->>JVM: Thread.sleep(2000)
    end

    Note over T1,T2: Scheduler interleaves both threads concurrently
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Class & Method Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MainClass[MultiThreadDemo]
        A1[main method] --> A2[Create SimpleThread t1]
        A1 --> A3[Create RobustThread t2]
        A2 --> A4[t1.start]
        A3 --> A5[t2.start]
        A4 --> A6[Thread.sleep 10000 ms]
    end

    subgraph ThreadOne[SimpleThread extends Thread]
        B1[run method] --> B2[System.out.println Java is simple]
        B2 --> B3[Thread.sleep 1000 ms]
        B3 --> B1
    end

    subgraph ThreadTwo[RobustThread extends Thread]
        C1[run method] --> C2[System.out.println Java is robust]
        C2 --> C3[Thread.sleep 2000 ms]
        C3 --> C1
    end

    A4 -.Invokes.-> B1
    A5 -.Invokes.-> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrows in the block architecture represent the **cross-thread invocation** initiated by the `start()` call from the main thread to the user-defined thread objects.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define a *thread* in Java. Differentiate between a process and a thread.
>
> **Model Answer (for board valuation):**
> A **thread** is the smallest independent unit of execution within a process. It is a lightweight sub-process that shares the memory and resources of its parent process.
>
> | Process | Thread |
> |---------|--------|
> | Heavyweight; has its own memory. | Lightweight; shares memory with peer threads. |
> | Inter-process communication is expensive. | Intra-thread communication is cheap. |
> | Context switching is costly. | Context switching is fast. |
> | Created via OS API. | Created via `Thread` class or `Runnable`. |
>
> *(Word count target: ~80 words for 3 marks.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is the role of the `start()` method? Why can't we directly call `run()`?
>
> **Model Answer:** The `start()` method is responsible for **registering the thread with the JVM thread scheduler**, allocating a **new call stack**, and then invoking the `run()` method on that new stack — thereby creating a truly concurrent execution path.
> If `run()` is called directly (e.g., `t1.run()`), it executes as a **normal method** in the current (main) thread's call stack. No new thread is created, and the program becomes **single-threaded** despite the code looking identical.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU 2024 Expected Pattern]

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the **two ways of creating threads in Java** with suitable code snippets. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a complete Java program to create **two threads** — `ThreadA` prints *"Java is simple"* every **1 second** and `ThreadB` prints *"Java is robust"* every **2 seconds**. Explain the role of `Thread.sleep()` in the program. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Way 1: Extending the `Thread` class**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Thread running: " + getName());
    }
}
// Usage: new MyThread().start();
```

- ✅ **Advantage:** Simple to write; direct access to `Thread` methods like `getName()`.
- ❌ **Disadvantage:** Occupies the single inheritance slot; cannot extend any other class.

**Way 2: Implementing the `Runnable` interface**

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Task running");
    }
}
// Usage: new Thread(new MyTask()).start();
```

- ✅ **Advantage:** Class can still extend another class; separates task from thread.
- ❌ **Disadvantage:** Slightly more verbose; need a `Thread` wrapper object.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```java
class ThreadA extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (true) {
                System.out.println("Java is simple");
                Thread.sleep(1000);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
    }
}

class ThreadB extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (true) {
                System.out.println("Java is robust");
                Thread.sleep(2000);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        ThreadA a = new ThreadA();
        ThreadB b = new ThreadB();
        a.start();
        b.start();
        Thread.sleep(10000); // Keep main alive
    }
}
```

**Role of `Thread.sleep()`:** It pauses the currently executing thread for a specified number of milliseconds, allowing the **thread scheduler to allocate CPU time to other threads**. It is essential in this program to enforce the **1-second and 2-second periodic intervals** and to demonstrate **concurrent execution**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **lifecycle of a thread** in Java. Indicate the role of the `sleep()` method in the state transitions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods with a suitable example. Write a Java program to demonstrate thread creation by implementing the `Runnable` interface for the same two-message scenario. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Thread Lifecycle States:**

1. **New** → Thread object created.
2. **Runnable** → `start()` called; awaits CPU.
3. **Running** → Scheduler selects; `run()` executes.
4. **Blocked / Waiting / Timed-Waiting** → `sleep()`, `wait()`, I/O.
5. **Terminated** → `run()` returns; thread dead.

**Role of `sleep()`:** Transitions the thread from **Running** to **Timed-Waiting**. After the timer expires, the thread returns to **Runnable** and competes for CPU again.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```java
class MessageTask implements Runnable {
    private final String message;
    private final int delay;
    
    public MessageTask(String msg, int d) {
        this.message = msg;
        this.delay = d;
    }
    
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (true) {
                System.out.println(message);
                Thread.sleep(delay);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
    }
}

public class RunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new MessageTask("Java is simple", 1000));
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new MessageTask("Java is robust", 2000));
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        Thread.sleep(10000);
    }
}
```

**Difference Table:**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Thread creation | ✅ Creates new thread. | ❌ No new thread. |
| Call stack | New stack allocated. | Uses current stack. |
| Invocation | Called once per thread. | Can be called multiple times. |
| Concurrency | Achieved. | Not achieved. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Marks lost for calling `run()` directly** — Many students write `t1.run()` instead of `t1.start()`. This makes the program single-threaded and **loses 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Missing `try-catch` around `Thread.sleep()`** — Compilation error. The `InterruptedException` is **checked**; must be caught or declared. **Loses 2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to make `run()` `public`** — Override must be `public void run()`. Writing `void run()` (default access) is a common mistake. **Loses 1 mark**.
> 4. **No infinite loop** — Without `while(true)`, the thread prints only **once** and terminates. Examiners deduct **3 marks** for not showing periodic behavior.
> 5. **Wrong time unit** — Writing `Thread.sleep(1)` thinking it is 1 second. Argument is in **milliseconds**; 1 second = `1000`. **Loses 2 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**
>
> - ✅ A **thread** is a lightweight sub-process; the **JVM** creates the *main thread* automatically.
> - ✅ Java supports **two ways** to create threads: extending `Thread` OR implementing `Runnable`.
> - ✅ `Runnable` is **preferred** (allows inheritance, separates task from thread).
> - ✅ `start()` creates a **new call stack** and invokes `run()`; `run()` alone is just a method call.
> - ✅ `Thread.sleep(ms)` is **static**, takes **milliseconds**, and causes **Timed-Waiting** state.
> - ✅ `InterruptedException` is a **checked exception** → must use `try-catch`.
> - ✅ `run()` must be declared `public void run()` with **no arguments**.
> - ✅ A thread **cannot be restarted** once it terminates; calling `start()` again throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
> - ✅ In this experiment: **T1** sleeps `1000` ms (1 s), **T2** sleeps `2000` ms (2 s).
> - ✅ The program uses **infinite `while(true)`** to demonstrate continuous periodic output.
> - ✅ The **main thread** must also sleep (e.g., 10 s) to keep the JVM alive for observation.
> - ✅ Output **interleaving is non-deterministic** — depends on OS thread scheduler.
> - ✅ Total threads in this program = **3** (1 main + 2 user threads).
> - ✅ `Thread.yield()` is a **hint** to the scheduler, not a guarantee.
> - ✅ `System.out.println` is **thread-safe** internally (synchronized), so output lines won't be corrupted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program for a simple calculator using Java Swing with buttons for digits and the + - * % operations, a text field to display the result, and exception handling for divide by zero

Write a Java program using Swing to create a frame with two text fields, two labels, and a button to calculate the factorial of a number entered in the first text field and display it in the second text field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program using Swing to create a frame with two text fields, two labels, and a button to calculate the factorial of a number entered in the first text field and display it in the second text field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Java Swing** is a lightweight, platform-independent GUI (Graphical User Interface) toolkit that is part of the Java Foundation Classes (JFC) and is built on top of the older Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT). It is located in the `javax.swing` package and provides a rich set of components such as `JFrame`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, and `JButton` for building desktop applications.

In the context of this experiment, the program constructs a top-level container (`JFrame`) and embeds five essential components: **two `JLabel` objects** (for static descriptive text), **two `JTextField` objects** (for user input and programmatic output), and **one `JButton` object** (to trigger the factorial computation). A factorial is a mathematical function defined on non-negative integers as the product of all positive integers from 1 to n, denoted as n!.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Factorial)**: For a non-negative integer n, `n! = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 2 × 1`, with the special case `0! = 1` (empty product). For negative numbers, the factorial is **undefined** in the integer domain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **vending machine panel**:
- The **slot where you insert a coin** → `JTextField` (input field).
- The **printed sticker describing the slot** → `JLabel` (descriptive caption).
- The **"Dispense" push button** → `JButton` (event trigger).
- The **slot where the can drops out** → second `JTextField` (output field).
- The **glass cabinet holding all these parts** → `JFrame` (the top-level window).

When you type a number, click the button, an internal "spring mechanism" (the `ActionListener`) fires, computes the result, and pushes it into the output slot — just like our factorial calculator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL408)**: This experiment directly tests the following Course Outcomes — **CO1: Develop Java programs using object-oriented concepts**, **CO2: Design GUI-based applications using Swing and AWT**, and **CO3: Implement event-driven programming using listeners**.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

| Aspect | KTU Expectation |
|---|---|
| Component familiarity | Naming all 5 components correctly |
| Event handling | Wiring `ActionListener` properly |
| Exception handling | `NumberFormatException` for invalid input |
| Code modularity | Constructor-based initialization |
| Output demonstration | Executing with sample inputs (5! = 120) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Swing Hierarchy

Java Swing follows a strict **containment hierarchy**. Every visible component on screen must be added to a container before it becomes visible.

```
java.awt.Component  ←  Top-level abstract class
        │
        ├── java.awt.Container
        │         │
        │         ├── JComponent (Swing base)
        │         │       ├── JLabel
        │         │       ├── JButton
        │         │       ├── JTextField
        │         │
        │         └── JFrame (Top-Level Window)
        │
        └── JLabel, JButton, JTextField (direct subclasses of JComponent)
```

**Operational Flow of the Program:**

1. **`main()` method** creates an instance of the class (which extends `JFrame`).
2. The **constructor** sets the title, size, layout manager, close operation, and adds all 5 components.
3. `setVisible(true)` makes the frame appear on screen.
4. When the user clicks the **button**, the JVM dispatches an `ActionEvent` to all registered `ActionListener`s.
5. The overridden `actionPerformed()` method parses input, computes factorial, and writes the result back.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Range / Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Factorial (mathematical) | $n! = \prod_{i=1}^{n} i$ | $n \geq 0$ |
| Base case | $0! = 1$ | By definition |
| Recursive definition | $n! = n \times (n-1)!$ | For $n \geq 1$ |
| Iterative definition | $n! = 1 \times 2 \times \ldots \times n$ | For $n \geq 0$ |
| `long` range | $\vert \text{long} \vert \leq 9.22 \times 10^{18}$ | Holds up to $20!$ |
| Max factorial in `long` | $20! = 2{,}432{,}902{,}008{,}176{,}640{,}000$ | 18,446,744,073,709,551,615 is `Long.MAX_VALUE` |
| `BigInteger` limit | Arbitrary precision (memory-bound) | Use for $n > 20$ |
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ for iterative factorial | Linear in n |
| Space complexity (iterative) | $O(1)$ | Constant auxiliary space |
| Space complexity (recursive) | $O(n)$ | Due to call stack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note**: For $n \geq 21$, the value of $n!$ exceeds the maximum value of a `long` (`9,223,372,036,854,775,807`). Students must use `BigInteger` from `java.math` package for accurate computation of larger factorials. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU viva voce.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cryptography**: Factorials and combinations appear in RSA key generation and combinatorial number theory.
- **Statistical Mechanics**: The Boltzmann distribution uses factorials in Stirling's approximation.
- **Permutation/Combination Algorithms**: Every "arrange r items from n" problem uses factorials.
- **GUI Development Pattern**: The same `Component → Container → EventListener` pattern appears in **JavaFX**, **Python Tkinter**, **C# WinForms**, and **Android XML layouts** — making Swing foundational knowledge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete, Lab-Ready Java Program (Version 1 — `long` Based)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class FactorialCalculator extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // Step 1: Declare all GUI components as instance variables
    JLabel lblInput, lblOutput;
    JTextField txtInput, txtOutput;
    JButton btnCalculate;

    // Step 2: Constructor - sets up the frame and all components
    public FactorialCalculator() {
        setTitle("Factorial Calculator");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 15, 20));
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Step 3: Instantiate the five required components
        lblInput    = new JLabel("Enter a Number:");
        lblOutput   = new JLabel("Factorial:");
        txtInput    = new JTextField(15);
        txtOutput   = new JTextField(20);
        txtOutput.setEditable(false);   // user cannot type in output box
        btnCalculate = new JButton("Calculate Factorial");

        // Step 4: Register the event listener with the button
        btnCalculate.addActionListener(this);

        // Step 5: Add components to the JFrame in display order
        add(lblInput);
        add(txtInput);
        add(btnCalculate);
        add(lblOutput);
        add(txtOutput);

        // Step 6: Make the frame visible
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Step 7: Handle the button-click event
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == btnCalculate) {
            try {
                int n = Integer.parseInt(txtInput.getText().trim());

                if (n < 0) {
                    txtOutput.setText("Undefined (negative input)");
                } else if (n > 20) {
                    txtOutput.setText("Overflow: use BigInteger");
                } else {
                    long fact = 1;
                    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
                        fact = fact * i;
                    }
                    txtOutput.setText(String.valueOf(fact));
                }
            } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
                txtOutput.setText("Invalid Input!");
            }
        }
    }

    // Step 8: Main method - program entry point
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FactorialCalculator();
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Analytical Walkthrough

**Line Group 1 — Imports**
```java
import javax.swing.*;     // Swing components: JFrame, JLabel, JTextField, JButton
import java.awt.*;        // AWT support: FlowLayout (a layout manager)
import java.awt.event.*;  // Event classes: ActionListener, ActionEvent
```
*Why?* `javax.swing` provides the GUI widgets; `java.awt.FlowLayout` arranges them in a left-to-right flowing line; `java.awt.event` provides the listener interface for button clicks.

**Line Group 2 — Class Declaration**
```java
public class FactorialCalculator extends JFrame implements ActionListener
```
*Why extend JFrame?* The class itself becomes the window — no need to create a separate `JFrame` object.
*Why implement ActionListener?* This allows the class to **listen** for the button-click event, eliminating the need to create a separate listener class.

**Line Group 3 — Component Declarations**
```java
JLabel lblInput, lblOutput;
JTextField txtInput, txtOutput;
JButton btnCalculate;
```
*Why instance variables?* They must be accessible from both the constructor (where they are created) and from `actionPerformed()` (where they are read/written).

**Line Group 4 — Frame Configuration (inside constructor)**

$$
\text{Frame size} = 400 \text{ px (width)} \times 200 \text{ px (height)}
$$

| Call | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setTitle("Factorial Calculator")` | Sets the window title bar text |
| `setSize(400, 200)` | Width × Height in pixels |
| `setLayout(new FlowLayout(...))` | Arranges components left → right, top → bottom |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Closes JVM when window X is clicked |
| `setVisible(true)` | Displays the frame (must be the **last** call) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `setVisible(true)` is called LAST**: If called earlier, the user might briefly see components being added one-by-one (flicker effect). Placing it at the end ensures all components are mounted before display.

**Line Group 5 — Component Instantiation**

| Component | Constructor Argument | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `new JLabel("Enter a Number:")` | String | Static label text shown next to input |
| `new JTextField(15)` | int columns | Visible width = ~15 character columns |
| `new JButton("Calculate Factorial")` | String | Text displayed inside the button |

**Line Group 6 — Event Registration**
```java
btnCalculate.addActionListener(this);
```
*Why?* This single line wires the button to **this object's** `actionPerformed()` method. The keyword `this` refers to the current `FactorialCalculator` object (which implements `ActionListener`).

**Line Group 7 — The Mathematical Engine (factorial loop)**

$$
\text{fact} = \prod_{i=2}^{n} i
$$

```java
long fact = 1;                    // Initialize accumulator to multiplicative identity
for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {    // Loop from 2 to n (1 contributes nothing)
    fact = fact * i;              // Multiply accumulator by current i
}
```

For $n = 5$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: } & fact = 1 \times 2 = 2 \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & fact = 2 \times 3 = 6 \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & fact = 6 \times 4 = 24 \\
\text{Iteration 4: } & fact = 24 \times 5 = 120 \\
\text{Final Answer: } & 5! = 120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line Group 8 — Exception Handling**

```java
catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
    txtOutput.setText("Invalid Input!");
}
```
*Why?* If the user types `"abc"` in `txtInput`, `Integer.parseInt()` throws `NumberFormatException`. We catch it and show a friendly error in the output box — preventing the program from crashing.

### 3.3 Reference Table of Pre-computed Factorials (0! to 20!)

| $n$ | $n!$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | **1** |
| 1 | **1** |
| 2 | **2** |
| 3 | **6** |
| 4 | **24** |
| 5 | **120** |
| 6 | **720** |
| 7 | **5,040** |
| 8 | **40,320** |
| 9 | **362,880** |
| 10 | **3,628,800** |
| 11 | **39,916,800** |
| 12 | **479,001,600** |
| 13 | **6,227,020,800** |
| 14 | **87,178,291,200** |
| 15 | **1,307,674,368,000** |
| 16 | **20,922,789,888,000** |
| 17 | **355,687,428,096,000** |
| 18 | **6,402,373,705,728,000** |
| 19 | **121,645,100,408,832,000** |
| 20 | **2,432,902,008,176,640,000** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap**: A common mistake students make is computing factorial using `int`. Since `int` overflows at `12! = 479,001,600` is still safe, but `13! = 6,227,020,800` overflows `int` (max ≈ 2.1 billion). Always use `long` for $n \leq 20$, and `BigInteger` for larger inputs.

### 3.4 Enhanced Version Using `BigInteger` (For Viva Credit)

```java
import java.math.BigInteger;

public static BigInteger factorial(int n) {
    if (n < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Negative input");
    BigInteger result = BigInteger.ONE;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        result = result.multiply(BigInteger.valueOf(i));
    }
    return result;
}
```

This version handles inputs up to several hundred digits correctly, limited only by JVM heap memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Component Layout Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["JFrame: FactorialCalculator"] --> B["JLabel: Enter a Number"]
    A --> C["JTextField: txtInput 15 columns"]
    A --> D["JButton: Calculate Factorial"]
    A --> E["JLabel: Factorial"]
    A --> F["JTextField: txtOutput 20 columns non-editable"]
    D --> G["ActionListener: this class"]
    G --> H["actionPerformed method"]
    H --> I{"Parse txtInput as int"}
    I --> J["NumberFormatException: show Invalid Input"]
    I --> K{"n less than 0"}
    K --> L["txtOutput: Undefined negative input"]
    K --> M{"n greater than 20"]
    M --> N["txtOutput: Overflow use BigInteger"]
    M --> O["Compute long fact equals 1 loop i 2 to n"]
    O --> P["txtOutput: String valueOf fact"]
```

### 4.2 Class Hierarchy of Swing Components Used

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["java.lang.Object"] --> C["java.awt.Component"]
    C --> CON["java.awt.Container"]
    C --> JC["javax.swing.JComponent"]
    CON --> JF["javax.swing.JFrame"]
    JC --> JL["javax.swing.JLabel"]
    JC --> JB["javax.swing.JButton"]
    JC --> JT["javax.swing.JTextField abstract"]
    JT --> JTF1["JTextField int columns"]
    JF --> APP["FactorialCalculator class"]
    AL["java.awt.event.ActionListener interface"] -.implements.-> APP
```

### 4.3 Execution Flow (User-Interaction Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant F as JFrame
    participant T as txtInput
    participant B as btnCalculate
    participant L as ActionListener
    participant O as txtOutput
    U->>T: Types number 5
    U->>B: Clicks Calculate
    B->>L: Fires ActionEvent
    L->>T: Reads text 5
    L->>L: ParseInteger returns 5
    L->>L: Loop computes 120
    L->>O: setText 120
    O-->>U: Displays 120 in output box
```

### 4.4 Runtime Output Visualization (Textual Mock)

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|        Factorial Calculator              [_][o][x]|
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  Enter a Number:  [   5   ]                       |
|                                                    |
|           [  Calculate Factorial  ]                |
|                                                    |
|  Factorial:       [   120   ]                      |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: This block-level schematic represents the exact spatial arrangement produced by `FlowLayout` when the program runs. The button appears between the input row and the output row, and the labels naturally sit to the left of their corresponding text fields because `FlowLayout` adds components left-to-right.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q:** List the five Swing components used in the factorial calculator program and state the purpose of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`JFrame`** — Top-level window that hosts all other components. (1 mark)
2. **`JLabel`** — Displays static descriptive text ("Enter a Number:", "Factorial:"). (1 mark)
3. **`JTextField`** — Two instances: one for accepting the user's numeric input, another (made non-editable) for displaying the result. (1/2 mark × 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q:** Why is `txtOutput.setEditable(false)` used in the program?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `setEditable(false)` call prevents the user from typing into the output text field. This ensures the factorial result displayed by the program cannot be accidentally overwritten or modified by the user, preserving the integrity of the computed output. (2 marks) It is a best practice in GUI design to make read-only output fields clearly distinguishable from input fields. (1 mark)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style — Question A (14 Marks)]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Swing class hierarchy used in this experiment. Discuss the role of `JFrame`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, and `JButton` with their constructors. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a frame that accepts a number in the first text field, computes its factorial when a button is clicked, and displays the result in the second text field. Handle invalid inputs using exception handling. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Swing Class Hierarchy Explanation (7 marks):**

Java Swing components are part of the `javax.swing` package and follow this hierarchy:

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JFrame}
$$

The class `Component` is the abstract superclass for all UI components. `Container` extends `Component` and can hold other components. `JComponent` (extends `Container`) is the base class for all Swing components, providing pluggable look-and-feel support. The top-level window `JFrame` extends `java.awt.Frame` and inherits from this hierarchy. (3 marks)

**Component roles:**

- **`JFrame`** is the top-level container with a title bar, border, and optional menu bar. Its constructor `JFrame()` creates an invisible frame; `JFrame(String title)` creates one with a title. Methods include `setSize()`, `setTitle()`, `setLayout()`, and `setVisible()`. (1 mark)
- **`JLabel`** displays a single line of read-only text, an image, or both. Constructor: `JLabel(String text)`. (1 mark)
- **`JTextField`** is a single-line text editor. Constructor: `JTextField(int columns)` sets the preferred width. (1 mark)
- **`JButton`** is a push button that generates an `ActionEvent` when clicked. Constructor: `JButton(String label)`. (1 mark)

**(b) Complete Program (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class FactorialApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel l1, l2;
    JTextField t1, t2;
    JButton b1;

    FactorialApp() {
        setTitle("Factorial Calculator");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        l1 = new JLabel("Enter Number:");
        l2 = new JLabel("Factorial:");
        t1 = new JTextField(15);
        t2 = new JTextField(20);
        t2.setEditable(false);
        b1 = new JButton("Calculate");

        b1.addActionListener(this);

        add(l1); add(t1); add(b1); add(l2); add(t2);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            int n = Integer.parseInt(t1.getText());
            long f = 1;
            for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) f *= i;
            t2.setText("" + f);
        } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
            t2.setText("Invalid Input");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FactorialApp();
    }
}
```

*Mark distribution:* Correct imports (1), class declaration extending JFrame and implementing ActionListener (1), constructor setup with all 5 components (2), actionPerformed with valid factorial logic and exception handling (2), main method (1).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice — Question B (14 Marks)]**
>
> **(a)** Differentiate between `java.awt` and `javax.swing` packages. Why is Swing preferred for desktop GUI applications? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Modify the factorial calculator program to use `GridLayout(3, 2)` instead of `FlowLayout`, and display an error message "Number Too Large" in `txtOutput` if the input exceeds 20. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AWT vs Swing (7 marks):**

| Aspect | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
|---|---|---|
| Dependency | Platform-dependent (uses OS peers) | Platform-independent (pure Java) |
| Look & Feel | Matches host OS | Pluggable Look & Feel |
| Component prefix | `Button`, `TextField` | `JButton`, `JTextField` |
| Weight | Heavyweight | Lightweight |
| Package size | Smaller (older API) | Larger, richer feature set |
| MVC support | Limited | Strong (e.g., `JTable`, `JTree`) |

(5 marks for table + 2 marks for explanation) Swing is preferred because it provides a consistent appearance across operating systems, supports pluggable look-and-feel, has a richer component set, and is fully written in Java (avoiding OS-specific bugs). (2 marks)

**(b) Modified Program with `GridLayout` (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class FactorialGrid extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel l1, l2;
    JTextField t1, t2;
    JButton b1;

    FactorialGrid() {
        setTitle("Factorial Grid");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 10, 10));   // 3 rows, 2 columns
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        l1 = new JLabel("Enter Number:");
        l2 = new JLabel("Factorial:");
        t1 = new JTextField();
        t2 = new JTextField();
        t2.setEditable(false);
        b1 = new JButton("Calculate");

        b1.addActionListener(this);

        add(l1); add(t1);
        add(b1); add(l2);
        add(new JLabel("")); add(t2);    // filler label for grid alignment

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            int n = Integer.parseInt(t1.getText());
            if (n > 20) {
                t2.setText("Number Too Large");
            } else {
                long f = 1;
                for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) f *= i;
                t2.setText("" + f);
            }
        } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
            t2.setText("Invalid Input");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FactorialGrid();
    }
}
```

*Mark distribution:* Correct import of `GridLayout` (1), layout set as `new GridLayout(3, 2, 10, 10)` (2), components added in row-major order (1), valid range check `n > 20` with error message (2), exception handling (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** at the end of the constructor → program compiles but no window appears. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Using `int` for factorial** instead of `long` → silent overflow for $n \geq 13$. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Not calling `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** → JVM keeps running after window close. (−0.5 mark)
> 4. **Missing `try-catch` around `Integer.parseInt()`** → program crashes on non-numeric input. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Registering listener on wrong object** (e.g., on `txtInput` instead of `btnCalculate`) → button click does nothing. (−2 marks)
> 6. **Adding components in wrong order** with `FlowLayout` → UI looks jumbled. (−0.5 mark)

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Five Components Required**: `1 JFrame + 2 JLabel + 2 JTextField + 1 JButton` (the prompt explicitly mandates these counts).
- ✅ **Package Imports**: `javax.swing.*`, `java.awt.*`, `java.awt.event.*` — all three are mandatory.
- ✅ **Class Declaration Pattern**: `class <Name> extends JFrame implements ActionListener` — the keyword `implements` (not `extends`) is used for the listener interface.
- ✅ **`addActionListener(this)`**: Must be called on the button (not on the frame or label). The argument `this` works only because the class implements `ActionListener`.
- ✅ **Factorial Range**: Use `long` for $0 \leq n \leq 20$; use `BigInteger` for $n > 20$.
- ✅ **Base Case**: `0! = 1` — must initialize the loop variable `fact = 1` (NOT 0, because multiplication by 0 gives 0).
- ✅ **Loop Structure**: `for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++)` — starting from 2 is correct since multiplying by 1 has no effect.
- ✅ **Exception**: `Integer.parseInt()` throws `NumberFormatException` for non-numeric strings — must be caught in `try-catch`.
- ✅ **`setEditable(false)`**: Applied to `txtOutput` to make it read-only.
- ✅ **`setVisible(true)`**: Must be the LAST line in the constructor.
- ✅ **Layout Choice**: `FlowLayout` is simplest (left-to-right); `GridLayout(rows, cols)` creates a tabular layout; `BorderLayout` has 5 regions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER).
- ✅ **Output Method**: `txtOutput.setText(String.valueOf(fact))` or simply `"" + fact` for string concatenation.
- ✅ **Constructor over `init()` method**: KTU prefers constructing GUI inside the class constructor (not in a separate `init()` method).
- ✅ **`setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`**: Constant imported from `JFrame`; without it, the JVM keeps running after the window closes.
- ✅ **Memorize**: $5! = 120$, $6! = 720$, $7! = 5040$ — these are the most commonly asked sample outputs in KTU lab exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to display all records from a table using Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL408 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (JAVA) LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to display all records from a table using Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)** is a Java API (Application Programming Interface) that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases. It is part of the `java.sql` package and provides a standard set of classes and interfaces for executing SQL statements, retrieving results, and managing database connections.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** JDBC is a Java-based data access technology used for Java database connectivity. It is a part of the Java Standard Edition (Java SE) platform and defines how a client may access a database. It provides methods to query and update data in a database and is oriented towards relational databases.

A **Record** in the context of a relational database is a single row (tuple) in a table, representing a complete set of related data entries. "Displaying all records" means iterating through the entire `ResultSet` returned by a `SELECT * FROM table_name;` query.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are at a **library counter** and you want a librarian to fetch *every book* from a specific shelf and show them to you, one by one.

- **You (the Java Program)** write a request slip (the **SQL Query**).
- The **Librarian (the JDBC Driver)** knows which library (database) you are talking to and translates your slip into the local language.
- The **Library Door (the Connection)** is opened for you.
- The **Book Cart (the ResultSet)** comes back with all the books (records).
- The **Receipt (the Statement)** is your proof of work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Just like you must *return* the library door key (`close()` the connection) after use, JDBC requires proper resource management to avoid **memory leaks** and **connection leaks** in production systems.

## 1.3 Core Components of JDBC (Mandatory Recall)

| Component | Type | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `DriverManager` | Class | Manages a list of database drivers; provides `getConnection()` |
