| `Connection` | Interface | Represents a session/connection to a specific database |
| `Statement` | Interface | Used for executing static SQL queries |
| `PreparedStatement` | Interface | Pre-compiled SQL statement (parameterized) |
| `ResultSet` | Interface | Represents the tabular data returned by executing a query |
| `SQLException` | Class | Exception class for database access errors |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** When asked "List the JDBC components", always write the **5-step JDBC process** along with the API: *Load Driver → Establish Connection → Create Statement → Execute Query → Close Connection*. This is worth **2 marks minimum** in any Java lab exam.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in JDBC

- **Default Port for MySQL:** `3306`
- **Default Port for Oracle:** `1521`
- **Default Port for PostgreSQL:** `5432`
- **JDBC URL Format:** `jdbc:<subprotocol>://<host>:<port>/<databaseName>`
- **Standard MySQL JDBC URL:** `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktuDB`
- **Class.forName() for MySQL Connector/J 8.x+:** `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`
- **Class.forName() for older MySQL:** `com.mysql.jdbc.Driver`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Step JDBC Operational Logic (The Core "Why" and "How")

The JDBC API follows a strict sequential workflow. Skipping any step results in a `SQLException` or `NullPointerException`.

### Step 1: Load / Register the Database Driver
- **Why?** Java needs to know *which* database vendor it is talking to. Each vendor (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL) ships its own driver class.
- **How?** The class `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver` is loaded into memory using either:
  - `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");` (Explicit, older method)
  - Automatic loading via `META-INF/services` in JDBC 4.0+ (since Java 6)

### Step 2: Establish a Connection
- **Why?** A live network session must be opened between Java and the DBMS.
- **How?** Call `DriverManager.getConnection(URL, username, password)`.
- The method returns a `Connection` object, which represents a physical TCP socket to the database.

### Step 3: Create a Statement Object
- **Why?** SQL queries cannot be sent directly via the `Connection` object. A vehicle is required.
- **How?** Call `connection.createStatement()`. Returns a `Statement` object.

### Step 4: Execute the Query and Obtain a ResultSet
- **Why?** The DBMS needs to receive the SQL, execute it, and return the rows.
- **How?** Call `statement.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students")`. The DB engine returns a cursor pointing to the result table. This cursor is wrapped in a `ResultSet` object in Java.

### Step 5: Iterate the ResultSet and Close Resources
- **Why?** Data must be extracted row-by-row using column indices or names. Resources must be released to prevent connection pool exhaustion.
- **How?** Use `resultSet.next()` in a `while` loop, then call `resultSet.getString(col)` or `getInt(col)`. Finally, call `.close()` on `ResultSet`, `Statement`, and `Connection` — ideally in a `finally` block or using **try-with-resources** (Java 7+).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Key Methods & Equations)

| Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose / Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Class.forName(String className)` | `Class<?>` | Dynamically loads a JDBC driver class at runtime |
| `DriverManager.getConnection(String url, String user, String pwd)` | `Connection` | Establishes a session: $C = f(URL, U, P)$ |
| `Connection.createStatement()` | `Statement` | Creates a vehicle to carry static SQL |
| `Connection.prepareStatement(String sql)` | `PreparedStatement` | Creates a pre-compiled parameterized query |
| `Statement.executeQuery(String sql)` | `ResultSet` | Executes a `SELECT` and returns a cursor: $R = Q(SQL)$ |
| `Statement.executeUpdate(String sql)` | `int` | Executes `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE`; returns row count |
| `ResultSet.next()` | `boolean` | Moves cursor to next row; returns `false` past last row |
| `ResultSet.getString(int colIndex)` | `String` | Fetches column value as `String` |
| `ResultSet.getString(String colName)` | `String` | Fetches column value by column label |
| `ResultSet.getInt(int colIndex)` | `int` | Fetches column value as `int` |
| `ResultSet.close()` | `void` | Releases the cursor resources |
| `Connection.close()` | `void` | Terminates the DB session |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of JDBC

JDBC is the **backbone of enterprise Java**:
- **Web Applications:** Servlets and JSPs use JDBC to authenticate users, fetch product catalogs, and persist orders.
- **Standalone Desktop Tools:** IDEs, reporting tools, and DB clients (like DBeaver) are built on JDBC.
- **ORM Frameworks:** Hibernate, JPA, and Spring Data JPA all internally use JDBC — they are *abstraction layers* over raw JDBC.
- **Data Migration Scripts:** Backend engineers frequently write raw JDBC programs for ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) jobs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **URL** is the single most important string. The format is:
> $$URL = \text{"jdbc:"} + \text{subprotocol} + \text{://"} + \text{host} + \text{:"} + \text{port} + \text{"/"} + \text{dbName}$$
> For example: `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/studentDB`. Forgetting `jdbc:` prefix throws a `SQLException: No suitable driver found`.

## 2.4 Exception Hierarchy & Resource Management

JDBC methods throw `java.sql.SQLException`, which is a **checked exception**. This forces the programmer to either:
1. Wrap the code in `try-catch` blocks.
2. Declare `throws SQLException` in the method signature.

> [!TIP]
> **Try-with-Resources (KTU 2024 Recommended):** Since JDBC classes implement `AutoCloseable`, the cleanest way to write the program in 2024 is:
> `try (Connection con = ...; Statement st = ...; ResultSet rs = ...) { ... }`
> This guarantees `.close()` is called even if an exception is thrown.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Program Construction & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Lab Record Format)

> **Aim:** Write a Java program to display all records from a table named `student` having columns `roll_no`, `name`, `marks`, and `grade` using Java Database Connectivity (JDBC). Assume MySQL as the backend.

## 3.2 Prerequisites (Must be done BEFORE running the program)

### Step A: Create the Database and Table in MySQL
```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktuDB;
USE ktuDB;

CREATE TABLE student (
    roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name   VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    marks  INT,
    grade  CHAR(2)
);

INSERT INTO student VALUES
(1, 'Anand Krishnan',  92, 'A'),
(2, 'Divya Raj',       85, 'B'),
(3, 'Faisal Ahmed',    78, 'C'),
(4, 'Meera Pillai',    95, 'A'),
(5, 'Rohit Menon',     88, 'B');
```

### Step B: Add the MySQL Connector/J JAR to the Project Classpath
- Download `mysql-connector-java-8.x.x.jar` from the official MySQL site.
- In Eclipse/IntelliJ: `Right-click Project → Build Path → Add External Archives`.
- For command line: `javac -cp .:mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.jar DisplayRecords.java` and `java -cp .:mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.jar DisplayRecords`.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Java Program (Full Source Code with Line-by-Line Logic)

```java
// =======================================================
// File: DisplayRecords.java
// Experiment 2: Display all records from a table using JDBC
// KTU 2024 Scheme - PCEOL408 Object Oriented Programming Lab
// =======================================================

// STEP 1: Import JDBC classes from java.sql package
import java.sql.Connection;          // Represents session with DB
import java.sql.DriverManager;       // Manages DB drivers
import java.sql.ResultSet;           // Tabular result of a query
import java.sql.ResultSetMetaData;   // Info about columns in ResultSet
import java.sql.SQLException;        // JDBC exception class
import java.sql.Statement;           // Vehicle for static SQL queries

public class DisplayRecords {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // STEP 2: Define connection parameters
        //  Format:  jdbc:<vendor>://<host>:<port>/<database>
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktuDB";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "root123";

        // STEP 3: Use try-with-resources (Java 7+)
        //  This guarantees Connection, Statement, and ResultSet are closed
        //  automatically, even if an exception occurs.
        try (
            Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
            Statement  st  = con.createStatement();
            ResultSet  rs  = st.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM student")
        ) {
            // ---- 3a. Load the driver explicitly (optional in JDBC 4.0+) ----
            // Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");

            // ---- 3b. Print header using ResultSetMetaData ----
            ResultSetMetaData md = rs.getMetaData();
            int columnCount = md.getColumnCount();

            // Print column names dynamically
            for (int i = 1; i <= columnCount; i++) {
                System.out.printf("%-15s", md.getColumnName(i));
            }
            System.out.println();

            // Print separator line
            for (int i = 1; i <= columnCount; i++) {
                System.out.print("---------------");
            }
            System.out.println();

            // ---- 3c. Iterate the ResultSet row by row ----
            // rs.next() moves cursor to the next row.
            // Returns false after the last row, exiting the loop.
            boolean found = false;
            while (rs.next()) {
                found = true;
                // Read each column by its index (1-based in JDBC!)
                int    rno   = rs.getInt("roll_no");
                String sname = rs.getString("name");
                int    marks = rs.getInt("marks");
                String grade = rs.getString("grade");

                System.out.printf("%-15d %-15s %-15d %-15s%n",
                                   rno, sname, marks, grade);
            }

            // ---- 3d. Handle empty table case ----
            if (!found) {
                System.out.println("No records found in the student table.");
            }

        } catch (SQLException e) {
            // STEP 4: Handle SQL errors gracefully
            System.out.println("Database error occurred!");
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
        // STEP 5: Resources are auto-closed by try-with-resources
    }
}
```

## 3.4 Expected Output

```
roll_no         name            marks           grade
--------------- --------------- --------------- ---------------
1               Anand Krishnan  92              A
2               Divya Raj       85              B
3               Faisal Ahmed    78              C
4               Meera Pillai    95              A
5               Rohit Menon     88              B
```

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation (Why Each Statement Exists)

Let $P$ denote the Java program, $D$ the database, $T$ the table. The execution flow is a state machine:

$$
P \xrightarrow{\text{Step 2}} (URL, U, P) \xrightarrow{\text{Step 3a}} \text{Driver Loaded}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Step 3b}} \text{Connection Established} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 3c}} \text{Statement Created}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Step 3d}} \text{ResultSet } R = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Step 3e}} \text{Loop: } \forall i \in [1, n]: \text{print}(r_i) \xrightarrow{\text{Step 4}} \text{Close}
$$

### Detailed Logical Walkthrough:

1. **`import java.sql.*;`** — Java does not load JDBC classes by default. The `java.sql` package must be explicitly imported.
2. **`DriverManager.getConnection(...)`** — Internally, the `DriverManager` iterates through all registered drivers and asks each one: *"Can you connect to this URL?"* The first driver that returns a non-null connection wins.
3. **`con.createStatement()`** — Creates a `Statement` object bound to that specific `Connection`. The `Statement` carries a SQL string to the DB engine.
4. **`executeQuery("SELECT * FROM student")`** — Sends the SQL to MySQL. The MySQL engine compiles it, executes it, and returns a row-by-row cursor wrapped in a `ResultSet`.
5. **`rs.next()`** — JDBC result cursors start *before* the first row. You must call `next()` to reach row 1, then again for row 2, etc. When it returns `false`, the cursor has moved past the last row.
6. **`rs.getInt("roll_no")`** — Retrieves the value of the `roll_no` column in the current row. JDBC column indices are **1-based**, not 0-based — a common KTU exam pitfall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Compilation Pitfall (Valuation Trap):** Forgetting to add the MySQL JAR to the classpath. The compiler won't complain, but at runtime you get:
> `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`
> Or: `java.sql.SQLException: No suitable driver found for jdbc:mysql://...`

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Variations (KTU-Asked Patterns)

| Variation | Modification Required | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Display specific columns only | Change `SELECT *` to `SELECT name, marks FROM student` | Tests SQL knowledge alongside JDBC |
| Search by a condition | `SELECT * FROM student WHERE grade='A'` | Tests parameterized logic |
| Count records | Loop with a counter variable; print after loop | Tests loop logic |
| Use `PreparedStatement` | Replace `createStatement` with `con.prepareStatement(sql)` | Tests pre-compiled query concept |
| Use Oracle DB | Change URL to `jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:XE` and driver to `oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver` | Tests vendor portability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: JDBC 5-Step Execution Flow (Mermaid-Compliant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Java Program] --> B[Import java.sql Package]
    B --> C[Load JDBC Driver: Class.forName]
    C --> D[Establish Connection: DriverManager.getConnection]
    D --> E{Connection Successful?}
    E -- No --> F[Throw SQLException]
    E -- Yes --> G[Create Statement Object]
    G --> H[Execute Query: SELECT * FROM student]
    H --> I[Receive ResultSet Cursor]
    I --> J{rs.next() Returns True?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Read Current Row Columns]
    K --> L[Print Row to Console]
    L --> J
    J -- No --> M[Close ResultSet, Statement, Connection]
    M --> N[End of Program]
    F --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: JDBC Class & Interface Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Java Application] --> B[DriverManager Class]
    B --> C[Connection Interface]
    C --> D[Statement Interface]
    C --> E[PreparedStatement Interface]
    D --> F[ResultSet Interface]
    E --> F
    F --> G[ResultSetMetaData Interface]
    C --> H[DatabaseMetaData Interface]
    F --> I[Rows of Table]
    I --> J[Column 1 Value]
    I --> K[Column 2 Value]
    I --> L[Column N Value]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of JDBC Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["APPLICATION LAYER (Java Code)"]
        A1[DisplayRecords.java]
    end
    subgraph S2["JDBC API LAYER (java.sql package)"]
        B1[DriverManager]
        B2[Connection]
        B3[Statement]
        B4[ResultSet]
    end
    subgraph S3["JDBC DRIVER LAYER (Vendor Specific)"]
        C1[MySQL Connector-J JAR]
    end
    subgraph S4["DATABASE LAYER"]
        D1[MySQL Server on Port 3306]
        D2[Database ktuDB]
        D3[Table student]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 -.returns rows.-> B4
    B4 -.ResultSet data.-> A1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Try-With-Resources Resource Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main method
    participant Con as Connection
    participant St as Statement
    participant Rs as ResultSet
    participant DB as MySQL Database

    Main->>Con: getConnection (URL, user, pass)
    Con->>DB: Open TCP session
    DB-->>Con: Session established
    Main->>St: con.createStatement()
    Main->>Rs: st.executeQuery(SELECT)
    Rs->>DB: Send SQL and fetch rows
    DB-->>Rs: Return cursor with all rows
    loop While rs.next() is true
        Main->>Rs: getString, getInt (read columns)
        Rs-->>Main: Column values
    end
    Note over Main,Rs: End of try block
    Rs-->>Main: close() called automatically
    St-->>Main: close() called automatically
    Con->>DB: Close session
    Con-->>Main: close() called automatically
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define JDBC. List any four key interfaces of the `java.sql` package.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is a Java API that provides a standard mechanism for Java applications to interact with relational databases using SQL. It is part of `java.sql` and is platform- and database-independent.
Four key interfaces are:
1. `Driver` — establishes connection to a specific DB.
2. `Connection` — represents a session with a database.
3. `Statement` — used to execute static SQL queries.
4. `ResultSet` — represents the tabular result of a `SELECT` query.
*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for interfaces, 1 mark for layout)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of `DriverManager` and `ResultSet` in JDBC.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **`DriverManager`:** It is a class in `java.sql` that manages the set of registered JDBC drivers. Its main role is to provide the static method `getConnection(url, user, password)`, which selects an appropriate driver from the registered ones and returns a `Connection` object representing a live session with the database. *(1.5 marks)*
- **`ResultSet`:** It is an interface that represents the tabular data returned by executing a `SELECT` query. It maintains a cursor pointing to the current row. The method `next()` moves the cursor forward, and methods like `getString()`, `getInt()` retrieve column values from the current row. *(1.5 marks)*

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a) — 5 Steps of JDBC (7 Marks)

The five essential steps to develop any JDBC application are:

1. **Load the Driver Class** — Use `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");` to register the vendor-specific driver. *(1 mark)*
2. **Establish the Connection** — Call `DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/CollegeDB", "user", "pass");` to open a session. *(1 mark)*
3. **Create the Statement** — Use `Connection.createStatement()` to obtain a `Statement` object. *(1 mark)*
4. **Execute the Query** — Use `stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Employee");` to send SQL and obtain a `ResultSet`. *(2 marks)*
5. **Process ResultSet & Close** — Iterate using `rs.next()`, extract values using `getString()` / `getInt()`, and close all resources. *(2 marks)*

### Solution to Question A(b) — Java Program (7 Marks)

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class DisplayEmployee {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/CollegeDB";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "root123";

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
             Statement  st  = con.createStatement();
             ResultSet  rs  = st.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Employee")) {

            System.out.printf("%-10s %-20s %-10s %-15s%n",
                              "emp_id", "emp_name", "salary", "department");
            System.out.println("--------------------------------------------------");

            while (rs.next()) {
                int    eid   = rs.getInt("emp_id");
                String ename = rs.getString("emp_name");
                double sal   = rs.getDouble("salary");
                String dept  = rs.getString("department");

                System.out.printf("%-10d %-20s %-10.2f %-15s%n",
                                   eid, ename, sal, dept);
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Imports and driver URL: 1 mark
- Try-with-resources structure: 1 mark
- `executeQuery` call with correct SQL: 1 mark
- `while (rs.next())` loop: 1 mark
- Correct column extraction methods: 1 mark
- Formatted output: 1 mark
- Exception handling block: 1 mark

### Solution to Question B(a) — Statement vs PreparedStatement (7 Marks)

| Feature | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SQL type | Static SQL queries | Pre-compiled, parameterized SQL |
| Performance | Compiled every execution | Compiled once, reused |
| SQL Injection | Vulnerable | Immune (uses `?` placeholders) |
| Readability | Hard to maintain with many params | Cleaner with `?` |
| Usage | One-off queries | Queries run multiple times |
| Method | `executeQuery(String)` | `executeQuery()` after `setXxx()` |

**When to prefer `PreparedStatement`:**
- When the **same query** is executed many times with different parameter values (e.g., batch inserts, search-by-id).
- When user input is involved, to **prevent SQL Injection** attacks.
- For better performance due to **pre-compilation** by the DB engine.

### Solution to Question B(b) — Program for Salary Filter (7 Marks)

```java
import java.sql.*;

public class HighSalaryEmployees {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String url  = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/CollegeDB";
        String user = "root";
        String pass = "root123";

        String sql = "SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE salary > ?";

        try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
             PreparedStatement ps = con.prepareStatement(sql)) {

            ps.setDouble(1, 50000.0);  // Bind the parameter

            try (ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery()) {
                System.out.println("Employees with salary > 50,000:");
                System.out.printf("%-10s %-20s %-10s%n",
                                  "emp_id", "emp_name", "salary");
                System.out.println("--------------------------------------------");

                while (rs.next()) {
                    System.out.printf("%-10d %-20s %-10.2f%n",
                        rs.getInt("emp_id"),
                        rs.getString("emp_name"),
                        rs.getDouble("salary"));
                }
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Correct URL and connection: 1 mark
- Using `prepareStatement` (not plain `Statement`): 2 marks
- `setDouble(1, 50000.0)` for parameter binding: 1 mark
- Correct `executeQuery()` and iteration: 1 mark
- Output formatting: 1 mark
- Exception handling: 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **-1 mark:** Forgetting to add `Class.forName(...)` if the question specifies the older JDBC method.
> 2. **-1 mark:** Writing `rs.getInt(0)` instead of `rs.getInt(1)`. JDBC column indices are **1-based**, NOT 0-based.
> 3. **-1 mark:** Using `Statement` instead of `PreparedStatement` for parameterized queries — loses 2 marks in B-part.
> 4. **-1 mark:** Not closing the `Connection` — KTU values "resource management" as a separate sub-criterion.
> 5. **-1 mark:** Hardcoding the password directly inside the URL or as a string constant without comment (best practice penalty).
> 6. **-0.5 mark:** Wrong import — writing `import javax.sql.*` instead of `import java.sql.*`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minent Revision Checklist)

- **JDBC** = Java Database Connectivity, found in `java.sql` package.
- **5 Steps:** Load Driver → Connect → Create Statement → Execute Query → Process & Close.
- **MySQL Driver Class (new):** `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`.
- **MySQL JDBC URL:** `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/databaseName`.
- **`Class.forName()`** is optional in JDBC 4.0+ but **mandatory** in older code.
- **`DriverManager.getConnection()`** returns a `Connection` object.
- **`Statement.executeQuery()`** returns a `ResultSet`; **`.executeUpdate()`** returns an `int`.
- **JDBC column indices are 1-based**, not 0-based — this is a classic KTU pitfall.
- **`ResultSet.next()`** moves the cursor forward; returns `false` after the last row.
- **Always close** `ResultSet`, `Statement`, and `Connection` (preferably via **try-with-resources**).
- **`PreparedStatement` is preferred** over `Statement` for parameterized queries and to prevent SQL injection.
- **Common exception:** `java.sql.SQLException` (checked) — must be `try`-`catch`ed.
- **Common runtime error:** `No suitable driver found` → wrong URL prefix (missing `jdbc:`).
- **Common runtime error:** `Communications link failure` → MySQL not running on port 3306.
- **`ResultSetMetaData`** lets you dynamically discover column names and counts — useful for generic table displays.
- **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Use `try-with-resources` and `PreparedStatement` to score full marks.
